players.name: Ricky Price
players.tagline: His jumper saved the 1996 season.
players.now: Founder, Game Ready Skills & Development; speaker; author
players.stat: McDonald’s AA dunk champ 1994 • 3rd-Team All-ACC • 1,000+ pts • 10 yrs pro overseas

bio.road:
When people who grew up playing basketball in Los Angeles in the early 1990s talk about the best hoopers to come out of the area, one name always surfaces: Ricky Price. Not sometimes. Always. The Not In My House podcast called him ‘without question one of the greatest high school basketball players to ever come out of the Los Angeles area.’ His mid-range game was automatic. At 6-foot-6, with explosive athleticism and a scorer’s instinct, he was the complete package.

Price was born February 4, 1976, in Carson, California — a working-class city in the South Bay, fifteen miles south of downtown LA. At Carson High School, he averaged 28 points and 11 rebounds and was unanimously named a USA Today/AP/Parade Top 5 player nationally. Gold medal representing the U.S. in the Junior Olympics in Argentina. And at the 1994 McDonald’s All-American Game, he won the slam dunk contest — the first Duke player ever to do so, a lineage that would later include Gerald Henderson (2006), Grayson Allen (2014), and Zion Williamson (2018). At that same McDonald’s Game, he played alongside future Duke teammate Trajan Langdon and against future Duke teammate Steve Wojciechowski.

His recruitment came down to Duke and Kansas. Roy Williams was his first choice for a while. But as Price spent more time with Coach K, the decision clarified. When he broke the news to Williams, something remarkable happened: the relationship didn’t end. ‘It’s amazing because, of course, I hate Carolina,’ Price said. ‘I think if you’re a Duke guy, naturally, that’s embedded in your DNA. But I love Roy Williams. He’s one of the few honest guys in the game. We still talk frequently. After every Duke-Carolina game, I always make it a point to go see him and say hello. He still asks about my mom. Here we are 20 years later and he’s still asking about my mom and dad. That just shows you the character of the type of guy he is.’
bio.duke:
What happened to Ricky Price at Duke cannot be separated from what happened to Duke in 1994–95. He arrived as one of three prized freshmen — Price, Langdon, and Wojciechowski — joining a program expected to contend. Price had never had consistent coaching in high school, and Duke was supposed to change that. Instead, it got worse.

Coach K had undergone back surgery and rushed back. Twelve games in, with Duke 9–3, the pain became unbearable. Krzyzewski left on medical leave. Pete Gaudet, making just over $300 a week as the ‘restricted earnings’ coach, took over. Duke went 4–15 under Gaudet, finishing 13–18 and 2–14 in the ACC — dead last. Tommy Amaker, who had helped build the program as a player a decade earlier, saw the confidence draining: ‘Once we lost a few games, we lost our confidence. That’s where these guys miss Coach K.’

For Price, 3,000 miles from home: ‘It was very difficult as a freshman. You are still trying to find your way. As a freshman you want to lean on your head coach.’ He didn’t have that opportunity. ‘It was embarrassing. You don’t want to be part of a team that goes down as one of the worst in recent memory. It was a very difficult year and shows how important leadership is.’

But from that disaster, Price took a lesson that shaped the rest of his life. When Krzyzewski returned for 1995–96, the coach was different. ‘Prior to going out he did everything,’ Price recalled. ‘He was taking on too much of a burden. Post back surgery he trusted other guys to do other things. He let other guys step up and do their part.’ The principle of delegation — that leadership means trusting others — became the foundation of Price’s speaking career decades later.

With Krzyzewski back, Price’s sophomore season was his best at Duke: 14.2 ppg, career-high 28 against Virginia, a buzzer-beater to beat Maryland that Duke Basketball Report later credited with putting Duke back in the NCAAs. Third-Team All-ACC. He reached 1,000 career points. Duke won the ACC regular-season title — ‘only five short years after Ricky Price put Duke in the NCAAs.’

As a junior, a broken finger wrecked his jump shot. For a player whose mid-range game was his signature, the injury was devastating. He slumped to just over 9 ppg. Then senior year: academic probation after submitting a paper that wasn’t his. Suspended for the first semester, lost his starting role. His own accountability advice years later: ‘Take accountability for what you did, correct it, apologize to the people you need to apologize to, and then you’ve got to do some self-improvement and make sure that never happens again.’ He didn’t quit. He came back, played off the bench, helped Duke win its second straight ACC regular-season title. He graduated with a degree in sociology.
bio.after:
Price went undrafted in 1998. Not long after, he was playing 5,000 miles from the NBA — in Slovenia. Also the Netherlands, Germany, France, Finland, Asia. NBA preseason stints with the Miami Heat, Detroit Pistons, Orlando Magic, and Charlotte Bobcats — close enough to taste it, never close enough to stay. For about a decade, he saw different arenas across Europe and Asia and played in American pro leagues like the IBL and the CBA.

As a player, he was known for his above-the-rim game. But of all his accomplishments, he has said that learning to become a solid defensive player was his biggest achievement — the thing that kept him employed for a decade overseas when the dunk contest highlights faded. After retiring, he worked as VP of Operations for Worldwide Hoops Sports Marketing (representing overseas and NBA players) and as a Raycom Sports correspondent covering ACC basketball.
bio.now:
In 2010, Price founded Game Ready Skills and Development LLC in North Carolina. His first summer camp in Charlotte drew 150 kids. Former Duke players Ryan Kelly, Seth Curry, Rodney Hood, Andre Dawkins, and Tyler Thornton have appeared. But Price’s focus is development, not exposure: ‘My focus is 100% skills development. My job is to get them ready to get to that level. Unfortunately, a lot of our kids don’t have the necessary essentials, the basics, the fundamentals on how to play the game. How to pivot off your left and right foot, how to pump fake and one dribble, how to shoot a one dribble pull up. You’d be amazed how many kids can’t do that.’

He commands thousands of dollars per speaking engagement, drawing parallels between basketball teamwork and corporate environments, leaning heavily on the lessons from that devastating freshman year. His book, Celebrate Your Wins, has a foreword by Coach K. He still volunteers coaching middle schoolers — spending back-to-back five-hour Sunday sessions with varsity and JV players at schools that might otherwise get overlooked.

Ricky Price’s story is the most candid in the Transition Era. The five-star recruit from the LA basketball machine. First Duke player to win the McDonald’s dunk contest. Chose Duke over Kansas and kept Roy Williams’ friendship for life. Arrived at Duke and immediately endured the worst season in Krzyzewski history. Peaked as a sophomore, lost his jumper to a broken finger, lost his starting job to academic probation, and never quit. Played ten years on three continents. Turned the worst season of his life into the foundation of a speaking career.

Duke Basketball Report observed: ‘If he had the choice to do things over again, he’d still choose Duke.’ That’s the line that matters. Not the dunk contest trophy. Not the All-ACC season. Not the broken finger or the probation. The choice. He’d make it again.

nba.postCareer: Founder, Game Ready Skills & Development; speaker; author

sources.title: Ricky Price - Sports Reference College Basketball
Ricky Price Duke Career - Duke Basketball Report

players.name: Greg Newton
players.tagline: The Canadian who talked trash to Tim Duncan. Suspended, grieving, redeemed, benched in his final game. Eleven countries. The Brotherhood includes him, too.
players.now: Former assistant coach, Brock University (Ontario); lives in Niagara Falls region; retired from professional basketball ~2005
players.stat: 4 Duke seasons • 107 games • 12.2 PPG/8.2 RPG (best season) • Team Captain • Canadian National Team • 2000 Olympics

sources.label: Virginia Pilot: Newton Is Pulling His Weight at Duke (1996)
Duke Basketball Report: 1997 Team — Catching Up With Wake Forest and Tim Duncan
From Way Downtown: Tim Duncan — The Can’t-Miss Kid (1997)
Wikipedia: Greg Newton
Eurobasket: Greg Newton professional stats
Sports Reference: Greg Newton college stats

highlights.note: Led Canada in scoring (11.5) and rebounding (10.1) at FIBA World Championship
Member of Canadian Olympic team, 2000 Sydney Olympics (7th place)

overseas.league: Israel, Brazil, Argentina, Slovenia, Italy, Bulgaria, Serbia, Russia, Bosnia, Spain

nba.postCareer: Retired from professional basketball ~2005; assistant coach at Brock University (2011–13); Niagara Falls, Ontario area

bio.road:
Gregory Michael Newton was born September 7, 1974, in Niagara Falls, Ontario — on the Canadian side of the falls, in a country where hockey was king and basketball was an afterthought. His father had pitched in the Milwaukee Braves organization. His mother still played recreational basketball. An older brother played college basketball in Canada. The Newtons were athletic, but Niagara Falls didn’t produce basketball recruits.

At A.N. Myer Secondary School, Newton grew to 6’10” and dominated: 28 points, 15 rebounds, 4 blocks per game as a senior. He was a force at the OFSAA provincial tournament. But Canadian high school basketball existed in obscurity — American coaches rarely crossed the border.

So Newton’s father built it himself. He started an AAU team, traveled south of the border, entered Greg in elite American tournaments. Newton played in the Beach Ball Classic in Myrtle Beach — unheard-of for a Canadian kid — and made the Canadian National Team while still in high school. Duke recruited him. He was expected to be the next great Duke big man, heir to a lineage running from Alarie through Ferry, Laettner, and Cherokee Parks.
bio.duke:
Greg Newton played four years at Duke, 1993–97, and his career was defined by promise, turbulence, and one unforgettable rivalry.

Freshman year: backup to Cherokee Parks and Erik Meek, 25 games, 4.0 PPG, 3.4 RPG. Learning from the bench.

Then everything went sideways. In 1995, Newton was suspended for two semesters after being found guilty of cheating on a computer science exam. He denied the charge, but the suspension held. During this period, his father died. A smoking habit he’d picked up at fifteen worsened. He drank. Both habits, he admitted, were things Coach K hadn’t known about. The death of the father who had built the AAU team, who had driven his son across the border, who had pitched in the Braves organization — that loss hollowed something out.

Newton returned for 1995–96 — the year after the 4–15 disaster. With Coach K back and the program rebuilding, Newton had his best year: 12.2 PPG, 8.2 RPG. Aggressive, physical, invaluable on a thin frontcourt. Dave Odom said he’d gotten better every single game. But the defining narrative was his rivalry with Tim Duncan.

Newton told reporters he knew how to shut down Duncan: "You’ve got to be physical with him. He can’t play physical." Duncan’s response: "Peace be to Greg Newton." When pressed: "I think he’s the best player I’ve ever seen." The sarcasm was surgical. Duncan demolished Newton on national television. The quote became legendary — not for what Newton said, but for how completely Duncan dismantled him.

Senior year: team captain. But playing time shrank. Twenty-one total points over the final 11 games. Six minutes in the last regular-season game against Wake. And in his final college game — Duke’s loss to Providence — Newton did not play. DNP–Coach’s Decision. A team captain, on the bench, in his last game.
bio.after:
Newton went undrafted. No team expressed interest. He started with the USBL’s Raleigh Cougars, who promoted him by noting he was one away from Duke’s top ten in career blocks.

What followed was one of the most far-flung careers in Duke history. He played professionally in Belgium, Israel, Brazil, Argentina, Slovenia, Italy, Bulgaria, Serbia, Russia, Bosnia and Herzegovina, and Spain. Eleven countries across three continents over nearly a decade. A basketball journeyman — a 6’10” Canadian who had played at Duke, been suspended, lost his father, talked trash to Tim Duncan, and spent his twenties and thirties in gymnasiums most Americans would never see.

Through it all, he represented Canada. At the 1998 FIBA World Championship, he led the Canadian team in scoring (11.5 PPG) and rebounding (10.1 RPG). At the 2000 Sydney Olympics, he was a member of the team that finished seventh — the best Olympic finish for Canadian basketball in decades. On the international stage, the kid from Niagara Falls was his country’s best big man.
bio.now:
Newton’s professional career ended around 2005. In 2011, he joined the coaching staff at Brock University in St. Catharines, Ontario — a short drive from Niagara Falls. He served as an assistant until 2013. He had come home, back to the Canadian side of the falls, back to where his father had built an AAU team from nothing.

Greg Newton was born in Niagara Falls, Ontario, in a country where basketball was an afterthought. His father pitched in the Braves organization, started an AAU team, and drove his son to American tournaments. Newton was recruited to Duke as the next great big man. He was suspended for cheating. His father died and his habits got worse. He came back and had his best season. He told the world he could shut down Tim Duncan, and Duncan replied with the most politely devastating put-down in ACC history. He was named team captain and didn’t play in his final game. He went undrafted and played in eleven countries. He led Canada in scoring and rebounding at the World Championship. He played in the Olympics. He came home to coach near where he was born. His career was messy, controversial, and human — full of the mistakes and stubbornness and grief that make a life real. He was not the next Laettner. He was Greg Newton. And the Brotherhood includes him, too.

players.name: Roshown McLeod
players.tagline: Coach K’s first transfer. Jersey City to St. John’s to Duke. First Team All-ACC. First-round pick. Then the year everything fell apart — and the long road back.
players.now: Vice President / Home Lending Manager, JPMorgan Chase (Georgia); completed Duke degree 2006
players.stat: 2 Duke seasons • 15.3 PPG/5.6 RPG (senior) • 1st Team All-ACC • 32-4 record • 20th pick • 113 NBA games

sources.label: Duke Basketball Report: Roshown McLeod’s Life Fell Apart at 25 (2020)
Duke Basketball Report: Jim’s Look at Duke Transfers, Part II (2021)
Indiana Athletics: Roshown McLeod coaching bio
Basketball Reference: Roshown McLeod career stats
Wikipedia: Roshown McLeod

teams.note: Signed but permanently injured; never played

highlights.note: Career-high 24 pts vs Orlando Magic (Nov 14, 2000)

coaching.team: Woodward Academy (Atlanta) + Next Level Players Development

nba.postCareer: VP / Home Lending Manager, JPMorgan Chase (Georgia); completed Duke degree 2006; AAU coach and basketball trainer

bio.road:
Roshown McLeod was born November 17, 1975, in Jersey City, New Jersey. He was raised primarily by his mother, Diane McLeod — known to everyone as Ms. Diane — in one of the toughest cities in the New York metro area. Jersey City basketball was a proving ground where talent was forged on outdoor courts and in cramped gymnasiums.

McLeod attended St. Anthony High School, the legendary program coached by Bob Hurley Sr. — the same Bob Hurley whose son Bobby had led Duke to back-to-back championships in 1991 and 1992. Under Hurley Sr., McLeod learned defense, discipline, toughness, and the understanding that basketball was a privilege earned through effort. He was tournament MVP as a senior — 18 points, 9 rebounds, and a key block in the championship game. The connection between St. Anthony’s and Duke ran through Bobby Hurley’s bloodline.

McLeod committed to St. John’s, staying close to home. Over two seasons with the Red Storm, he averaged 7.2 PPG and 5.8 RPG in 57 games — struggling to find consistent playing time. He announced he was transferring.

At the time, transfers to Duke were unheard of. Coach K didn’t take them. But the Hurley connection gave Krzyzewski information he wouldn’t have had otherwise. Roshown McLeod became the first transfer Coach K ever accepted. The door he opened would later be walked through by Dahntay Jones, Seth Curry, and Rodney Hood. Each became All-ACC. Each played in the NBA. McLeod was the one who proved a transfer could work at Duke.
bio.duke:
After sitting out 1995–96 under transfer rules, McLeod played two years at Duke, and the transformation was dramatic. The player who averaged 7.2 at St. John’s became one of the best forwards in the ACC.

Junior year, 1996–97: 12.1 PPG, 5.3 RPG, helping Duke win the ACC regular-season title and reach 24–9. He was part of the bridge generation — alongside Langdon, Wojciechowski, and Carrawell — that carried the program from the 4–15 disaster back to national relevance.

Senior year, 1997–98: the masterpiece. 15.3 PPG, 5.6 RPG, 49.4% from the field. Led Duke in scoring. First Team All-ACC. Honorable mention All-American. Duke went 32–4, won the ACC regular-season title. His best game: Senior Day against Vince Carter, Antawn Jamison, and North Carolina. Duke trailed by 17 in the second half. McLeod scored 23, led the comeback. Duke won 77–75. In the 1998 NCAA Tournament, he averaged 17.5 points across four games before Duke fell to Kentucky in the Elite Eight.

The kid from Jersey City who couldn’t crack the lineup at St. John’s had become a first-round pick from Duke. Coach K’s gamble on his first transfer paid off in full.
bio.after:
Atlanta selected McLeod 20th overall in 1998. Three NBA seasons: two with the Hawks, a stint with Philadelphia. 113 games, 7.2 PPG, 2.7 RPG. Career-high 24 against Orlando. In 2001–02, he signed with the Celtics, but a permanent injury ended his career before he played a game. He was twenty-five years old.

Then his mother died. Ms. Diane — the woman who raised him in Jersey City, the center of his world — was gone. In the same year that injury ended his career, McLeod lost the person who mattered most. He fell into a deep depression. He moved out of the house where his children lived. Fans see players for a couple of hours and think we know them. We knew nothing about the dark places McLeod went.

Years later, he published an open letter to his mother — raw, honest, deeply personal. A man in his forties reckoning with the loss that had broken him at twenty-five and the long road back.
bio.now:
McLeod rebuilt through coaching and eventually a career beyond basketball. Assistant at Fairfield under Ed Cooley. Next Level Players Development and AAU coaching in Atlanta. Indiana University staff under Tom Crean (2008–10). He replaced Dan Hurley — Bob Hurley Sr.’s other son — at St. Benedict’s Prep in Newark, closing a circle that connected Jersey City, Duke, and the Hurley family. He coached at Fellowship Christian School in Georgia.

He completed his Duke degree in 2006, returning to Durham to finish what he started. He transitioned to finance: lending manager at JPMorgan Chase, promoted to VP and home lending manager in Georgia. He has a son Anthony and a daughter Anisa.

Roshown McLeod was born in Jersey City and raised by his mother. He played for Bob Hurley Sr. at St. Anthony’s. He went to St. John’s and couldn’t break through. He transferred to Duke — the first transfer Coach K ever accepted — and became First Team All-ACC and a first-round pick. He scored 23 on Senior Day to lead a 17-point comeback against Vince Carter’s North Carolina. He played 113 NBA games. Then his mother died and an injury ended his career in the same year, and he fell into a darkness fans never saw. He published a letter to his mother and found his way back. He coached at Indiana and St. Benedict’s. He finished his Duke degree. He became a VP at JPMorgan Chase. The door he opened — the first transfer door — led to Seth Curry and Rodney Hood and a tradition of second chances. He was the first to prove you don’t have to start at Duke to belong to the Brotherhood. You just have to earn it. And he earned it.

players.name: Nate James
players.tagline: A Marine’s son. McDonald’s All-American. Five consecutive ACC titles — a record no one else holds. Senior captain of the 2001 champions. Three title rings. Twelve countries. Then the Brotherhood brought him home.
players.now: Associate Head Coach, Howard University (reunited with Kenny Blakeney); married to actress Bobbi Baker James
players.drafted: Undrafted — played professionally overseas in 12 countries
players.stat: 5 Duke seasons • 135 games • 1,116 pts • 12.3/5.2 senior • 3rd Team All-ACC • All-Defensive • 3 title rings

sources.label: GoDuke.com: Nate James coaching bio
SI: Nate James Named Head Coach at Austin Peay (2021)
SI: Kenny Blakeney Gives New Opportunity to Fellow Duke Champ (2025)
Howard Athletics: Nate James joins Bison family (2025)
Austin Peay Athletics: Nate James coaching bio
Wikipedia: Nate James (basketball)

highlights.note: Carolinas Basketball League MVP
Philadelphia 76ers training camp invite

nba.postCareer: Associate Head Coach at Howard University; co-founded summer basketball camp in Durham with Chris Carrawell (12 years); assisted USA Basketball Olympic camps 2008, 2012, 2016

bio.road:
Nate James was born August 7, 1977, in Washington, D.C. He was the son of a Marine Sergeant Major, and like all military kids he grew up everywhere and nowhere — Quantico, Virginia; Albany, Georgia; San Diego, California; and back to Washington before his father retired. The constant was basketball. The constant was discipline.

James attended Saint John’s Catholic Prep in Buckeystown, Maryland, outside Frederick. By senior year he was one of the best players in the region — a 6’6 swingman who could shoot, defend, and lead. He was named a 1996 McDonald’s All-American. At the McDonald’s All-American Game, he won the Three-Point Shooting Contest. The kid Coach K would later describe as a high school center — meaning he had to reinvent himself at Duke — arrived in Durham as the best shooter in his class. He also received the McDonald’s Sportsmanship Award, the kind of honor that doesn’t make SportsCenter but tells you everything about who a player is.
bio.duke:
James spent five seasons at Duke (1996–2001), including a medical redshirt sophomore year. Over 135 games, 64 starts, he scored 1,116 points, made 111 three-pointers, grabbed 500 rebounds, and shot 47.3% from the floor.

The defining statistic: Duke won outright or shared the ACC regular-season championship in each of the five years James was on the roster. Five for five. No other player in ACC history can claim that. It is a record that may never be matched.

As a reserve in 1998–99, he averaged 5.0/2.6 in 14.7 minutes, helping Duke reach the Final Four. In 1999–2000, he was named co-captain alongside Chris Carrawell and Shane Battier, moved into the starting lineup, and averaged 11.0/4.5.

Senior year, 2000–01: co-captain again, 12.3 PPG/5.2 RPG, Third Team All-ACC, All-ACC Defensive Team. Duke went 35–4 and won the national championship, beating Arizona 82–72. James played 20 minutes in that title game. Coach K said of him: I’ve seen him grow from being a high school center, to becoming one of the most selfless leaders in Duke basketball history.
bio.after:
James graduated with a sociology degree and African-American studies minor. Undrafted, he tried out for Wizards and Kings summer league teams. He was named Carolinas Basketball League MVP in 2003 and earned a 76ers training camp invite in 2004. The NBA never opened its door.

So he saw the world. Over six years, James played in twelve countries: Bosnia, Brazil, France, Germany, Greece, Holland, Hungary, Italy, Japan, the Philippines, Poland, and Russia. He won league championships in the Netherlands and Hungary — teaming with former Duke teammate Casey Sanders for the Hungarian title.

During offseasons, he always returned to Durham. James co-founded a summer basketball camp with Chris Carrawell that ran twelve years, teaching fundamentals of the game and of life. Even while playing in Poland or Russia, Duke was home.
bio.now:
In 2007, James retired and returned to Duke as an assistant strength coach — the bottom rung. He was promoted to basketball ops director, then full assistant when Johnny Dawkins left for Stanford. Duke won the 2010 championship, making James the first person to win titles at Duke as both player and coach.

He spent thirteen years on staff, rising to associate head coach alongside Jon Scheyer in 2018. During that span: two national titles (2010, 2015), five ACC Tournament titles, twenty-one first-round draft picks. He helped recruit and develop Kyrie Irving, Jayson Tatum, Zion Williamson, Brandon Ingram, the Plumlee brothers, Jahlil Okafor, and Marvin Bagley III. He assisted USA Basketball Olympic camps in 2008, 2012, and 2016. His three championship rings are more than anyone at Duke except Coach K.

In April 2021 — exactly twenty years after the 2001 title game — he was named head coach at Austin Peay. He went 21–39 in two seasons and was fired in March 2023.

Then in October 2025, the Brotherhood called. Kenny Blakeney — Duke’s 1992 champion and Howard’s head coach — hired James as associate head coach. Two Duke champions, separated by a decade as players, reunited on the same bench at an HBCU in their shared hometown of Washington, D.C. Howard had reached the NCAA Tournament in two of the previous three seasons with Duke DNA on its staff: Tyler Thornton had been assistant before joining Scheyer in Durham. Now James and Blakeney coach together.

James is married to actress Bobbi Baker James. They have two sons, Nathanial Drake James III and Michael Dash James. He still visits Cameron regularly.

Five consecutive ACC titles as a player — a record no one else holds. Three championship rings. Twelve countries. The most selfless leader in Duke basketball history, according to Coach K. And when the head coaching dream didn’t work out, Kenny Blakeney called, because that is what the Brotherhood does. It catches you. Nate James is coaching basketball in Washington, D.C. — the city where a Marine’s son first learned the game — with a fellow Duke champion on the same bench. The Brotherhood always brings you home.

players.name: Carmen Wallace
players.tagline: Delaware’s Player of the Year. 2,004 career points. Survived the 4–15 disaster. Captain of the 1997 ACC championship team. Then built one of the most powerful sports agencies in the world.
players.now: NFL/NBA Certified Contract Advisor, Athletes First (Laguna Hills, CA); founding member; negotiated contracts for Rodgers, Palmer, Lewis, Matthews
players.drafted: Did not play professionally — went directly into sports management
players.stat: 4 Duke seasons • 82 games • 3.4 PPG • 43.3% 3PT • Team Captain 1997 • ACC Champions

sources.label: FOX Sports: NFL Agent Carmen Wallace Draft Blog (2014)
Duke Alumni Magazine: Hoop Profiles — Carmen Wallace
Tower Hill School: Forum Speaker Series (2014)
Duke Basketball Report: Turning Points — Duke 1997 Part I
Sports Reference: Carmen Wallace college stats
NBPA Agent Directory: Carmen Wallace

nba.postCareer: Founding member and NFL/NBA Certified Contract Advisor at Athletes First; previously Marketing Specialist at Steinberg Moorad & Dunn (1997-2001); clients include Carson Palmer, Aaron Rodgers, Ray Lewis, Clay Matthews, Miles Austin

bio.road:
Carmen Wallace grew up in Wilmington, Delaware, and attended Tower Hill School, a small private school where basketball was not supposed to produce Division I talent. Over four years at Tower Hill, he scored 2,004 career points — the third-highest total in Delaware state history. As a senior in 1993, he averaged 25.5 PPG, shot 58.2% from the field, 43.8% from three, and added 190 rebounds and 96 blocked shots. He led Tower Hill to a 40–8 record over his final two seasons. The Delaware Sportswriters and Broadcasters Association voted him the 1993 Boys Basketball Player of the Year and first-team All-State for the second time.

Duke offered Wallace a basketball scholarship. A kid from a small Delaware prep school was headed to Cameron Indoor Stadium to play for Coach K. It was an unlikely leap, but Wallace had earned it with 2,004 points and a state Player of the Year trophy.
bio.duke:
Wallace’s four years at Duke (1993–97) were the most turbulent stretch in modern program history. He arrived the year after the back-to-back championships and left the year the dynasty began rebuilding.

Freshman year, 1993–94: barely played — 37 minutes in 28 games. He appeared alongside Grant Hill, Antonio Lang, and Cherokee Parks on the team that lost to Arkansas in the championship game.

Sophomore year, 1994–95: the catastrophe. Coach K underwent back surgery and missed most of the season. Duke went 13–18, including the 4–15 stretch that remains the worst in program history. Wallace was there for every minute of it.

Junior year, 1995–96: Coach K returned. When the roster thinned out, Krzyzewski played the 6’5 Wallace at power forward out of desperation. In a key win over Florida State, Wallace had 13 points, 11 rebounds, and 2 blocks — playing center against bigger men. But he injured his knee in February and was lost for the season. Duke finished 18–13.

Senior year, 1996–97: the payoff. Wallace was named team captain of a squad that now included Langdon, McLeod, Carrawell, and Wojciechowski. Duke won the ACC regular-season championship. Over 82 career games, he averaged 3.4 PPG and 1.7 RPG while shooting 43.3% from three. The numbers don’t tell the story. The captaincy does.
bio.after:
Wallace graduated from Duke in 1997 with degrees in history and sociology. He went directly into the sports industry, joining Steinberg Moorad & Dunn — one of the most powerful agencies in America — as a marketing specialist. His first major client connection was Drew Bledsoe, the Patriots quarterback.

In February 2001, Wallace became a founding member of Athletes First, which would grow into one of the largest sports agencies in the world. He was there from the very first draft class — 2001, with first-round picks Reggie Wayne and Jamal Reynolds. Over two decades, he has assisted with contract negotiations for Carson Palmer, Aaron Rodgers, Ray Lewis, Clay Matthews, and Miles Austin. He has been present at more than seventeen NFL Drafts from the inside.
bio.now:
Carmen Wallace is an NFL and NBA Certified Contract Advisor at Athletes First, based in Laguna Hills, California. His role encompasses salary cap strategy, marketing, and full-service client representation. He became NBA-certified in 2024, expanding from football to basketball — a full-circle return to the sport where his career began.

In 2024, Tower Hill School honored him with its Distinguished Alumni Award. He has spoken at Tower Hill’s Forum speaker series about recruiting, training, and placing athletes — and about giving back.

When he blogs from the NFL Draft, Wallace still thinks about Coach K. “It makes me think back to something Coach K used to say to us before games,” he wrote. “I was captain of the Duke basketball team way back in 1997.” That sentence contains the entire arc. He was captain in the year Duke came back.

Carmen Wallace scored 2,004 points in a Delaware prep school. He went to Duke and endured the worst four-year stretch in program history. He played power forward at 6’5 because Coach K needed him to. He tore a knee. He came back and captained the team that won the 1997 ACC title. He graduated, walked into sports management, and helped build Athletes First from its founding into one of the most powerful agencies in the world. He has negotiated contracts for Aaron Rodgers, Carson Palmer, and Ray Lewis. The kid from Tower Hill who averaged 3.4 points at Duke now represents athletes whose contracts are measured in the hundreds of millions. The Brotherhood is not just about the players who scored the most points. It is about the ones who stayed when staying was hardest. Carmen Wallace stayed. And then he built an empire.

players.name: Shane Battier
players.tagline: The No-Stat All-Star. The soul of the 2001 champions.
players.now: Operating Partner, Garnett Station; minority owner, Tampa Bay Rays
players.stat: 146 games • Naismith POY • 3x DPOY • 2x NBA champ • Final Four MOP

bio.road:
Shane Battier grew up in Birmingham, Michigan, the only child of a Black father and a white mother. By seventh grade at Detroit Country Day School, he was already 6-foot-4. A year later, 6-foot-7. He was also the only biracial kid at Country Day, a prestigious private school in the Detroit suburbs. Michael Lewis, who profiled Battier in a landmark 2009 New York Times Magazine piece, captured the tension: the inner-city AAU kids treated Battier as a suburban kid with a white game, while his suburban teammates treated him like a visitor from another planet.

Battier turned that in-between status into fuel. He led Country Day to three consecutive Class B state championships from 1995 to 1997, earned Michigan Mr. Basketball as a senior, and graduated with a 3.96 GPA while being named the school’s outstanding student. He was the Naismith Prep Player of the Year in 1997 — the first signal of the award he’d bookend four years later.

Dean Smith recruited him for North Carolina. Battier chose Duke instead, beginning one of the most decorated four-year careers in the program’s history.
bio.duke:
Battier was the prototype of the modern do-everything forward before the term existed. At 6-foot-8, 220 pounds, he could guard every position on the floor, shoot threes at a 41.6% clip, post up smaller defenders, and anchor a defense. The Cameron Crazies honored his relentless charge-taking with a chant: ‘Who’s your daddy? Battier!’

His freshman year (1997–98) was solid but ended in heartbreak: Duke blew a 17-point lead to Kentucky in the regional finals, a loss to the eventual national champion. As a sophomore, the Blue Devils reached the Final Four before falling to Connecticut. The program was building toward something. It arrived in 2000–01.

That senior season was one of the greatest individual years in Duke history. Battier averaged 19.9 points, 7.3 rebounds, 2.3 blocks, and 2.1 steals while leading the Blue Devils to a 35–4 record, an undefeated ACC regular season (16–0), and the national championship. In the title game against Arizona, he scored 21 points with six rebounds and four assists. He was named Final Four MOP, consensus National Player of the Year (Naismith, Wooden, AP, Rupp), NABC Defensive Player of the Year for the third straight time, and Academic All-American of the Year.

He is the only player in history to win both the Naismith Prep Player of the Year and the Naismith College Player of the Year. Duke retired his No. 31, and he was inducted into the College Basketball Hall of Fame in 2019. Over 146 career games: 13.6 points, 6.1 rebounds, a 50/41/78 shooting line, and a career record of 131–15. He graduated with a degree in religion.
bio.after:
Memphis took Battier sixth overall in the 2001 draft. He spent five seasons with the Grizzlies (2001–06), becoming their defensive anchor and averaging 10.6 points, 4.8 rebounds, and 1.0 blocks. But his value transcended the box score — a theme that would define his entire professional life.

The Houston Rockets acquired Battier in 2006, and it was there that his statistical legend crystallized. The Rockets made him their only player with access to the team’s highly sophisticated analytical data on opposing players. Battier studied film and data obsessively, using it to contest shots from the exact angles where opponents were least comfortable. He earned NBA All-Defensive Second Team honors in both 2008 and 2009. Michael Lewis’ 2009 profile, ‘The No-Stat All-Star,’ demonstrated that Battier’s teams consistently performed better with him on the court despite his unremarkable counting stats. Harvard Business School turned his approach into a 2010 case study.

In 2011, Battier signed with the Miami Heat and stepped into the most important role of his career: the glue guy on a super-team. Playing alongside LeBron James, Dwyane Wade, and Chris Bosh, Battier provided the defense, the corner threes, and the veteran steadiness that championship teams need from their supporting cast. The Heat won back-to-back titles in 2012 and 2013. Battier retired after the 2013–14 season at age 35. In his final season, the NBA named him the inaugural Teammate of the Year. Career totals: 977 games, 8.6 points, 4.2 rebounds, 38.4% from three.
bio.now:
Battier briefly worked for ESPN as an analyst before the Heat hired him as their Vice President of Analytics and Basketball Development in February 2017. He served until 2021, when he transitioned to a consulting role with the organization.

Since then, Battier has built a diversified post-basketball portfolio. He became an operating partner at Garnett Station Partners, a private equity firm, in December 2023. He serves on the board of directors at Yext, a tech company. He speaks regularly at the MIT Sloan Sports Analytics Conference and corporate events. He has advised Intel on their sports technology division.

In October 2025, Battier joined the ownership group of the Tampa Bay Rays as a minority owner, part of the Patrick Zalupski-led consortium that purchased the franchise. It marked his first foray into professional sports ownership.

In 2010, Battier and his wife Heidi — his high school sweetheart from Country Day — founded the Battier Take Charge Foundation. The organization provides scholarships, mentorship, and leadership training for underserved youth in Miami, Houston, Detroit, and Camden, New Jersey. As of 2025, more than 84% of Battier Scholars are first-generation college students, and over 95% have completed a bachelor’s degree or higher. In 2024, the foundation received a $1.4 million grant from the KPMG US Foundation. After relocating to Charlotte, North Carolina, in mid-2024, Battier established a new outpost for the foundation there.

At 47, Battier lives in Charlotte with Heidi. He is, in many ways, the Brotherhood’s most complete post-playing success story: championships at every level, a pioneering role in basketball analytics, a thriving business career, a meaningful philanthropic legacy, and now a stake in a professional sports franchise.

The kid who navigated between two worlds in suburban Detroit — too Black for one, too white for the other — found a way to belong everywhere. His No. 31 hangs in the Cameron rafters, and his influence hangs over every team that values defense, data, and the things that don’t show up in the box score.

highlights.note: NBA All-Rookie First Team
NBA Champion with Miami Heat (started Game 7 of Finals)
2x NBA Champion with Miami Heat

nba.postCareer: Operating Partner, Garnett Station; minority owner, Tampa Bay Rays

sources.title: Shane Battier - The No-Stats All-Star - New York Times Magazine
Shane Battier Miami HEAT Analytics Role - Miami Herald

players.name: Elton Brand
players.tagline: NABC POY as a sophomore. Left early and never looked back.
players.now: GM, Philadelphia 76ers
players.stat: 16.2 ppg • 8.9 rpg • 37–2 • first 16–0 ACC season • Naismith POY

bio.road:
Elton Tyron Brand was born March 11, 1979, in Cortlandt Manor, New York, and grew up in Peekskill — a small, gritty city on the Hudson River about an hour north of Manhattan. At age thirteen, he enrolled at Peekskill High School and was immediately placed on the varsity roster. By the time he graduated, he was averaging 40 points and 20 rebounds per game — numbers so absurd they sounded invented. He played AAU basketball with future NBA players Lamar Odom and Ron Artest.

He was named New York State Mr. Basketball, a McDonald’s All-American, and Parade Magazine’s High School Player of the Year. He chose Duke, arriving in the fall of 1997 alongside Shane Battier and a freshman class that would define the next era of the program.
bio.duke:
Brand’s Duke career lasted only two seasons, but they were among the most productive in program history. As a freshman in 1997–98, he averaged 13.4 points and 7.3 rebounds and was named ACC Rookie of the Year. Duke reached the regional finals before blowing a 17-point lead to eventual national champion Kentucky.

His sophomore season was a masterpiece. Brand averaged 17.7 points, 9.8 rebounds, and 3.1 blocks per game while leading the Blue Devils to a 37–2 record and the first undefeated ACC regular season (16–0) in league history. He was named consensus National Player of the Year — winning the Naismith, Wooden, and Associated Press awards — ACC Player of the Year, and a consensus First-Team All-American. Duke reached the national championship game, where they fell to Connecticut 77–74.

Over two seasons: 72 games, 16.2 points, 8.9 rebounds, 50.0% shooting. He was the most physically dominant Duke player since Danny Ferry, perhaps since Laettner — but with a low-post power game neither of them possessed. He declared for the NBA Draft after his sophomore year.
bio.after:
The Chicago Bulls selected Brand with the first overall pick in the 1999 NBA Draft. He shared the NBA Rookie of the Year award with Steve Francis after averaging 20.1 points and 10.0 rebounds — the first player to average 20/10 as a rookie since Tim Duncan. He was traded to the Los Angeles Clippers in 2001 and became the franchise’s best player, earning two All-Star selections (2002, 2006) and an All-NBA Second Team nod in 2006, when he averaged 24.2 points per game.

In 2008, Brand signed a five-year, $82 million free-agent contract with the Philadelphia 76ers. An Achilles tendon injury lingered, and Brand was never the same explosive player. He was amnestied in 2012 with $18 million still owed. He spent time with the Dallas Mavericks and Atlanta Hawks before retiring in August 2015. In January 2016, he came out of retirement at Coach K’s urging, signing with the Sixers to mentor their young core during the Process era. He played 26 games at age 36, becoming the 51st player in NBA history to reach 9,000 career rebounds. Career totals: 17 seasons, 1,058 games, 15.9 points, 8.5 rebounds, 1.7 blocks, 50.0% FG. Approximately $172 million in NBA salary.
bio.now:
Brand transitioned to the front office immediately. In 2016, the Sixers hired him as a player development consultant. By 2017, he was GM of the Delaware Blue Coats (G League affiliate). On September 20, 2018, the Sixers named Brand their general manager — a meteoric rise from retirement to running a franchise in less than three years.

As GM, Brand traded for Jimmy Butler and Tobias Harris, pushing the eventual champion Toronto Raptors to a dramatic seven-game series decided by Kawhi Leonard’s buzzer-beater. But the offseason that followed haunted him: Harris re-signed for $180 million, Simmons extended for $170 million, Horford signed for $97 million — while Butler and JJ Redick left. Butler led Miami to the Finals twice. Horford was traded after one season. Simmons eventually refused to play.

In late 2020, the Sixers hired Daryl Morey as President of Basketball Operations. Brand’s contract was extended, but his role shifted behind the scenes. In the 2025 NBA Draft, he selected VJ Edgecombe 3rd overall.

At 46, Brand remains GM of the 76ers in his seventh year. He is one of only a handful of former No. 1 overall picks to transition into a senior front-office role. The kid from Peekskill who averaged 40 and 20 in high school and played AAU ball with Lamar Odom and Ron Artest now builds rosters, evaluates draft prospects, and navigates the salary cap.

highlights.note: NBA Rookie of the Year; 20.1 PPG/10.0 RPG
2x NBA All-Star (2002, 2006)
Led Clippers to first playoff series win in 30 years

nba.postCareer: GM, Philadelphia 76ers

sources.title: Elton Brand 76ers GM Tenure - Philadelphia Inquirer
Elton Brand - Sports Reference College Basketball
Elton Brand Duke Career - 1999 National Player of the Year - Duke Athletics

players.name: Jay Williams
players.tagline: The motorcycle accident that changed everything.
players.now: ESPN NBA analyst; author; College Basketball Hall of Fame (2017); motivational speaker
players.stat: 109 games • 2,079 pts (6th all-time) • 2x NABC POY • Naismith + Wooden • 2001 NCAA Champion • Jersey #22 retired

bio.road:
Jason David Williams was born September 10, 1981, in Plainfield, New Jersey — a city of stark racial and economic divides. His father, David, worked in management information systems for American Express. His mother, Althea, was a guidance counselor who would earn a second master’s degree while working full-time as assistant principal at North Plainfield High. Jay was their only biological child. Strict household. Education first. You never quit something you committed to.

But the Williams family expanded unexpectedly. Althea was counseling a girl named Jackie Taylor, whose mother had died of cancer. David and Althea brought Jackie home. Never officially adopted her, but raised her, put her through school. ‘They’re mom and dad,’ Taylor said. Jay had a sister.

In fourth grade, David and Althea put up a hoop in the backyard. They had to make Jay come inside at night because of the snow. By fifth grade he’d taken the coach’s son’s job at point guard. He tried everything — karate, volleyball, soccer, baseball, tennis, swimming, chess. He was the state volleyball player of the year as a senior. But basketball consumed him.

At St. Joseph High School in Metuchen, he started all four varsity years. School records: 1,977 points, 407 steals. Senior stats: 19 PPG, 7.0 APG, 4.2 RPG, 3.7 SPG. New Jersey Player of the Year. Parade and USA Today All-American. McDonald’s All-American (20 points in the game). Morgan Wootten Award. 3.6 GPA. David and Althea rarely missed a game despite international travel schedules. Althea started the first girls’ cheerleading squad at the all-boys school. His coach, Mark Taylor: ‘They’re a special family.’
bio.duke:
Williams became one of the few Duke freshmen to average double figures in scoring (14.5 PPG, 6.5 APG). ACC Rookie of the Year. National Freshman of the Year.

Sophomore year was the championship. He started all 39 games alongside Shane Battier, the program’s moral compass. Averaged 21.6 PPG — the first Duke player since Danny Ferry (1989) to lead the ACC in scoring. Also 6.1 APG (2nd in ACC). NABC Player of the Year. Led all NCAA Tournament scorers at 25.7 PPG. Duke beat Arizona 82–72 in Minneapolis for Coach K’s third national title. Williams and Battier combined for over 1,500 points — one of only two Duke duos to each score 700+ in a single year.

Then the decision that defined his character. Projected lottery pick. Could have left for millions. He stayed. His father: ‘If you kept the larger picture in mind, the short-term things will work themselves out.’ His mother was simultaneously earning her second master’s. ‘It was quite a joy to be writing a paper and staying up all night while he was writing a paper and staying up all night. The problem came in that I had to go to work.’

Junior year was a coronation. 21.3 PPG, 5.7 APG, 3.5 RPG. His 841 points broke Dick Groat’s 49-year Duke scoring record. Set the NCAA Tournament record for three-pointers attempted. Won both the Naismith and Wooden Awards. Consensus first-team All-American for the second straight year. Two-time NABC POY. Graduated in three years with a sociology degree — the first Duke athlete to do so. Jersey #22 retired on Senior Day. Career: 2,079 points, 6th all-time in Duke history.

Coach K later described him as having ‘as fast a first step as any point guard in the history of basketball.’
bio.after:
The Chicago Bulls took Williams second overall in the 2002 draft, behind only Yao Ming. He was given Michael Jordan’s locker. He had a three-unit apartment on the 40th floor of the Park Hyatt on Michigan Avenue — floor-to-ceiling windows, Lake Michigan stretching out like an ocean. He was 20 years old.

Rookie season: 75 games, 9.5 PPG, 4.7 APG. Inconsistent but promising. Triple-double against the Nets. All-Rookie Second Team. Represented the US at the 2002 FIBA World Championship.

Then June 19, 2003. A perfect Chicago summer day. He took his black Yamaha R6 with red accents instead of the Corvette. No helmet. No motorcycle license. His contract forbade it. ‘I was that prototypical young hotshot who thought he had all the answers. Motorcycle license? What for? Money and arrogance were all I needed.’

He crashed into a streetlight at Belmont and Honore in Roscoe Village. Fractured pelvis. Severed main nerve in his leg. Three torn ligaments including ACL. Life-threatening internal bleeding. Doctors considered amputation. In an instant, the man with the fastest first step in basketball could no longer walk.

The Bulls drafted Kirk Hinrich a week later. They waived Williams but gave him $3 million for rehab despite having no contractual obligation. He became addicted to OxyContin and other painkillers. Depression. ‘For the longest time I felt like being alive was more of a consolation prize.’ The Nets signed him in 2006 — released after three weeks. Austin Toros D-League — three games, waived. Career over at 25.
bio.now:
Jay Williams rebuilt himself from the wreckage. Not quickly. Not cleanly. But completely. Coach K’s “next play” philosophy — don’t dwell on what happened, move forward — became the operating system for his second life. He went from NBA millions to $30,000 at ESPN.

He is now one of ESPN’s most prominent basketball voices: NBA Countdown, studio shows, game telecasts. He co-hosted the “Keyshawn, JWill & Max Show” on ESPN Radio. Launched “The Limits with Jay Williams” podcast on NPR. Motivational speaker. Co-founded Simatree consulting. Author of Life Is Not an Accident: A Memoir of Reinvention (2016). Grant Hill: ‘Jay’s story is about strength, resilience, and redemption.’ Charles Barkley: ‘A lot of athletes have been to the mountaintop and back to the bottom. But not Jay.’

Inducted into the College Basketball Hall of Fame in 2017. Married Nikki Bonacorsi in May 2018. Daughter Amelia Brooklyn-Rose (October 2018). Son Zane David. Lives in Plainfield, New Jersey — the same town where David and Althea put up a hoop in the backyard and had to make their boy come inside because of the snow.

He is 43 years old. He had everything — a national championship, the Wooden Award, the #2 pick, Jordan’s locker, the fastest first step in basketball — and threw it into a streetlight on a perfect June evening because he thought he was invincible. He wasn’t. But the crash that almost killed him at twenty-one was not the end of his story. It was the beginning of the one that mattered. He is convinced the accident was no accident. It was a tragedy that taught him how to live.

charity.name: The Jay Williams Foundation
charity.label: Give Back with Jay Williams
charity.description: Jay Williams advocates for mental health awareness and second chances, drawing on his own experience recovering from a near-fatal motorcycle accident and addiction. His work focuses on youth mentorship and resilience.

highlights.note: Career ended by motorcycle accident June 2003; suffered severed nerve in left leg, fractured pelvis, dislocated knee

nba.postCareer: ESPN analyst and commentator; motivational speaker; author of Life Is Not an Accident; podcast host

sources.title: Jay Williams Life Is Not an Accident - Book
Jay Williams - Sports Reference College Basketball
Jay Williams 2001 NCAA Championship and Motorcycle Accident - ESPN

players.name: Carlos Boozer
players.tagline: Duke to the Dream Team.
players.now: Utah Jazz front office; ACC Network analyst; author; Naismith HOF (2008 Team USA)
players.stat: 109 college games • 2001 NCAA Champion • 13 NBA seasons • 16.2 PPG/9.5 RPG • 2x All-Star • Olympic Gold 2008

bio.road:
Carlos Austin Boozer Jr. was born November 20, 1981, at a U.S. military base in Aschaffenburg, West Germany. His father, Carlos Sr., was an Army sergeant. His mother Renee raised five children while both parents worked two jobs. The family settled in Washington, D.C., after Carlos Sr.’s Army service ended.

What happened next changed everything. Six-year-old Carlos was playing neighborhood basketball with his friend Chris. Chris made a basket and knocked another player down. An older kid — a gang member — pulled a gun from his backpack and shot Chris. Then rode away on his bicycle. Chris died in Carlos Boozer’s arms.

Carlos Sr. and Renee loaded the car and drove 2,800 miles to Bellingham, Washington. A ferry carried them to Juneau, Alaska. They were looking for the safest place in America.

Juneau was another planet. Population 30,000. Rainforest. Eagles. Glaciers. No roads in or out. ‘I was very different. My swag was different. I’m coming into school in that old ’85 Dodge van, bumping Tupac and Biggie, and nobody had ever heard of those rappers. They embraced me.’ The family lived in an apartment in Douglas, seven people, lower-middle class. No basketball hoop. So Carlos Sr. drove his son thirty minutes to Auke Bay Elementary, where there was a covered outdoor court. Even in snow: homework, wait for Dad, drive to Auke Bay, go at it for two hours, come home for dinner. Every single day for four years.

At Juneau-Douglas High: 95–12 record. Back-to-back state titles (1997, 1998). Three-time Alaska POY. Two-time Parade All-American. Senior: 28 PPG/14 RPG. Gatorade National POY. McDonald’s All-American (22 pts/11 reb). ‘Think about a kid at 12 years old, in Juneau, Alaska, telling his parents, “I want to go to the NBA.”’ His parents let him dream. Coach K came to Juneau. Boozer chose Duke.
bio.duke:
Boozer arrived in 1999 with Jay Williams. ACC All-Freshman Team. Sophomore year was the masterpiece: Williams, Boozer, Shane Battier, Mike Dunleavy Jr., Chris Duhon — one of the most talented Duke rosters ever. Boozer was the 6-9, 260-pound power forward with a soft touch and a competitive fire forged on that outdoor court in Auke Bay.

Duke went 35–4 and won the 2001 NCAA Championship, beating Arizona 82–72 in Minneapolis. Coach K’s third national title. Boozer averaged double figures alongside Williams (ACC scoring leader) and Battier (National POY).

Junior year, with Williams and Battier gone, Boozer stepped into the starring role: 18.2 PPG, 8.7 RPG. He, Williams, and Dunleavy became the only Duke trio to each score 600+ in a season. 2002 ACC Tournament MVP. NABC Third-Team All-American. Duke reached the Sweet Sixteen. After the season, Boozer declared for the draft.
bio.after:
Drafted 35th overall — massively undervalued. Rookie in Cleveland: 10.0 PPG, 7.5 RPG, All-Rookie Second Team. By year two: 15.5 PPG, 11.4 RPG alongside a rookie named LeBron James.

Then the controversy. The Cavaliers released Boozer from his $695K contract expecting him to re-sign for ~$39M. Instead, he signed a 6-year, $70M deal with Utah. Cleveland’s owner: ‘I decided to trust Carlos. He did not show that trust and respect in return.’ Boozer maintained NBA tampering rules forced his hand. The episode tagged him with a reputation he never fully shook.

In Utah he became a star: 2x All-Star (2007, 2008). Averaged 20+ PPG in 2006–07, leading the Jazz to the Western Conference Finals. Scored 41 against Houston in the playoffs. Six seasons in Utah: 19.3 PPG, 10.6 RPG.

Olympics: bronze in Athens 2004, then the legendary “Redeem Team” gold in Beijing 2008 alongside LeBron, Kobe, Wade, CP3, and Carmelo. The 2008 team was inducted into the Naismith HOF in 2025.

Chicago Bulls: 5-year, $80M contract (2010). Helped the Bulls win the East’s top seed in 2010–11 with Derrick Rose. Four seasons. Lakers 2014–15. Guangdong Southern Tigers (China) 2016–17. Retired. 861 NBA games, ~$130M career earnings.
bio.now:
The most important story of Carlos Boozer’s life has nothing to do with basketball.

On May 31, 2006, his firstborn son Carmani was diagnosed with sickle cell disease. Both Carlos and wife CeCe (Cindy Blackwell, whom he’d met when she was at NC State and he was at Duke) carried the gene. ‘The thought of me losing my son, what do I do? Do I put my kid through a life full of torture? Or do I roll the dice?’

They rolled the dice. Doctors at Emory told them about a bone marrow transplant using stem cells from a sibling’s umbilical cord. The Boozers conceived through IVF — screening embryos to be sickle-free and bone marrow matches. 34 eggs harvested. 10 viable embryos. Two perfect matches. Both implanted.

On July 18, 2007, Cameron and Cayden Boozer were born. Three weeks later, doctors transplanted their umbilical cord stem cells into Carmani. He spent 40 nights in Miami Children’s Hospital. It worked. Carmani was cured.

Those twins became the most dominant high school basketball duo in America. Cameron: 6-9, first player since LeBron to win Mr. Basketball USA (2023, 2025), projected top pick 2026 NBA Draft. Cayden: 6-4 guard, five-star recruit, USA Basketball gold medalist. Three straight Florida state championships at Christopher Columbus High. ESPN’s 2024 documentary Blood Brothers told their story.

Both committed to Duke on October 11, 2024. They are on the 2025–26 Blue Devils roster. The Brotherhood is now literal: a father’s legacy continued by sons who began their lives saving someone else’s.

Carlos joined the Utah Jazz front office in May 2025. ACC Network analyst. Published Every Shot Counts (2023). Still runs camps in Juneau every summer; proceeds go to sickle cell research. His parents moved back to Juneau. ‘We missed Juneau. We came back and decided to live here the rest of our lives.’

He is 43. Born on a military base in Germany. His childhood friend died in his arms. His parents drove 2,800 miles to Alaska. He shot baskets in the snow for four years. Won a championship at Duke, made $130 million, won Olympic gold. Then watched his firstborn diagnosed with a disease that could kill him — and conceived twins through IVF whose bone marrow cured their brother. Those twins are now at Duke, chasing the same championship their father won in 2001. And his parents are back in Juneau, where everybody knows their neighbor’s dog’s name.

charity.name: Sickle Cell Disease Association of America
charity.label: Support Sickle Cell Research
charity.description: Carlos Boozer’s son Carmani was diagnosed with sickle cell disease in 2006. The Boozer family’s IVF story, documented in ESPN’s Blood Brothers, raised awareness of sickle cell disease and the importance of bone marrow transplant research.

highlights.note: Averaged 15.5/11.4 alongside LeBron James in Cleveland
2x NBA All-Star (2007, 2008)
Averaged 21.1/10.4 with Utah

nba.postCareer: Utah Jazz front office; ACC Network analyst; author; Naismith HOF (2008 Team USA)

sources.title: Carlos Boozer - Sports Reference College Basketball
Carlos Boozer Utah Jazz Career - Deseret News

players.name: Mike Dunleavy Jr.
players.tagline: The coach’s son who carved his own path.
players.now: General Manager, Golden State Warriors
players.stat: 3 Duke seasons • 2001 NCAA Champion (team-high 21 in title game) • Consensus All-American • 15 NBA seasons • 986 games

bio.road:
Michael Joseph Dunleavy Jr. was born September 15, 1980, in Fort Worth, Texas. His father, Mike Dunleavy Sr., was an NBA player whose career spanned 11 seasons before chronic back pain forced his retirement. His mother Emily raised three boys — Mike Jr., Baker, and James — in a household that never stayed in one city long. Basketball was the family business. It still is: Baker played at Villanova and coached at Quinnipiac, James played at USC and became an NBA agent.

When Mike Jr. was ten, his father took the head coaching job with the Los Angeles Lakers. The team featured Magic Johnson and James Worthy. The 1990–91 Lakers reached the NBA Finals before losing to Jordan’s Bulls. A ten-year-old boy had a front-row seat to the end of one era and the beginning of another. ‘Magic Johnson is one of the great athletes, leaders, and winners of all time. To be around somebody like that was great.’

The family moved when the jobs moved. Milwaukee (Bucks), where Mike Jr. attended University School of Milwaukee and Homestead High. Then Portland (Trail Blazers), where his father won NBA Coach of the Year in 1999. Mike transferred to Jesuit High School in Beaverton, Oregon — and did something almost no high schooler gets to do: he practiced against his father’s players. Steve Smith. Bonzi Wells. Rasheed Wallace. ‘Hey, I was a 16-year-old kid playing against pros. It definitely made me better.’ He led Jesuit to the 1999 Oregon 4A State Championship, 65–38 over North Salem.

The biggest lesson was what he saw, not what he was told. ‘Preparation was my dad’s biggest thing. I remember him being up late at night, all night, watching film. That’s something I’ve applied to my craft.’ He chose Duke. Coach K saw in him the most versatile forward since Grant Hill.
bio.duke:
Dunleavy arrived in 1999 alongside Jay Williams, Carlos Boozer, and Chris Duhon. All four became NBA players. Three were first-round picks.

Sophomore year was the championship. Duke went 35–4 with Williams leading the ACC in scoring, Battier winning National POY, Boozer providing muscle inside. Dunleavy was the Swiss Army knife — 6-9 with shooting touch, passing vision, and the basketball IQ of a kid raised in film sessions. In the title game against Arizona: team-high 21 points, including key three-pointers that stretched the lead. Duke won 82–72. That game was the announcement: the coach’s kid wasn’t just along for the ride.

Junior year, with Battier gone: 17.3 PPG, 7.2 RPG. He, Williams, and Boozer each scored 600+ points — a feat matched at Duke only by Scheyer/Singler/Nolan Smith. Consensus second-team All-American. First-team NABC All-American. Duke went 31–4, reached the Sweet Sixteen. He declared for the draft.
bio.after:
Golden State selected Dunleavy 3rd overall in 2002 — one spot behind Duke teammate Jay Williams. Five-year, $44M extension in 2005. But early-2000s Golden State was a wasteland. The #3 pick averaged 12 points on a team that couldn’t make the playoffs. He became an unfair symbol of franchise mediocrity. Traded to Indiana in January 2007.

With the Pacers, Dunleavy became the player Duke always knew he could be. First full season: all 82 starts, career-high 19.1 PPG. A legitimate three-level scoring wing. But injuries (wrist, then nagging issues) slowed him. By the time Indiana returned to the postseason in 2010–11, he’d waited 624 games and nine seasons without a playoff appearance — the longest drought in the Brotherhood.

Milwaukee (2011–13). Then Chicago, where everything clicked: alongside Derrick Rose, Joakim Noah, and Jimmy Butler. April 25, 2014: playoff career-high 35 points with 8 three-pointers against Washington — a Bulls franchise playoff record. Cleveland briefly (2016 championship roster). Atlanta (2017, final stop). Retired June 30, 2017.

Fifteen seasons. Six franchises. 986 games — second-most in the 2002 draft class. Career: 11.2 PPG, 4.5 RPG, 37.7% from three. ~$80M in earnings.
bio.now:
The son of a coach became something his father never was: a general manager of an NBA dynasty.

After retiring, Dunleavy joined the Golden State Warriors’ front office. The franchise that drafted him #3 in 2002 — when the Warriors were terrible and Dunleavy was the symbol of their futility — was now the NBA’s preeminent organization, built around Steph Curry, Klay Thompson, and Draymond Green. He rose through the ranks and was named General Manager.

During the 2025–26 trade deadline, he navigated rumors of a Giannis Antetokounmpo pursuit and managed a franchise in transition — aging champions, salary cap constraints, the pressure to compete while planning for the future. The preparation his father modeled at two in the morning is now his daily reality.

Married Sarah, whom he met at Duke. Three children. A residential sports camp in Eagle River, Wisconsin — Camp Menominee — named its basketball courts ‘The Mike Dunleavy Jr. Courts.’ The Dunleavy family has been in professional basketball, without interruption, for nearly fifty years.

He is 45. Born in Fort Worth. Never stayed anywhere long because his father’s career kept the family moving. Watched Magic Johnson at ten. Guarded Rasheed Wallace at sixteen. Scored a team-high 21 in a championship game at twenty. Was the #3 pick who spent nine years without making the playoffs. Hit 8 threes in a playoff game for the Bulls at thirty-three. Played 986 games and retired quietly at thirty-six. Now he runs the Warriors. The franchise that once represented everything that had gone wrong is the franchise he’s charged with keeping great. The coach’s kid became the coach’s boss.

highlights.note: Career-best 17.1 PPG with Golden State
Key 3-and-D role for Chicago Bulls playoff runs

nba.postCareer: General Manager, Golden State Warriors

players.name: Chris Duhon
players.tagline: The four-year floor general.
players.now: Head coach, Gaston Christian School (NC); Greater New Orleans Sports HOF (2024)
players.stat: 133 starts • 123–21 record • Duke all-time: steals (300), minutes (4,813) • 2001 NCAA Champion • 3 ACC titles

bio.road:
Christopher Nicholas Duhon was born August 31, 1982, in Mamou, Louisiana — a Cajun prairie town of about 3,000 people in the heart of Evangeline Parish. He was raised primarily by his mother, Vivian Harper, a former high school point guard who introduced her son to basketball before he could spell the word. He has a younger brother, Thomas.

The family moved from Mamou to New Orleans, then to Slidell — a suburban community on the north shore of Lake Pontchartrain — when Chris was ten. There was a weathered rim in the family driveway. Duhon shot on it every day. His mother’s guidance was constant. She had been a point guard herself. She knew the position. She understood that the game was about making other people better.

At Salmen High School in Slidell, Duhon became the best player in Louisiana. Senior year: Louisiana Mr. Basketball. McDonald’s All-American. Parade All-American. McDonald’s National HS Player of the Year. Won the McDonald’s Three-Point Shootout. USA Basketball Male Athlete of the Year. Ranked #13 nationally by ESPN, #6 by RSCI.

‘Growing up in Louisiana, LSU was always the school you kind of always wanted to go to. I thought that was the only place I would be able to go.’ But when every program in America called, he chose Duke. ‘I thought Duke gave me the best of both worlds.’
bio.duke:
Duhon arrived in 2000 with Jay Williams, Carlos Boozer, and Mike Dunleavy Jr. He was the point guard — the table-setter. Freshman year: started all 39 games, initially backing up Williams, then moving into the starting lineup alongside him when Boozer was injured. Duke went 35–4 and won the 2001 NCAA Championship.

What made Duhon irreplaceable was durability, steadiness, and defense. 133 starts in four years. 4,813 minutes — most in Duke history. 300 steals — most in Duke history. 819 assists — second in Duke history. A 123–21 record — second-winningest player in ACC history behind teammate Shane Battier. Three ACC Championships. The only ACC player to amass 1,000+ points, 400+ rebounds, 700+ assists, and 200+ steals.

Senior year: 10 PPG, 6 APG, 2.2 SPG, 4.1 RPG. Duke reached the Final Four. Duhon was a finalist for the Wooden Award, Naismith Award, and Rupp Trophy. Not flashy enough to win. Too important to ignore.

What did Coach K teach him? ‘You just learned how to be a man. Everything he taught us was about taking responsibility, and going out and working hard for what you want.’ Regrets about staying four years? ‘No, no regrets at all. Being in college for four years helped me play at a high level. It came with maturity and understanding the game; becoming a man.’
bio.after:
Drafted 38th overall by Chicago in 2004. Rookie year: all 82 games, 5.9 PPG, 4.9 APG. Hit 8 of 9 threes in a comeback win over Atlanta — a Bulls franchise record. In 2006–07, the Bulls won their first playoff series since the Jordan era, sweeping Miami. Duhon was the sixth man who kept the machine running. Career-high 34 points with 9 assists against Golden State in February 2008.

New York Knicks: 2-year, $12M deal. November 29, 2008: 22 assists against Golden State — a Knicks franchise single-game record. The kid from Mamou owned the Garden for one night.

But the most important thing Duhon did had nothing to do with basketball. In August 2005, Hurricane Katrina devastated the Gulf Coast. Slidell was hammered. Duhon founded the Stand Tall Foundation and distributed over 3,000 boxes of supplies worth $450,000 to Slidell residents. Thanksgiving dinners. Free youth clinics. The kid who shot on a weathered driveway rim came back to feed the neighbors who watched him grow up.

Orlando (4-year, $15M). Lakers (2012–13, final season). Nine NBA seasons. 606 games. 6.5 PPG, 4.4 APG. ~$33 million in career earnings. Never an All-Star. Always the glue.
bio.now:
The transition wasn’t smooth. In November 2015, while coaching at Marshall, Duhon was arrested for aggravated DUI. In January 2017, arrested again for driving on a revoked license. He resigned from Marshall three days later. A painful public reckoning for a man who’d built his identity on steadiness.

He rebuilt. Illinois State coaching staff as an assistant (2018). In 2023, his wife Andrea — a mathematics professor — accepted a position at Davidson College. The family relocated to Gastonia, North Carolina. Duhon became head coach of the boys’ basketball team at Gaston Christian School. His eldest son Jeremy plays on the team. Five children: Jeremy, Eliana, Christopher, Faith, and Miya. The family chose the move to be together.

Inducted into the Greater New Orleans Sports Hall of Fame in 2024. His cousin is Jarrett Jack, the longtime NBA point guard. Point guard bloodlines run in the family.

He is 42. Born in Mamou, Louisiana, population 3,000. His mother was a point guard. He shot on a weathered rim in the driveway in Slidell. Chose Duke over LSU. Started 133 games, played more minutes and recorded more steals than anyone in Duke history. Won a championship as a freshman, reached the Final Four as a senior. Set the Knicks’ all-time single-game assist record. When Katrina destroyed his hometown, he drove 3,000 boxes of supplies back to Slidell and fed his neighbors. He stumbled after basketball — the DUI, the resignation — but he stood back up, which is what point guards do. Now he coaches his son’s high school team. His wife teaches math at Davidson. The foundation is still called Stand Tall.

charity.name: Stand Tall Foundation
charity.label: Support Chris Duhon’s Stand Tall Foundation
charity.description: Founded after Hurricane Katrina, the Stand Tall Foundation has distributed over $450,000 in supplies to Slidell, Louisiana, and continues to serve communities through youth clinics and outreach.

highlights.note: Set NBA record with 22 assists in a game (since broken)
Started 82 games as Bulls rookie PG

nba.postCareer: Head coach, Gaston Christian School (NC); Greater New Orleans Sports HOF (2024)

players.name: Luol Deng
players.tagline: From Sudanese refugee to NBA All-Star.
players.now: President, South Sudan Basketball Federation; OBE; led South Sudan to 2024 Olympics
players.drafted: 1st Rd, 7th Overall — Phoenix (traded to Chicago Bulls)
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 15.1 PPG/6.9 RPG • Final Four • 15 NBA seasons • 2x All-Star • All-Defensive Team

bio.road:
Luol Ajou Deng was born April 16, 1985, in Wau, in what is now South Sudan. He is a member of the Dinka ethnic group. His father, Aldo Ajou Deng, was a member of the Sudanese parliament. His mother, Martha Leon John, raised nine children. When Luol was three, his father moved the entire family out of Sudan to escape the Second Sudanese Civil War. They settled in a tiny apartment in Cairo. They were refugees. They had nothing.

‘My parents were my greatest role models. What we went through, how they were always positive. They never lost their edge. My mom is very religious and we always just believed it was going to get better.’

In Egypt, the Deng family met Manute Bol — the former NBA center, 7-foot-7, a Dinka from Sudan who had played for the Bullets, Warriors, and 76ers. After his NBA career, Bol ran a basketball school in Cairo for Sudanese refugees. He taught Luol’s older brother Ajou how to play, and Ajou taught Luol. The game that would carry Deng out of refugee life was delivered by a 7-foot-7 legend in an Egyptian apartment. ‘Manute stood for the right things. He was always going back home and giving back. That’s who he was and basketball didn’t change him.’

The family was granted political asylum in the UK. They settled in Brixton, South London. Luol was about ten. He didn’t speak a word of English. Arabic was his first language. But he was good at football — every kid on the playground wanted him on their team. ‘I was able to learn English quickly because of sports.’ Coach Jimmy Rogers at the Brixton Topcats convinced him to play basketball instead of football.

At fourteen, Deng moved to Blair Academy in New Jersey alone. Another culture shock. By senior year: #2 recruit in America. The only player ranked above him was LeBron James.
bio.duke:
One season at Duke, 2003–04, but it was enough. He averaged 15.1 points and 6.9 rebounds as a freshman — the team’s leading scorer on a roster with Chris Duhon, Shelden Williams, Daniel Ewing, and J.J. Redick. Duke went 31–6 and reached the Final Four before falling to UConn.

Deng played with the maturity and composure of someone who had been through things that made college basketball seem manageable. A refugee from a civil war does not panic in a half-court set. He declared for the draft after one season.
bio.after:
Phoenix selected Deng 7th overall and immediately traded him to Chicago. He spent ten seasons with the Bulls (2004–14). Never the face of the franchise — that was Derrick Rose — but Dime magazine called him ‘the most underrated player in the NBA.’ Coach Thibodeau: ‘The glue that holds the Bulls together.’ GM John Paxson: ‘He really does epitomize everything I had hoped for as a person and a basketball player.’

All-Rookie First Team (2005). Two-time All-Star (2012, 2013). All-Defensive Second Team (2012). Career-high 40 points vs Portland. Six-year, $71M extension. Averaged 43 minutes per game in the 2011 playoffs, praised for defending LeBron in the Eastern Conference Finals.

Cleveland (traded 2014). Miami (two seasons). LA Lakers (4-year, $72M — a contract that became infamous when they benched him during their rebuild). Minnesota (final stop, 2018–19). Fifteen seasons. 900+ games. ~$150M in career earnings. NBA Sportsmanship Award. J. Walter Kennedy Citizenship Award. UN Refugee Agency Humanitarian of the Year (2008). ‘I love basketball, but if I don’t share my success, what’s the point.’
bio.now:
In 2005, one year into his NBA career, Deng founded the Luol Deng Foundation. In 2010, he returned to South Sudan for the first time since fleeing as a toddler. He didn’t recognize anything he saw. He built basketball courts, donated computers to refugee camps, opened the Manute Bol Court in Juba — dedicated to the man who taught him the game in Cairo.

In 2012, he captained Great Britain’s basketball team at the London Olympics. Became a British citizen in 2006. Appointed OBE in 2021. ‘A refugee is a survivor. A refugee is someone that has no choice but to make a difficult decision.’

In 2019, Deng became President of the South Sudan Basketball Federation. Using his own money, he built the national team from nothing into a global force. They qualified for the 2023 FIBA World Cup (their first ever) and the 2024 Paris Olympics — the first time South Sudan, a nation that has existed only since 2011, competed in Olympic basketball. Deng served as assistant coach. The team flew 18-hour flights to Rwanda for training camp. They squeezed big men into exit rows. In Paris, South Sudan defeated Puerto Rico in their Olympic debut.

He is 40. Born in Wau during a civil war. His father packed nine children into a Cairo apartment. Manute Bol taught his brother basketball there, and his brother taught him. A ten-year-old who didn’t speak English learned the language playing football in Brixton. Ranked #2 behind only LeBron. One season at Duke, Final Four. Drafted 7th, fifteen NBA years, two-time All-Star, OBE. Then he went home. He opened the Manute Bol Court. He became president of the basketball federation of a country that didn’t exist when he was born. He funded the national team from his own pocket. He took South Sudan to the Olympics. Because Manute Bol gave him hope in an Egyptian apartment, and Luol Deng decided the only way to repay that was to give it to everyone else.

charity.name: Luol Deng Foundation
charity.label: Support the Luol Deng Foundation
charity.description: The Luol Deng Foundation builds basketball courts, donates computers to refugee camps, and funds South Sudan’s national basketball program. Luol has used his own money to take South Sudan to the Olympics.

highlights.note: NBA All-Rookie First Team
2x NBA All-Star (2012, 2013)
Averaged 17.0 PPG for 49-win Bulls team
South Sudan / British national; FIBA Basketball World Cup with Great Britain

nba.postCareer: President, South Sudan Basketball Federation; OBE; led South Sudan to 2024 Olympics
