players.name: Corey Maggette
players.tagline: One of Duke’s first one-and-dones.
players.now: Fox Sports analyst (Clippers/college basketball); Corey Cares Foundation; 2018 BIG3 MVP
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 10.6 PPG • 1999 NCAA Championship Game • 14 NBA seasons • ~$100M career earnings

bio.road:
Corey Antoine Maggette was born November 12, 1979, in Melrose Park, Illinois — a working-class suburb on Chicago’s western edge. His father was Jimmie Maggette. His mother Marguerite was one of five sisters in a family revolving around their father, Reverend Willie Dugan, a Baptist minister. The sisters lived within a short drive of each other — an extended clan bound by church, meals, and proximity.

There was a Christmas when money was tight. Corey’s brother Jimmie declined his own gifts so Corey could have what he needed. A small act of sacrifice in a modest home that crystallized the family’s values: you share what you have, you look out for the people beside you.

Corey found basketball at the Boys & Girls Club at twelve. The dream started there — not on a manicured AAU circuit, but in a community center where kids shot on bent rims. That origin never left him. When he made the NBA, he started Flight 50 Basketball Camp, initially inviting fifty kids.

At Fenwick High School in Oak Park: 23 PPG/10 RPG as a senior. State quarterfinals. Two-time All-State. McDonald’s All-American. Parade All-American. Also competed in the long jump.

The recruiting decision came down to Georgetown and Duke. John Thompson sold him like a father figure. But Coach K had a shared geography. ‘He’s from the South Side of Chicago, so right there you knew it was some type of connection being a fellow Chicagoan.’ The Chicago kid chose the Chicago coach.
bio.duke:
Maggette arrived in 1998 with Elton Brand (#1 recruit in America) and William Avery. Shane Battier was a sophomore. Trajan Langdon a senior. Duke went 37–2 — the best winning percentage in program history — and went undefeated in ACC play.

Maggette was the sixth man, the explosive freshman off the bench. 10.6 PPG, 3.9 RPG. ACC All-Rookie Team. His teammates nicknamed him “Maximus” for his jumping ability. His defining moment: a thunderous follow-up dunk against Temple in the East Regional Final. Julius Erving later compared his explosiveness to a young Vince Carter.

The championship game against UConn: Duke was favored by 9.5 — the biggest title-game spread in twenty years. TV networks showed Duke fans in Durham already celebrating. UConn saw it in their hotel. They won 77–74. Maggette scored 8 in 11 minutes. Langdon traveled in the final seconds. The best Duke team that never won a championship.

Then Maggette, Brand, and Avery made history: the first players to leave Duke early under Coach K. Before 1999, no Blue Devil had ever departed before his eligibility expired. ‘No one in Duke’s history had ever left, especially as a freshman. I felt it was a great opportunity to pursue this dream I started at the Boys & Girls Club.’
bio.after:
A year after leaving, the Myron Piggie shadow fell. Maggette admitted accepting ~$2,000 from his AAU coach while in high school — an NCAA violation. Piggie was convicted. Duke was cleared in 2004 (unaware of payments). But the episode haunted both Maggette and the program.

Drafted 13th by Seattle, traded to Orlando, then to the LA Clippers (June 2000). The Clippers became home for eight years. “Maximus” in Staples Center: a 6-6, 225-pound freight train who lived at the free-throw line, a perennial 15+ PPG scorer. 2001 Slam Dunk Contest. Career-high 39 vs the Lakers. In 2006, the Clippers won their first playoff series since moving from Buffalo.

Golden State: 5-year, $50M. Chris Mullin praised his work ethic as a model for young players. Averaged 19.3 PPG over two seasons as sixth man. Then Milwaukee, Charlotte, Detroit (2013). Fourteen NBA seasons. ~$100M in career earnings.

His family moved to Orlando when he was drafted — Marguerite leaving her sisters behind so her son wouldn’t be alone. Charles Barkley once complimented her parenting. Julius Erving became a mentor. Michael Finley and Juwan Howard, fellow Chicago guys, advised him on saving money.
bio.now:
In 2006, Maggette founded the Corey Cares Foundation — serving, mentoring, and inspiring underprivileged youth through basketball. Partners with Boys & Girls Clubs. Runs the Flight 50 camp (grown from 50 kids to hundreds). Raises scholarship money. Born from the same impulse as that Christmas when his brother gave away his gifts.

In 2017, he joined Ice Cube’s BIG3 league. Opening game: tore his Achilles. Most 37-year-olds would have retired. Maggette came back the next season, was named 2018 BIG3 MVP and Captain of the Year, scored 27 in the championship, and led Power to the title at age 38.

Now a Fox Sports analyst for the LA Clippers and college basketball. Married Milagro Martinez in 2006. Son Sergio is an emerging high school basketball talent in Orange County. Lives in Southern California.

He is 45. Born in Melrose Park. His brother gave up Christmas presents so Corey could have his. Found basketball at the Boys & Girls Club at twelve. Chose Duke because Coach K was from the South Side. Part of the best team that never won — 37-2, 9.5-point favorites, beaten by UConn. One of the first three to ever leave Duke early. The Piggie scandal cast a shadow. Fourteen NBA seasons, $100 million, called Maximus, Charles Barkley complimented his mother’s parenting. Tore his Achilles at 37, won BIG3 MVP at 38. The camp he started with fifty kids is still running. The foundation is called Corey Cares, because that’s the whole point — he does.

charity.name: Corey Cares Foundation
charity.label: Support the Corey Cares Foundation
charity.description: The Corey Cares Foundation works with Boys & Girls Clubs to provide youth basketball programs, mentorship, and community support. The Flight 50 camp has grown from 50 kids to hundreds.

highlights.note: Averaged 22.2 PPG with Clippers — career best
First Duke player to leave for NBA after one season

nba.postCareer: Fox Sports analyst (Clippers/college basketball); Corey Cares Foundation; 2018 BIG3 MVP

Corey Maggette First Duke One-and-Done - Duke Basketball Report

players.name: William Avery
players.tagline: Augusta, Georgia. High school teammates with Ricky Moore — who beat him in the 1999 title game wearing a UConn jersey. One of the first to leave early under Coach K. 14th pick. Three NBA seasons. Eight countries. Then came back to Duke at age 40, graduated in 2023, and joined Scheyer’s coaching staff.
players.now: Assistant Coach, Duke University (hired 2023 under Jon Scheyer); graduated Duke 2023 in African American Studies
players.stat: 2 Duke seasons • 74 games • 14.9/5.0 soph • 49.5% FG • 2nd Team All-ACC • 37-2 • Championship game

sources.label: GoDuke.com: Will Avery coaching bio
Basketball Reference: William Avery career stats
EuroLeague: William Avery profile
Wikipedia: William Avery (basketball)

coaching.note: First full-time college coaching position; hired by Jon Scheyer

nba.postCareer: Assistant Coach at Duke University; founded Will Avery Basketball Academy in Evans, GA (helped 20+ players earn college scholarships); graduated Duke 2023 (African American Studies)

bio.road:
William Franklin Avery Jr. was born August 8, 1979, in Augusta, Georgia. He grew up in a modest household — his mother lived just above the poverty line and worked at a power plant. Basketball was the way out.

At Westside High School, Avery played alongside Ricky Moore. Together they led Westside to the 1995 Georgia state championship — the school’s first title. Moore would go on to play at UConn. The two childhood teammates from Augusta would meet again on the biggest stage in college basketball, wearing different jerseys.

To bolster his academics, Avery transferred to Oak Hill Academy in Virginia for his senior year. Coach K had initial concerns about his grades at Westside, but Oak Hill smoothed the path. He graduated as a third-team Parade All-American and one of the top point guards in the Class of 1997.
bio.duke:
Avery played two seasons at Duke (1997–99). Freshman year: backed up Steve Wojciechowski, averaged 8.7/2.5 in 35 games, learning behind one of Duke’s toughest defenders. The team went 32–4 and reached the Elite Eight.

Sophomore year was the masterpiece. Starting PG alongside Elton Brand, Trajan Langdon, Shane Battier, Chris Carrawell, and Corey Maggette. Averaged 14.9/5.0, shot 49.5% from the field and 41.1% from three. Second Team All-ACC. Duke went 37–2 — 32 straight wins, 16–0 in ACC play, sweeping both conference titles.

In the championship game against UConn, his old Westside teammate Ricky Moore was guarding Duke’s perimeter. Two boys from Augusta, one title game, different jerseys. UConn won 77–74.

Then the decision: Avery, Brand, and Maggette left for the NBA — among the first to leave early under Coach K. Krzyzewski had wanted Avery back to help groom incoming freshman Jason Williams. The departure created a rift that took two decades to heal.
bio.after:
Minnesota took Avery 14th overall. Three seasons: 142 games, 2.7 PPG, 1.4 APG. He never established himself as a rotation player. After his contract expired in 2002, no NBA team signed him.

What followed was a decade-long odyssey: France (Strasbourg), Israel (Hapoel Tel Aviv, Hapoel Jerusalem), Ukraine (Azovmash Mariupol), Greece (Makedonikos, Panionios Athens), Poland (Energa Czarni Slupsk). Eight countries, eight leagues, eight languages. A first-round pick playing in gyms where fans threw things and paychecks were uncertain. He kept playing because basketball was all he knew.

He retired in 2012, returned to Evans, Georgia, and founded the Will Avery Basketball Academy, inspired by his daughter Yasmine’s interest in basketball. The academy helped over twenty young players earn college scholarships.
bio.now:
In the fall of 2019 — twenty years after leaving — William Avery did something extraordinary: he came back to Duke. At age forty, he re-enrolled to complete his degree in African American Studies. While earning his degree, he assisted the basketball team, reconnecting with the program he’d left so abruptly two decades earlier. He graduated in 2023.

Following graduation, Avery was hired as an assistant coach on Jon Scheyer’s staff — his first full-time college coaching position. The kid from Augusta who had been one of the first to leave early is now on the bench in Cameron Indoor Stadium, teaching the next generation. He works alongside Chris Carrawell, his teammate from the 1999 championship game team, completing a Brotherhood circle that stretches across a quarter century.

William Avery was born in Augusta and raised by a mother who worked at a power plant. He won a state championship alongside Ricky Moore, who later guarded him in the 1999 national title game wearing a UConn jersey. He went to Oak Hill, then Duke. He was the starting PG on a team that went 37–2. He left early and the departure created a wound. He was drafted 14th and played three NBA seasons. He played in eight countries across a decade. He retired, went home to Augusta, and started a basketball academy for his daughter. And then, at age forty, he came back to Duke and finished what he had started. He graduated in 2023. He is now an assistant coach under Scheyer, on the same bench where Coach K sat, in the same arena where he once played point guard on one of the greatest teams in Duke history. The Brotherhood doesn’t just welcome you back. Sometimes it waits twenty years for you to be ready. William Avery was ready. And Duke was still there.

players.name: Dahntay Jones
players.tagline: The Rutgers transfer who became Duke’s best player. Defensive stopper. 624 NBA games, nine teams, fourteen seasons. Won a championship in Cleveland. LeBron paid his fines. Married in the Duke Chapel. Now coaching the Clippers.
players.now: Assistant Coach, Los Angeles Clippers (promoted to full assistant 2024-25 under Tyronn Lue)
players.drafted: 1st Rd, 20th — Boston Celtics (traded to Memphis Grizzlies)
players.stat: 2 Duke seasons • 17.7/5.5 senior • All-ACC • 2x ACC All-Defensive • 20th pick • 624 NBA games • 2016 Champion

sources.label: GoDuke.com: Dahntay Jones player bio
Duke Basketball Report: Jim’s Look at Duke Transfers Part II
Basketball Reference: Dahntay Jones career stats
TrentonDaily: In the Paint with Dahntay Jones
NBA Coaches Association: Dahntay Jones bio
Wikipedia: Dahntay Jones

highlights.note: Career-best 10.2 PPG with Indiana Pacers
NBA Champion with Cleveland Cavaliers; key minutes in Game 6 of Finals
LeBron James paid his ejection fine (,000) after trash-talk technicals vs Toronto
G-League career-high 45 points with Grand Rapids Drive at age 35

coaching.note: Promoted under Tyronn Lue; Summer League head coach 2024

nba.postCareer: Assistant Coach, Los Angeles Clippers; father of five; previously married Valeisha Butterfield (Congressman G.K. Butterfield’s daughter) in Duke Chapel (2011)

bio.road:
Dahntay Lavall Jones was born December 27, 1980, in Trenton, New Jersey, and raised in nearby Hamilton Square. Basketball ran in the family: his father Larry played at St. Peter’s College, and his cousin Al Harrington would play sixteen NBA seasons.

At Steinert High School in Hamilton Township, Jones became one of the most decorated players in Mercer County history. He scored a school-record 1,675 career points, averaged 24 points and 9 rebounds as a senior, earned first-team All-State honors, honorable mention McDonald’s All-America, and Prep High School Academic All-American status. He captained his team as both a junior and senior.

Duke recruited Jones out of high school, but Corey Maggette’s commitment closed that door. He enrolled at Rutgers. By his sophomore year, he was the Scarlet Knights’ best player — 16 PPG, 4.6 RPG, Big East All-Rookie honors as a freshman. But Rutgers was an NIT program and Jones said publicly that coach Kevin Bannon wasn’t helping him get better.

When Maggette left Duke after one season — the first Blue Devil to go to the NBA after a single year — the spot reopened. Jason Williams, Jones’s best friend from New Jersey, was already at Duke and helped seal the deal. Jones became Coach K’s second transfer ever, following Roshown McLeod five years earlier. He sat out the 2001 championship season but contributed in practice daily against Battier, Dunleavy, and Boozer.
bio.duke:
Jones played two Duke seasons (2001–03) and the arc tells a story of transformation.

Junior year, 2001–02: stepped into the starting lineup alongside Williams, Dunleavy, and Boozer on a #1-ranked team. Averaged 11.2/4.2/1.1. Honorable mention All-ACC. Named to the ACC All-Defensive Team and the national All-Defensive Team. Duke’s primary defensive stopper, tasked with shutting down the opponent’s most dangerous scorer every night. Duke won the ACC Tournament.

Then the exodus: Williams drafted 2nd overall. Boozer to Cleveland. Dunleavy to Golden State. Three of Duke’s four best players gone in one offseason.

Senior year, 2002–03: Jones emerged as Duke’s best player. He improved his three-point shooting from 23% to 40% in a single offseason. Averaged 17.7/5.5/1.2 steals across 33 games. All-ACC. ACC All-Defensive Team for the second straight year. Against UNC in February 2003: 23 points, 13 rebounds in an 83–74 Duke victory. He had become a complete two-way player — guard the other team’s best scorer, then score 17 of his own.
bio.after:
Boston selected Jones 20th overall in 2003. His rights were traded to Memphis for Kendrick Perkins and Marcus Banks. Four seasons in Memphis as a defensive role player (5.0 PPG).

What followed was one of the great NBA odysseys of his generation. Sacramento Kings. G-League with the Fort Wayne Mad Ants. Denver Nuggets (2009 Western Conference Finals). A four-year deal with Indiana (peaked at 10.2 PPG in 2009–10). Dallas Mavericks. Atlanta Hawks. Los Angeles Clippers on 10-day contracts. Back to the G-League with Grand Rapids Drive, where at age 35 he scored 45 points in a single game — because he refused to stop playing.

On April 13, 2016, Cleveland signed Jones. He scored 13 in his debut. Fifteen games later, he was in the NBA Finals. He played key minutes in Game 6 as Cleveland completed the historic 3–1 comeback to win its first championship. In the 2017 playoffs, he was ejected for trash-talking Toronto’s Norman Powell — fine: ,000 against his ,127 postseason salary. LeBron covered the fine. His final game: 2 minutes in the 2017 Finals. Nine teams. Fourteen seasons. 624 games. One ring.
bio.now:
On July 23, 2011, Jones married Valeisha Butterfield — daughter of Congressman G.K. Butterfield — in the Duke University Chapel. Two hundred guests. People Magazine coverage. They later divorced.

In November 2020, Jones joined the LA Clippers as player development and video coach. By 2024–25, he was promoted to full assistant under Tyronn Lue, with expanded responsibilities in player development, film, and strategy. He coached the Clippers’ 2024 Summer League team. His coaching emphasizes the defensive fundamentals and toughness that defined his playing career. He is the father of five children; his son Dahntay Jr. plays youth basketball as a combo guard.

Dahntay Jones was born in Trenton. His father played at St. Peter’s, his cousin Al Harrington played sixteen NBA seasons. He scored 1,675 points at Steinert and was an Academic All-American. He went to Rutgers and became the best player on the team, then transferred to Duke because Jason Williams — his best friend — told him to come. He was Coach K’s second transfer, following McLeod through the same door. He sat out a championship. He became Duke’s defensive stopper, then Duke’s best player when Williams and Boozer and Dunleavy left. He improved his three-point shooting from 23% to 40% in one summer. He was drafted 20th overall. He played for nine teams across fourteen seasons. He went to the G-League at 35 and scored 45 in a single game. He won a championship in Cleveland and LeBron paid his fines. He got married in the Duke Chapel. He is now an assistant coach for the LA Clippers, teaching defensive fundamentals to a new generation. The kid from Trenton who couldn’t get to Duke the first time found his way there the second time, and the second time changed everything.

players.name: JJ Redick
players.tagline: The most hated player in America. Now he coaches the Lakers.
players.now: Head Coach, Los Angeles Lakers
players.stat: 2,769 pts — Duke all-time scorer • 457 career 3s • Naismith POY

bio.road:
JJ Redick was born in Cookeville, Tennessee, and raised in Roanoke, Virginia, the second of five children in a family wired for basketball. His father, Ken, had played two seasons at Ohio Wesleyan University. His mother, Jeanie, was a nurse. His twin sisters, Catie and Alyssa, both played at Campbell University. His younger sister Abigail played at Virginia Tech and Drexel. His brother David was a football player at Marshall. In the Redick household, competition was the family language.

His nickname came from the twins. As a toddler, the family called him ‘J.’ The sisters kept repeating it — ‘J, J’ — and it stuck. His middle name, Clay, came from his father’s background as a stoneware potter. Jonathan Clay Redick became JJ to the world before he could walk.

At Cave Spring High School in Roanoke, Redick became the best player in Virginia. He averaged 28.3 points, 5 rebounds, and 4 assists as a senior. In the 2002 VHSL Class AAA state championship game, he scored 43 points — a championship-game record across all classifications that stood for 16 years, until Mac McClung broke it in 2018. He finished as Virginia’s all-time AAA leading scorer with 2,215 career points and shot over 44 percent from three. He played AAU ball for the Boo Williams All-Stars, whose national championship teams faced future NBA stars like Dwyane Wade.

He was the 2002 McDonald’s All-American Game MVP, scoring 26 points on 10-of-15 shooting and winning the three-point competition. Ranked the No. 2 shooting guard and No. 13 overall prospect in the country. Virginia Mr. Basketball. Parade All-American. Every major program wanted him. He chose Duke.
bio.duke:
JJ Redick left Duke as the most prolific scorer in school history and one of the most decorated — and most despised — players in the history of college basketball. He scored 2,769 career points, a Duke record. He made 457 career three-pointers, an NCAA record at the time of his graduation. He set the ACC single-season free throw percentage record at .953 during his sophomore year, including a streak of 54 consecutive free throws. He led Duke to a 116-23 record, three ACC Tournament championships, and a Final Four. He was a two-time National Player of the Year, two-time ACC Player of the Year, two-time ACC Tournament MVP, three-time All-American, and the 2006 Sullivan Award winner as the nation’s top amateur athlete. His jersey No. 4 was retired in February 2007.

Freshman (2002–03): The Arrival. Redick led the team with 30 points in the ACC Tournament championship game against NC State. He averaged 15.0 points per game — the fourth-highest freshman scoring average in Duke history behind only Johnny Dawkins, Gene Banks, and Mike Gminski. He set the ACC and Duke freshman record with a .919 free throw percentage. He made 95 three-pointers.

Sophomore (2003–04): The Final Four — and the Breaking Point. Duke reached the Final Four, where they fell to Connecticut. Redick was named to the All-Final Four team. But the season was defined by something darker: JJ Redick had become the most hated player in college basketball. Students from rival schools discovered his cell phone number; he estimated receiving 50 to 75 hate calls per day. Opposing crowds screamed vulgar things about his 12-year-old sister. Custom t-shirts with his photo that read ‘When I grow up, I want to name my kid JJ Redick’ on the front and ‘and beat him every day’ on the back. An NFL team owner talking trash to him courtside. An entire arena chanting an obscenity. He nearly quit. In December of his sophomore year, he had his twin sisters meet him on campus for dinner. ‘I don’t want to play anymore,’ he told them. ‘This is not for me. It’s not fun.’ He turned to writing poetry as an outlet. CBS Sports would later name him the ‘most hated current athlete in America.’

The Transformation: Summer Before Junior Year. Coach K sat him down in late May and told him the truth: ‘You’ve been good. You were All-ACC and a third-team All-American. We made the Final Four. So you’ve been good, but what you’re doing isn’t good enough.’ Krzyzewski kept him from playing USA Basketball that summer. Redick walked around campus carrying a jug of water. He lost 23 pounds, dropping from over 215 to 192. He won every preseason conditioning test.

Junior (2004–05): The transformed Redick averaged 21.8 points per game and won ACC Player of the Year and consensus National Player of the Year honors. Duke earned a No. 1 seed but lost in the Sweet Sixteen. He was playing over 37 minutes per game now, leaner, faster, more dynamic, able to create his own shot rather than just catch and shoot.

Senior (2005–06): The Record-Breaker. Redick averaged 26.8 points per game — a Duke single-season record. He broke Curtis Staples’ NCAA career three-point record against Wake Forest. He passed Dickie Hemric’s 51-year-old ACC career scoring record with a pair of free throws against Temple. He won every major national player of the year award: Naismith, Wooden, AP, NABC. He was a two-time team captain, a history major with a minor in cultural anthropology, and still the most hated player in America.

The final act was bittersweet. In the 2006 NCAA Tournament, LSU’s Garrett Temple — a 6’5 defensive specialist with a long wingspan — chased Redick relentlessly. The nation’s No. 2 scorer shot 3-for-18 and scored just 11 points. Duke lost.
bio.after:
The Orlando Magic selected Redick with the 11th pick in the 2006 draft. His early years were a test of patience. He competed for minutes, battled back spasms, and spent stretches as a third-string player. In January 2008, he posted on his blog that it had ‘been proven that even if I play well in the limited minutes I get, not much is going to change.’ He asked his agent about a trade. But he kept working. By the 2008-09 season, he was helping the Magic reach the NBA Finals. It was the first of 13 consecutive playoff appearances for Redick.

A three-team sign-and-trade sent Redick to the Clippers on a four-year, $27 million deal. He became a full-time starter for the first time in his career at age 29 — starting 218 of his first 219 games. He scored a career-high 40 points against Houston in January 2016 on 9-of-12 from three. He led the NBA in three-point shooting at 47.5% during the 2015-16 season. He made 200 or more three-pointers in four of five seasons from 2014 to 2019.

Redick signed a one-year, $23 million deal with Philadelphia in 2017 and posted his best overall statistical season in 2018-19: 18.1 points per game on 44% shooting and 39.7% from three. He moved to New Orleans in 2019, was traded to Dallas in 2021, and retired on September 21 of that year. His farewell was quintessentially Redick — announced on his own podcast: ‘I started playing basketball 30 years ago in my backyard on an uneven patch of dirt, gravel, and grass.’ Fifteen seasons. 940 games. 12,028 points. 1,950 career three-pointers — 15th most in NBA history.

In January 2016, while still an active player with the Clippers, Redick launched a podcast on Yahoo! Sports — becoming the first active NBA player to host one. The podcast evolved through multiple platforms. In August 2020, Redick and producer Tommy Alter co-founded ThreeFourTwo Productions — the name a reference to the 342 shots Redick took every Sunday during the offseason as part of his shooting regimen. Their flagship, ‘The Old Man and the Three,’ launched during the NBA’s COVID bubble with Damian Lillard as the first guest. It became one of the most popular sports podcasts in the world, amassing over 320 million YouTube views. The title was a play on Hemingway — Redick saw himself as the aging craftsman, still learning. After retirement, he joined ESPN as an analyst and quickly rose to the lead broadcast team by February 2024. LeBron James shared a clip and captioned it ‘JJ FOR PRESIDENT!!’ In March 2024, the two launched a joint podcast, ‘Mind the Game,’ focused on basketball strategy and analysis.
bio.now:
On June 26, 2024, the Los Angeles Lakers hired JJ Redick as their 29th head coach, signing him to a four-year contract. He had never coached at any level. The hire came after the Lakers’ pursuit of Dan Hurley was rebuffed (Hurley turned down a six-year, $70 million offer to stay at UConn). GM Rob Pelinka was sold on Redick’s basketball IQ, his ability to connect with players, and his relationship with LeBron James.

Redick’s first season exceeded expectations. He coached the Lakers to a 50-32 record, the third-best in the Western Conference, despite navigating a mid-season trade for Luka Doncic and the challenge of integrating ball-dominant superstars. The Lakers made the playoffs but were eliminated by the Minnesota Timberwolves in five games in the first round.

The season was also marked by personal devastation. On January 7, 2025, the Palisades Fire swept through Los Angeles. Redick’s rental home in Pacific Palisades burned to the ground. Chelsea evacuated their sons, Knox and Kai, while the team was on the road in Dallas. Everything they owned from nearly 20 years together was gone: his son’s charcoal painting of a lighthouse, the boys’ basketball card collections saved up from birthday money and chores, Chelsea’s engagement ring. Austin Reaves embraced him at the next practice.

In September 2025, the Lakers extended Redick’s contract through the 2029-30 season. He spent the offseason meeting with Rams coach Sean McVay to pick his brain on offensive concepts. He has set a remarkable NBA record: the Lakers are 57-4 when leading after three quarters under his watch, the best mark in league history.

He lives in Los Angeles with his wife, Chelsea Kilgore Redick, whom he married in 2010. They have two sons, Knox and Kai. His podcast empire continues without him — ‘The Old Man and the Three’ was rebranded as ‘The Young Man and the Three,’ with Cam Johnson of the Nuggets taking the host chair. Redick has sworn off social media to focus on coaching. ‘I am excommunicated from the content space,’ he said with a grin.

He has four tattoos of Bible verses on his body: Isaiah 40:31, Joshua 1:9, Psalm 40:1–3, and Philippians 4:13, plus a sleeve of the Virgin Mary. He graduated from Duke with a degree in history and a minor in cultural anthropology. He still holds Duke’s all-time scoring record. His net worth is estimated at approximately $50 million.

The transformation is the story. A kid from Roanoke who shot on an uneven patch of dirt, gravel, and grass. The most hated player in America who nearly quit because strangers were making signs about beating his future children. A poet who couldn’t find another way to process the pain. A role player who reinvented himself three times — as a starter, as a media mogul, and now as the coach of the Lakers.

highlights.note: Started for Orlando Magic NBA Finals team
Career-high 16.3 PPG with Clippers
Shot .440 from three — career 41.5% 3PT shooter

nba.postCareer: Podcaster (The Old Man and the Three); ESPN/NBA TV analyst; Head Coach, Los Angeles Lakers (2024-present)

players.name: Shelden Williams
players.tagline: The Landlord.
players.now: Coach/mentor; Shelden Williams Foundation; G League asst. coach
players.stat: 139 games • 13.9 ppg • 9.1 rpg • 3.0 bpg • 2x NABC DPOY • Duke career blocks/rebounds leader

bio.road:
Shelden DeMar Williams was born October 21, 1983, in Oklahoma City, Oklahoma, the son of educators Bob and Jeannette Williams. His father played basketball at Oklahoma Christian University and coached high school and junior high basketball. His brother Quincy played at North Texas. The value of learning was instilled early — Shelden was a member of the National Honor Society and the Fellowship of Christian Athletes.

At Midwest City High School, Williams became one of the most dominant big men in the country. As a senior, he averaged 20 points, 12 rebounds, three blocked shots, and three assists. He was named Gatorade Oklahoma Player of the Year in both 2001 and 2002, a Parade All-American, and rated the nation’s fifth-best prep player. His nickname — ‘The Landlord’ — stuck from high school, a nod to his ability to own the paint. He chose Duke, arriving in the fall of 2002 alongside J.J. Redick and Sean Dockery.
bio.duke:
Williams was the most dominant defensive player in Duke history. Over four seasons, he averaged 13.9 points, 9.1 rebounds, and 3.0 blocked shots while shooting 57.2% from the floor. He became the first player in NCAA history to register 1,750 points, 1,250 rebounds, 400 blocked shots, and 150 steals. He holds Duke’s career records in rebounds (1,217) and blocked shots (422), as well as the single-season blocks record (137 as a senior). He is the third player in ACC history to reach 1,500 points, 1,000 rebounds, 350 blocks, and 150 steals — joining Tim Duncan and Ralph Sampson.

He won the NABC Defensive Player of the Year in both 2005 and 2006, only the fifth player in history to win it consecutively. Three-time All-ACC, three-time All-ACC Tournament, two-time ACC Defensive Player of the Year, consensus All-American as a senior. His signature game: January 11, 2006, against Maryland — 19 points, 11 rebounds, and 10 blocked shots, only the third triple-double in Duke basketball history.

He was a tri-captain as a senior, helping the Blue Devils to a 116–23 four-year record, two ACC regular season titles, three ACC Tournament championships, four Sweet Sixteens, and the 2004 Final Four. Duke retired his No. 23 on January 28, 2007. Duke Athletics Hall of Fame, 2018. He graduated with a degree in sociology and a certificate in Markets and Management.
bio.after:
The Atlanta Hawks selected Williams 5th overall in the 2006 Draft. He showed flashes — 21 points and 10 rebounds against Indiana as a rookie, Eastern Conference Rookie of the Month for April 2007 — but his college dominance never fully translated. In February 2008, he was traded to the Sacramento Kings in the Mike Bibby deal. He bounced through seven NBA teams: Atlanta, Sacramento, Minnesota, Boston (part of the 2008 championship roster), Denver, New York Knicks, and New Jersey Nets.

NBA career totals: 4.5 points, 4.3 rebounds across 228 games. Approximately $12.3 million earned. After his last NBA season in 2011–12, he played overseas: Élan Chalon in France and Tianjin Ronggang in China for two seasons. He retired from playing around 2015.

In 2008, Williams married WNBA star Candace Parker. Their daughter, Lailaa Nicole Williams, was born in 2009. The couple divorced in 2016.
bio.now:
In 2019, Williams founded the Shelden Williams Foundation, dedicated to empowering student-athletes and their families through sports, education, and community involvement. The foundation operates in Oklahoma, Atlanta, and Durham — the three communities that shaped his life.

Williams has transitioned into coaching, serving as an assistant coach for the College Park Skyhawks in the NBA G League — the Atlanta Hawks’ affiliate, bringing his career full circle to the franchise that drafted him. He mentors young players on defense, positioning, and the mental discipline that made him The Landlord at Cameron Indoor.

At 42, his No. 23 hangs in the Cameron rafters. The records he set in blocks and rebounds may stand for decades. The son of educators who became one himself — the kid from Oklahoma City who owned every paint he stepped into is still teaching others how to claim theirs.

nba.postCareer: Coach/mentor; Shelden Williams Foundation; G League asst. coach

players.name: Gerald Henderson
players.tagline: The spark that reignited the engine.
players.now: Retired; Charlotte Hornets broadcast analyst
players.stat: 103 games • First-Team All-ACC 2009 • McDonald’s AA dunk champion • 535 NBA games • $37.4M career earnings

bio.road:
Gerald Henderson Jr. was born into basketball royalty and raised in the shadow of one of the most famous steals in NBA history. His father, Gerald Henderson Sr., was a 13-year NBA veteran who won three championship rings — two with the Boston Celtics (1981, 1984) and one with the Detroit Pistons (1990). He played alongside Larry Bird, Robert Parish, Kevin McHale, Patrick Ewing, Charles Barkley, Isiah Thomas, and Joe Dumars. His signature moment: the 1984 NBA Finals against the Lakers, when he stole a James Worthy pass and scored a game-tying layup that forced overtime. The Celtics won the game and the series.

Gerald Jr. was born December 9, 1987, in Caldwell, New Jersey, while his father was playing for the 76ers. Youngest of three children — older brother Jermaine (basketball at Saint Rose) and sister Jade (graduated from Villanova). The father played his son one-on-one in the backyard and never let him win. ‘I beat him that last time when he was in ninth, tenth grade,’ Henderson Sr. said. ‘I played him until I got to the point where I said, I can’t take this punishment no more. He was getting bigger and stronger. That athleticism — that’s something you can’t teach.’

‘I’ve seen that steal hundreds of times,’ Gerald Jr. said of his father’s defining moment. When the 2009 NCAA East Regional was held at Madison Square Garden, the Boston Globe ran a feature: ‘Son is in Forecast.’ Gerald Jr. was asked what he knew about his dad’s era. ‘All he talks about is our shorts and how the guys didn’t used to dunk as much.’

At Episcopal Academy in Merion, Pennsylvania: five-sport phenom. 18.4 ppg, 8.6 rpg as a junior; 25–3 record; state champion. Scratch golfer with all-conference honors. Two-time league champion in high jump and triple jump. His teammate was Wayne Ellington, a future four-year UNC starter. McDonald’s All-American. Won the 2006 McDonald’s dunk contest — the second Duke player to win it (after Ricky Price in 1994). Also won the Naismith Sportsmanship Award. Led the East with 16 points.
bio.duke:
Freshman year (2006–07): 6.8 ppg off the bench. Raw and electric — 45 dunks as a sophomore, the most by a Duke wing since Grant Hill’s 48 in 1991–92. Sophomore year: starting lineup, 12.7 ppg, leading the team in scoring seven times and rebounding eight times. In the nine biggest games — two UNC, two Clemson, Davidson, Wisconsin, two NCAA Tournament games — he averaged 14.2 ppg, 5.7 rpg, 1.6 blocks, above his season averages across the board.

The Belmont game. First round, 2008 NCAA Tournament, Washington’s Verizon Center. No. 15 Belmont led Duke by one with two minutes left. Henderson had kept Duke alive with 21 points on 8-of-14. With 11.9 seconds left, Duke facing elimination, Henderson drove coast-to-coast and tipped in the game-winning shot. DeMarcus Nelson preserved the win with a steal. Duke 71, Belmont 70. DBR described it as one of the closest calls in Duke tournament history.

Junior year (2008–09): unquestioned leader. 16.5 ppg. First-Team All-ACC — first Duke player to earn it since Redick in 2006. ACC Tournament champion. No. 2 seed. He left for the draft after three seasons.
bio.after:
Charlotte Bobcats, 12th overall, 2009. Six seasons in Charlotte — the longest stretch of his career. During the catastrophic 2011–12 season (7–59, worst record in NBA history), Henderson was one of the lone bright spots, averaging a career-high 15.1 points. Traded to Portland (2015). Signed with Philadelphia (2016). Career: 535 games, 333 starts, 11.2 ppg, $37.4 million.

In August 2017, hip surgery. Missed the entire 2017–18 season. Tried to come back in 2018 — worked out for teams, then tore his Achilles in a pickup game. The comeback was over before it started. January 2019: joined the Charlotte Hornets broadcast team. April 30, 2019: officially retired.
bio.now:
Gerald Henderson Jr. is 37 years old. Hornets broadcast analyst in Charlotte, close to the franchise that drafted him. His father and mother run a real estate business in Blue Bell, Pennsylvania.

His Brotherhood story is the quietest of the Between Era — and the most shaped by inheritance. He grew up watching his father’s championship steal on replay. Played one-on-one against a three-time NBA champion and lost every time until high school. Won the McDonald’s dunk contest — the second Henderson to define a moment on national television. Tipped in a shot with 11.9 seconds left to save Duke from a 15-seed upset. Was the best player on the worst team in NBA history. Tried to come back from hip surgery and tore his Achilles in a pickup game.

His father stole the ball from James Worthy and changed a Finals. His son tipped in a shot against Belmont and changed a first-round game. The scale is different. The gene is the same. Gerald Jr. never won a championship. But he carried the name for eight NBA seasons, and when the body said no, he picked up a microphone. The Henderson basketball story spans two generations, three rings (all his father’s), one legendary steal, one legendary tip-in, and a family that understood: you play until it doesn’t let you anymore.

highlights.note: Career-high 16.2 PPG
14.9 PPG for Charlotte

nba.postCareer: Retired; Charlotte Hornets broadcast analyst

players.name: Josh McRoberts
players.tagline: Flair, flash, and an early exit.
players.now: Assistant coach, Carmel HS; Hamilton County Basketball HOF
players.stat: 69 games • 10.8 PPG • 6.5 RPG • McDonald’s AA Game MVP • ACC All-Freshman • 11 NBA seasons

bio.road:
Josh McRoberts grew up in Carmel, Indiana, in a basketball-centric family. His father Tim was a Carmel High School alumnus who had played on the basketball team. His mother Jennifer was a teacher. One of his most influential mentors was the late Eric Clark, a beloved Carmel administrator and coach for whom the school’s athletic facilities were named.

What separates McRoberts from most blue-chip recruits is the modesty of his original ambition. In a 2016 WTHR interview, he stood on the Carmel court and said: ‘I just love playing basketball. Honestly my biggest dream was probably to play here, to play at Carmel High School. That was my biggest aspiration. So everything after that was like a bonus to me.’ His picture hangs on the wall outside the gym.

At Carmel: 17.9 ppg, 11.4 rpg, 4.2 apg, 3.0 bpg on 59.3% shooting as a senior. Career: 1,264 points, 759 rebounds, 255 assists, 259 blocks. No. 1 power forward nationally (Rivals/Scout). Morgan Wootten Award (McDonald’s Player of the Year). McDonald’s All-American Game MVP (17 pts on 7-of-8, game-high 12 rebounds). First-team Parade and USA Today All-American. Indiana Mr. Basketball finalist. Also a scratch golfer and two-time league champion in high jump and triple jump.
bio.duke:
McRoberts arrived in 2005 as the headliner of what was supposed to be the nation’s top class. Duke Basketball Report would later call it ‘a major disappointment — at least in Duke terms.’ Boateng and Boykin transferred. Pocius never contributed. Paulus finished playing football at Syracuse. Only McRoberts delivered.

A Sports Illustrated feature captured J.J. Redick driving McRoberts home in his 2001 Toyota Corolla. McRoberts’s 6-foot-10 frame jammed in, knees near his chin. ‘It seems like J.J.’s always driving me around, and I’m always paying for his gas.’ Coach K compared McRoberts’s potential to Grant Hill’s.

Freshman: 8.7 ppg, 5.3 rpg, 60.5% FG. ACC All-Freshman. Duke went 32–4, ACC regular-season and tournament champs. Before sophomore year: lumbar discectomy for chronic back problems. Named co-captain with Paulus — first two sophomore captains in Duke history. December 19 vs Kent State: 19 pts, 6 blocks, 6 reb, 4 ast, 4 stl — the first Duke player to tally 4+ in all five categories. All-ACC and ACC All-Defense.

Then VCU. First round, 2007 NCAA Tournament. McRoberts played all 40 minutes: 22 pts, 12 reb, 5 blocks, 4 ast. Missed two of four free throws in the final 1:41. Eric Maynor hit a game-winner with 1.8 seconds left. Duke lost 79–77. Draft stock fell from lottery to 37th — the sharpest decline of any Duke player in the Brotherhood.
bio.after: Eleven NBA seasons, seven teams: Portland, Indiana (traded on draft night 2008 to his hometown Pacers), Lakers (part of the Dwight Howard blockbuster), Orlando, Charlotte, Miami ($23M contract), Dallas. Pau Gasol called him ‘scrappy and a hustler.’ Best season: 2013–14 Charlotte, all 78 games started, career-high 24 pts. Torn meniscus in Miami. Stress fracture. Waived by Dallas February 2018. ~$30M career earnings across ~450 games.
bio.now: Josh McRoberts came home. During the 2011 NBA lockout, his brother Zach was a Carmel sophomore. In 2020–21, during COVID, McRoberts officially joined the Carmel varsity staff as an assistant. ‘Having been part of a team for as long as I had, I felt the absence of that kind of inclusion and purpose.’ Hamilton County Basketball Hall of Fame. He is 38, coaching the next generation of Greyhounds in the gym named after his mentor Eric Clark. Everything after Carmel was a bonus. The bonus brought him back to where he started.

highlights.note: Best year: 10.1/5.2/3.8 with Charlotte — key to playoff push

nba.postCareer: Assistant coach, Carmel HS; Hamilton County Basketball HOF

players.name: Greg Paulus
players.tagline: Born in Ohio, raised in Wisconsin, made in Syracuse. The nation’s best QB AND best PG simultaneously. Beat Ray Rice in a state football championship. Gatorade National Athlete of the Year. Chose Duke basketball. Won four ACC titles. Then went back to Syracuse and started at quarterback without playing football in four years. Head coach at Niagara. Two Halls of Fame. There will never be another.
players.now: Head Coach, Niagara University men’s basketball (since 2019); inducted into Greater Syracuse Sports HOF and NYSPHSAA HOF (2024)
players.drafted: Did not play professional basketball or football — went directly into coaching
players.stat: Duke: 4 seasons • 139 games • 1,193 pts • 468 assists • 39.8% 3PT • 112-28 • 4 ACC titles | Syracuse: 12 starts • 2,024 yds • 13 TDs

sources.label: GoDuke.com: Greg Paulus player bio
NYSPHSAA Hall of Fame: Greg Paulus (2024)
Greater Syracuse Sports Hall of Fame: Greg Paulus
GW Athletics: Greg Paulus coaching bio
Sports Reference: Greg Paulus college stats
Wikipedia: Greg Paulus

nba.postCareer: Head Coach at Niagara University; also started all 12 games at QB for Syracuse (2009) after 4 years without football; tried out with New Orleans Saints (2010); master’s degree from Syracuse Newhouse School

bio.road:
Gregory Russell Paulus was born July 3, 1986, in Medina, Ohio. He grew up in Appleton, Wisconsin, before his family moved to the Syracuse suburb of Manlius, New York. He was one of seven children — six brothers and a sister.

At Christian Brothers Academy in Syracuse, Paulus became something that shouldn’t be possible: the best high school quarterback AND the best high school point guard in New York State simultaneously, and arguably in the entire country. On the football field, he broke state records for career touchdowns (152) and career passing yards (11,760). In the 2004 state championship game, he led CBA to its first title, defeating New Rochelle and future NFL star Ray Rice 41–35 in the Carrier Dome. Sports Illustrated named him the best QB in the nation.

On the basketball court: 2,399 career points (12th in NYS history), 26.8/8.2/5.1/3.4 as a senior. McDonald’s All-American. New York Mr. Basketball. Nation’s #1 point guard by Rivals and Scout. Set a Nike Hoop Summit record with 10 assists.

He was named Gatorade National Football Player of the Year (2004) and Gatorade National Male Athlete of the Year (2005, all sports). Football offers from Miami and Notre Dame. Basketball offers from Duke, Syracuse, Georgetown, Florida, UNC, Xavier. He chose Duke. His father: “First and foremost, Greg has a passion for basketball.”
bio.duke:
Paulus played four seasons at Duke (2005–09): 139 games, 1,193 points, 468 assists, 39.8% from three. Three-year starter, two-time captain, three-time Academic All-ACC, two-time Academic All-American. His teams went 112–28, won four ACC championships, made four NCAA Tournaments with two Sweet Sixteens.

Freshman: led the ACC in assists (5.2 APG), ACC All-Freshman First Team. Set Duke freshman record with 15 assists vs Valparaiso — one short of Bobby Hurley’s all-time Duke record. Duke went 32–4 with the ACC double.

Junior year: led ACC in assist-to-turnover ratio, shot 42%+ from three, hit 82 threes, scored 18 in an 89–78 win over UNC. Third Team All-ACC.

Senior year was humbling: sophomore Nolan Smith’s development pushed Paulus out of the starting lineup. He started only 5 games and averaged 16 minutes. For the nation’s former #1 PG recruit, coming off the bench tested everything he was made of. His father’s old scouting report had said football made Greg tough — tough enough to handle this with grace.
bio.after:
What Paulus did next is one of the most remarkable athletic feats in modern college sports. After four years of only basketball, he used an NCAA grad transfer provision to play football at Syracuse — in the same Carrier Dome where he’d won a state championship five years earlier.

He hadn’t taken a competitive snap in four years. He competed for and won the starting job under first-year coach Doug Marrone. He started all 12 games. Completed 193 of 285 for 2,024 yards and 13 TDs, setting a Syracuse single-season record for completion percentage at 67.7%. The team went 4–8. He earned his master’s degree from the Newhouse School.

After going undrafted in 2010, he tried out with the New Orleans Saints at rookie minicamp, competing behind Drew Brees. No contract. The football dream was over. But the basketball dream had been fulfilled, and a coaching career was about to begin.
bio.now:
Paulus went into coaching immediately: Navy, then six years at Ohio State (video coordinator to assistant, 2012 Final Four, top-10 recruiting classes), Louisville, George Washington, then home to New York. In October 2019, he was named head coach at Niagara University. He is married to Megan.

In 2024, he was inducted into both the Greater Syracuse Sports Hall of Fame and the NYSPHSAA Hall of Fame.

Greg Paulus was born in Ohio, raised in Wisconsin, made in Syracuse. He broke state records for passing TDs and yards. He beat Ray Rice in a championship game in the Carrier Dome. He scored 2,399 basketball points. He was the Gatorade National Football POY and Gatorade National Athlete of the Year and NY Mr. Basketball and a McDonald’s All-American and the nation’s #1 point guard. He chose Duke over Miami football, played 139 games for Coach K, won four ACC titles, led the ACC in assists as a freshman, and came within one assist of Hurley’s record. He lost his starting job as a senior and handled it with grace. Then he went back to the Carrier Dome and started all 12 games at quarterback for Syracuse without having played football in four years. He tried out for the Saints. He became a head coach. He was inducted into two Halls of Fame. There has never been another Greg Paulus, and there probably never will be. He was the best at everything, and when he had to choose, he chose basketball. And then, because he was Greg Paulus, he went back and played football anyway.

players.name: DeMarcus Nelson
players.tagline: California’s all-time leading scorer (3,462 points). Pastor’s son from Oakland. Grew up an Arizona fan who didn’t like Duke until they showed him a Jay Williams video. Stayed all four years. ACC Defensive Player of the Year. Sole captain. Undrafted — then became the first undrafted rookie to start on NBA opening night in history. EuroLeague MVP. French Finals MVP. Vallejo retired his jersey — the first in 150 years.
players.now: Retired; lives in Las Vegas, NV; Vallejo Sports Hall of Fame (2024); jersey #21 retired at Vallejo High (Jan 2025, first in school’s 150-year history)
players.drafted: Undrafted — signed by Golden State Warriors
players.stat: 4 Duke seasons • 124 games • 14.5/5.8 senior • ACC DPOY • 1st Team All-ACC • 3rd Team All-American • Sole captain

nba.postCareer: Retired (Dec 2020); lives in Las Vegas; Vallejo Sports HOF 2024; jersey #21 retired at Vallejo High Jan 2025 (first in 150-year school history)

bio.road:
DeMarcus De’Juan Nelson was born November 2, 1985, in Oakland, California. His father Ron was a pastor who grew up playing basketball on the streets of Richmond. His mother Denise was a hair stylist. Ron had been offered a chance to play professionally in Switzerland but chose family instead. When DeMarcus was in sixth grade, his father took him from Vallejo’s local tournaments to Oakland’s courts, and the boy dominated everywhere.

At Vallejo High School, Nelson became a phenomenon. Fans lined up outside Bottari Gym to watch him play. He played three years at Vallejo before his family moved to Sacramento, finishing his senior year at Sheldon High. Over four years he scored 3,462 career points — the all-time record in California boys basketball history. California Mr. Basketball 2004. Second-team Parade All-American. McDonald’s All-American. His father said DeMarcus might even have been a better football player, but he gave up football to protect against injury.

At the 2004 McDonald’s All-American Game — Dwight Howard, Rajon Rondo, LaMarcus Aldridge, J.R. Smith — Nelson wasn’t named a starter. He took it personally. Entered midway through, scored 22 — second-highest in the game.

Growing up, he was an Arizona fan who didn’t like Duke — games came on too late on the West Coast. But when he visited Durham, they showed him a video of Jay Williams. “I just felt like I could have that same type of impact, and it would be no better platform.” He committed as a sophomore in 2002, planning to stay two or three years. He stayed all four.
bio.duke:
Nelson’s four years at Duke (2004–08) tested him in ways he never anticipated. Freshman: energy off the bench, 6.2/4.5 in 33 games. Then a torn thumb ligament and chipped bone in preseason. Another injury derailed a Team USA opportunity. The kid who planned to leave after two years kept getting knocked down and kept getting up.

Junior year: emerged as a starter, 14.4/5.1, playing 30+ minutes alongside Paulus and McRoberts. The injuries had sharpened his toughness. “The fear of failure is a good thing. It drove me to try to be as perfect as possible.”

Senior year, 2007–08: the culmination. Named sole team captain — the only captain on the roster. Averaged 14.5/5.8, led the team in scoring and steals. ACC Defensive Player of the Year. First Team All-ACC. ACC All-Tournament. Third Team All-American. The best defender in the conference, the leader of the program, the player who stayed when leaving would have been easier.

He was also the godfather to his Vallejo coach Duke Brown’s son — the infant who died at two months old. When Brown was grieving, Nelson called every morning and every evening. Not because he had to, but because he was a deeply caring person.
bio.after:
Despite ACC DPOY and Third Team All-American honors, Nelson was not drafted. The injuries had shaped how scouts evaluated him. The Golden State Warriors signed him September 9, 2008. On opening night, Nelson started — the first undrafted rookie to start on opening night in NBA history. Playing for his hometown team, friends and family in the stands. He appeared in 13 games (4.1 PPG) before being waived in January 2009.

Brief stints with Chicago and Milwaukee. Then overseas — where he thrived. Israel, Serbia, Spain, Monaco, France, Germany. In November 2013, playing for Crvena zvezda Belgrade alongside Boban Marjanovic, he was named EuroLeague MVP for Round 4. Selected to the ABA League Ideal Team. Obtained Serbian citizenship in 2014.

His finest chapter: ASVEL Lyon-Villeurbanne in France (2016–19). He helped win the LNB Pro A championship in 2019 and was named Finals MVP. Also won the French Cup that year. The undrafted kid from Vallejo had won championships and MVP awards on three continents. He retired in December 2020.
bio.now:
DeMarcus Nelson lives in Las Vegas, Nevada. On January 22, 2025, Vallejo High School retired his #21 jersey — the first jersey retirement in the school’s 150-year history. In 2024, he was inducted into the Vallejo Sports Hall of Fame. “Most of this record is Vallejo’s. It’s still my hometown and the people there can take most of the credit.”

DeMarcus Nelson was born in Oakland, the son of a pastor from Richmond who chose family over playing professionally in Switzerland. He grew up in Vallejo, where fans lined up outside Bottari Gym. He scored 3,462 points — the most in California history. He was the McDonald’s All-American who wasn’t named a starter and scored 22 with a chip on his shoulder. He grew up an Arizona fan who didn’t like Duke until they showed him a Jay Williams video and he saw himself. He planned to stay two years. He stayed four. He tore a ligament as a freshman. He kept getting up. He was ACC Defensive Player of the Year and sole captain. He called his coach every morning and night when the coach’s infant son died. He was undrafted. He started on opening night for the Warriors anyway — first undrafted player in NBA history to do so. He played in five countries. He was EuroLeague MVP. He won the French championship and was named Finals MVP. Vallejo retired his jersey — the first in 150 years. “The fear of failure is a good thing,” DeMarcus Nelson says. “It drove me to try to be as perfect as possible.” Perfectly imperfect, relentlessly tough, and forever Vallejo’s own.

players.name: Jon Scheyer
players.tagline: The Jewish Jordan from Northbrook. Scored 21 points in 75 seconds. Disliked Duke as a kid because all his friends liked Duke. Chose Duke anyway. Played every game for four years. 2010 National Champion. Recruited Zion, Tatum, Barrett, Banchero, and Cooper Flagg. Named the 20th head coach in Duke history. 2025 Final Four. National Coach of the Year. The kid who said “We’ll just do it here” is doing it here. And the Brotherhood continues.
players.now: Head Coach, Duke University men’s basketball (20th in program history, named June 4, 2021); 89 wins in first 3 seasons; 2025 Final Four; McLendon National Coach of the Year
players.drafted: Undrafted — played professionally in G-League, Israel (Maccabi Tel Aviv), and Spain (Gran Canaria)
players.stat: Player: 4 seasons • 138 games • 18.2/4.9 senior • 2nd Team All-American • 2010 Champion | Coach: 89 wins in 3 years • 2025 Final Four

coaching.note: 2022 Final Four; recruited Zion, Tatum, Barrett, Banchero
89 wins in 3 seasons (tied D-I record); 2025 Final Four; McLendon National Coach of the Year; recruited Cooper Flagg

nba.postCareer: 20th head coach in Duke basketball history; 6-year contract through 2028-29; married to Marcelle; three children: Noa, Jett, James; founded Scheyer Kid Captain program at Duke Children’s Hospital

bio.road:
Jonathan James Scheyer was born August 24, 1987, in Northbrook, Illinois, a suburb of Chicago. Youngest of three children of Jim and Laury Scheyer. Raised Jewish, had a basketball-themed Bar Mitzvah, started dribbling at age three. Played in the Fellowship of Afro-American Men league in Evanston. Tom Crean offered him a Marquette scholarship in eighth grade.

Everyone told his parents to transfer him to a powerhouse. He refused. “We’ll just do it here. We’ll build the success at Glenbrook North.” His father got chills thinking about it.

At Glenbrook North (coached by Dave Weber, Bruce Weber’s brother): 3,034 career points — 4th in Illinois history. Led the Spartans to the 2005 state championship as a junior with 27 in the title game. The team had an all-Jewish starting lineup — the only state championship team in the country known to have accomplished that. Nicknamed the “Jewish Jordan.” Illinois Mr. Basketball 2006, McDonald’s All-American, one of the 100 Legends of Illinois Basketball. His coach compared him to Larry Bird and Pistol Pete.

The legend: at the Proviso West Holiday Tournament as a senior, he scored 21 points in 75 seconds during a one-man comeback. He scored 33 against a Lawrence North team ranked #1 nationally that featured Mike Conley and Greg Oden.

He chose Duke over Arizona, Illinois, and Wisconsin. Irony: in junior high, he disliked Duke because all his friends liked Duke and he wanted to be different. He chose Duke anyway — Chris Collins, a Glenbrook North alum, was an assistant coach. Being different is not the same as being stubborn.
bio.duke:
Scheyer played four seasons (2006–10), starting all 138 games. Freshman: started every game at SG, 11.2 PPG, led ACC freshmen in FT%. Sophomore: came off bench, 11.7 PPG, .889 FT% (2nd in ACC), scored 27 off the bench at Miami (tied Duke record).

The transformation: midway through junior year, Coach K moved him from SG (91 games) to PG. Over the final 9 games and into the postseason: 19.7 PPG, 2.5 APG, 1 turnover per game. ACC Tournament MVP (21.7 PPG). Sports Illustrated: “Scheyer is one of the shyest Duke stars in recent memory. The next time he brags or trash-talks will be the first.”

Senior year: 18.2/4.9/1.6. Started all 40 games. Scored in double figures in all but two. Set the ACC single-season record for minutes played (1,470). First Team All-ACC. Consensus Second Team All-American. Duke went 35–5. On April 5, 2010, in Indianapolis, they beat Butler 61–59 for the national championship. The Big Three — Scheyer, Singler, Nolan Smith — had stayed four years and won the only trophy that mattered.
bio.after:
Undrafted. Training camp with the LA Clippers (waived). Played for Rio Grande Valley Vipers (G-League), Maccabi Tel Aviv in Israel — meaningful for a Jewish player — and Gran Canaria in Spain. An eye injury contributed to his transition to coaching.

In 2013, he returned to Duke as a special assistant. Promoted to assistant coach, then associate head coach alongside Nate James in 2018. Over the next decade, he became college basketball’s most important recruiter: the lead recruiter on Jayson Tatum, Zion Williamson, RJ Barrett, Paolo Banchero, and Cooper Flagg. He told Luke Kennard’s family: “We didn’t bring Luke here to be a basketball player. We brought him here to be a star.” Part of two national championships as coach (2010 player, 2015 coach) and two more Final Fours.
bio.now:
On June 4, 2021, Jon Scheyer was named the 20th head coach in Duke basketball history. He was thirty-five — the youngest at a power conference institution. Coach K’s final season ended with a 2022 Final Four loss to UNC. Then it was Scheyer’s program.

Year 1 (2022–23): ACC Tournament champions. Year 2 (2023–24): Elite Eight. Year 3 (2024–25): ACC regular-season and tournament championships, 32–6, Final Four (fell to Houston in the semifinals). His 89 wins in three seasons tied the D-I record. John McLendon National Coach of the Year. Duke averaged 83.2 PPG while allowing fewer than 63 — the only D-I team to achieve both. In 16 seasons as player and coach: 2 national championships, 4 Final Fours, 6 ACC Tournament crowns, 449–122 (.786).

Six-year extension through 2028–29. Married to Marcelle. Three children: Noa, Jett, and James. He and Marcelle founded the Scheyer Kid Captain program, inviting patients from Duke Children’s Hospital to spend game days with the team.

Jon Scheyer started dribbling at three. He was offered a scholarship in eighth grade. He refused to transfer and said “We’ll just do it here.” He scored 3,034 points at Glenbrook North with an all-Jewish starting lineup. They called him the Jewish Jordan. He scored 21 points in 75 seconds. He disliked Duke as a kid and chose Duke anyway. He played every game for four years. He was moved from SG to PG and became the ACC Tournament MVP. He led Duke to the 2010 championship. He played in Israel and Spain. He came back and became the most important recruiter in college basketball. He was named the 20th head coach at thirty-five. He won 89 games in three years and took Duke to the 2025 Final Four. He was named National Coach of the Year. He started the Kid Captain program at Duke Children’s Hospital. He is the bridge between the Krzyzewski era and whatever comes next — and what comes next is more of what Duke has always been: the standard. The kid from Northbrook who said “We’ll just do it here” is doing it here. And the Brotherhood continues.

players.name: Nolan Smith
players.tagline: His father won the 1980 championship with Louisville, outscored Michael Jordan, played nine NBA years, and died on a cruise ship near Bermuda when Nolan was eight. Uncle Johnny Dawkins raised him in basketball. Michael Beasley moved in as a brother. He looked at the ceiling in every arena for his father. Won the 2010 title in Indianapolis — same city as his father in 1980. ACC Player of the Year. Tattoo: Forever Watching. Now head coach at Tennessee State.
players.now: Head Coach, Tennessee State University (hired July 2025); previously assistant at Duke (2016-22), Louisville (2022-24), Memphis (2024-25)
players.stat: 4 Duke seasons • 143 games • 1,911 pts • 20.6/5.1 senior • ACC POY • Consensus All-American • 2010 Champion

coaching.role: Special Assistant / Dir. of Basketball Ops / Assistant Coach

First head coaching position; HBCU in Nashville

nba.postCareer: Head Coach, Tennessee State University; married Cheyna Elliott (UNC grad); three children: Camryn, Derek “Deuce,” and Kai

bio.road:
Nolan Derek Smith was born July 25, 1988, in Louisville, Kentucky. Named after Nolan Ryan. His mother Monica had a law degree from Louisville. His father was Derek Smith.

Derek arrived at Louisville in 1979 as a sixteen-year-old from Hogansville, Georgia — population barely a thousand — with his wardrobe in a brown paper sack. He was a starting forward on Louisville’s 1980 NCAA championship team. He outscored Michael Jordan 33–20 in a famous 1984 game. He played nine NBA years. Many credit him with helping popularize the high five. He became an assistant coach for the Washington Bullets, and little Nolan set screens on NBA paint as a toddler. “The first person to put a ball in my hands was my dad. I felt like it was at birth.”

On August 9, 1996, while on a family cruise near Bermuda, Derek suffered a fatal heart attack on deck. He was thirty-four. Nolan was eight. Their last conversation was about attitude: “If this is the sport you choose to play the rest of your life, it’s all going to come down to your attitude. Atttiiiituuuude. You hear me?”

After Derek’s death, the Bullets made Nolan a fixture at home games. Derek’s former teammate and Duke alumnus Johnny Dawkins became “Uncle Johnny” — the father figure. At thirteen, Nolan met Michael Beasley on a court. Beasley had his own problems at home. Nolan’s mother and stepfather Curtis Malone took him in. By eighth grade, Beasley had moved in permanently. “It was like the brother he never had.”

At Oak Hill Academy, Nolan played alongside Beasley and Ty Lawson. McDonald’s All-American. He considered Louisville — his father’s school — but knew the weight would be unbearable. Uncle Johnny, now at Duke, arranged a camp visit. Coach K saw the boy’s huge feet. Nolan committed January 12, 2006. He would write his own chapters while still being his father’s little shadow.
bio.duke:
Nolan played four years at Duke (2007–11), appearing in 143 games. His freshman year was emotionally devastating. Taking the floor at Cameron for the first time, he found himself looking at the ceiling for his father. It became his ritual. Every building in the ACC — always the roof. His mother shouted “Go to work!” before every tip-off. Their ode to Derek.

Freshman and sophomore years: 6.1 and 8.5 PPG. Coach K pulled him from the starting lineup midseason as a sophomore. It could have broken him. Instead, it forged him.

Junior year, 2009–10: the explosion. 17.4 PPG. Scored 29 in the Elite Eight vs Baylor. The championship game was in Indianapolis — the same city where his father won in 1980. Duke beat Butler 61–59. Nolan was a champion, thirty years after his father, in the same city. All-Final Four Team.

Senior year: the coronation. 20.6/5.1/4.5 — one of two players nationally averaging 20 and 5. Career-high 34 vs UNC. ACC Player of the Year. Consensus First Team All-American. Finalist for Naismith, Wooden, Robertson, and Cousy Awards. Duke went 32–5. His senior class finished 65–2 at home — .970, the best in Duke history.
bio.after: Portland took Smith 21st overall in 2011. Played 44 rookie games (3.8 PPG). Carried off on a stretcher in 2012 Summer League after an elbow to the head. G-League stint with Idaho Stampede (22.5 PPG). Signed with Cedevita Zagreb in Croatia (2013), where he hit a game-winner with 0.6 seconds left. The NBA career was brief. The overseas career was productive. The coaching career was inevitable.
bio.now:
Smith returned to Duke in 2016: special assistant, director of basketball ops, assistant coach by 2021. Six years on staff: 159-47 record (.772), 2022 Final Four, coached 25 future NBA players including Tatum, Williamson, Barrett, Banchero, and Bagley.

When Coach K retired, Nolan felt Louisville’s pull. Kenny Payne — Derek’s former teammate — hired him. But Louisville won just 12 games in two seasons; the entire staff was let go. Smith briefly joined Memphis before being named head coach at Tennessee State in July 2025 — an HBCU in Nashville, his first head coaching position.

He is married to Cheyna Elliott, a UNC graduate. Three children: Camryn, Derek “Deuce,” and Kai. His father is buried in Louisville’s Cave Hill Cemetery, near Colonel Sanders and a short distance from Muhammad Ali. On his right arm: a tattoo of his father’s face and the words “Forever Watching.”

Nolan Smith was born in Louisville and named after Nolan Ryan. His father arrived at Louisville with his wardrobe in a paper sack, won a championship, outscored Jordan, played nine NBA years, and died on a cruise ship near Bermuda when Nolan was eight. Johnny Dawkins became Uncle Johnny. Michael Beasley became the brother he never had. At Duke, Nolan looked at the ceiling in every arena. His mother shouted “Go to work!” He won the 2010 championship in Indianapolis — same city, thirty years later. ACC Player of the Year. Consensus All-American. Drafted 21st. Went back to Duke to coach. Went to Louisville to honor his father. Now the head coach at Tennessee State. His son is named Deuce. His father is buried near Muhammad Ali. And on his right arm, in permanent ink, Derek Smith is still watching. Forever.

bio.road:
Kyrie Andrew Irving was born March 23, 1992, in Melbourne, Australia. His parents, Drederick and Elizabeth Irving, were American expatriates. Drederick had played college basketball at Boston University, a steady scoring guard who averaged nearly 20 points as a senior and led the Terriers to a conference championship. After college, he moved his young family to Australia to play for the Bulleen Boomers. Kyrie was born in Melbourne and lived in the suburb of Kew until he was two. He holds dual American and Australian citizenship.

His mother, Elizabeth Ann Larson Irving, was African American and a member of the Standing Rock Sioux Tribe, of Lakota heritage. She had played basketball and volleyball at Boston University, where she met Drederick. Elizabeth died of an illness when Kyrie was four. She was twenty-nine. Drederick raised Kyrie and his older sister Asia with the help of Irving’s aunts, brought them back to the United States, and settled in West Orange, New Jersey. Everything he had dreamed for himself, he poured into his son.

Kyrie grew up watching Drederick’s adult-league games. His father parked Asia’s carriage at the end of the bench. Kyrie didn’t play with the other kids — he watched. In fifth grade, he attended a camp at Boston University and the head coach offered him a scholarship on the spot. He was ten years old. But the defining moment came in fourth grade, when his school visited Continental Airlines Arena. Kyrie played on the court during the trip and wrote on a piece of paper: I will play in the NBA, I promise.

He attended Montclair Kimberley Academy, a small private school, and became only the second 1,000-point scorer in school history: 26.5 PPG, 10.3 APG, 4.8 RPG, 3.6 SPG. Spectacular on the floor, invisible to the national rankings. His father believed he wasn’t getting the exposure he deserved.

Before his junior year, Kyrie transferred to St. Patrick High School in Elizabeth — a basketball powerhouse. On his first day, his host spotted him in the hallway: an untucked uniform two sizes too big, a mismatched vest, and two G-Shock watches on the same wrist. Then came the pickup game. Kyrie was shy at first. Then he threw the ball through a defender’s legs, ran after it, pulled back, and shot. The gym went quiet.

At St. Patrick, he played alongside Michael Kidd-Gilchrist. They led the team to a state championship. Senior year, the school was banned from the Tournament of Champions after an illegal preseason practice was caught on tape. Irving cried when he heard the news. That night he scored 28 against Oak Hill. He also performed in the school’s production of High School Musical. McDonald’s All-American. Jordan Brand Classic Co-MVP. FIBA Americas U18 gold. He committed to Duke on October 22, 2009, on live television.

His father told him in eighth grade he’d be the best guard in New Jersey. Coach K saw him in an AAU tournament and said: you are single-handedly killing all these teams. If you are fortunate to have a father like I have, Kyrie would later say, you’re given a foundation. You can be content with that, or take it and run with it, like I did.
bio.duke:
Kyrie Irving played eleven games at Duke. Eleven. It was enough. Through the first eight games of 2010–11, he averaged 17.4 points on 53.2% shooting, 5.1 assists, 3.8 rebounds, and 1.5 steals. He was the best freshman point guard in America. On December 1, he scored 31 against Michigan State — one of the most electrifying individual Duke freshman performances in decades.

Then, in the ninth game, he suffered a severe ligament injury to his right big toe. He missed the next twenty-six games. Duke’s season went on without him, and his absence was felt in every possession.

He returned March 17, the day before Duke’s first-round tournament game against Hampton. He scored 14 in his comeback, then 28 against Michigan in the second round — his final game in a Duke uniform. Duke lost to Arizona in the Sweet Sixteen.

Irving declared for the 2011 draft: "Duke has a special place in my heart. Even though I’m leaving this year, Duke will always be in my mind and my heart. I’m going to miss putting on that number one jersey." He promised his father he would finish his bachelor’s degree within five years.

Eleven games. 17.5 PPG, 4.3 APG, 3.4 RPG. 52.9% from the field, 46.2% from three. The shortest Duke career that ever produced a number-one overall pick. And arguably the most purely talented guard to ever wear Duke blue.
bio.after:
Cleveland selected Irving first overall in 2011. He was nineteen. Rookie of the Year. Rising Stars MVP (8-of-8 from three, 34 points). Youngest player to score 40 at Madison Square Garden — younger than Jordan when he did it. All-Star Game MVP in 2014.

LeBron came home. The Big Three — James, Love, Irving — reached the 2015 Finals, but Irving fractured his kneecap in the opener against Golden State. He watched from the sideline as Cleveland lost. He came back in 2015–16 determined to finish what was taken.

Game 7, 2016 NBA Finals. Cleveland at Golden State. The Warriors had won 73 regular-season games. The Cavaliers trailed 3–1 in the series. With 53 seconds remaining, the cumulative score of the entire Finals — through 335+ minutes — was 699–699. Irving caught the ball on the right wing. Curry switched onto him. Irving jabbed, gave him an in-and-out, created a half-step of space, and rose for a step-back three over the two-time MVP. It went in. Cleveland won 93–89. The franchise’s first championship in fifty-two years. The Wall Street Journal called it the biggest shot in NBA history. Irving hugged LeBron. He hugged his coach. He found his father and sister and gave them his jersey.

Then the wandering. Trade request in 2017 — tired of LeBron’s shadow — dealt to Boston. Knee surgery, turbulent exit. Brooklyn: three seasons with Kevin Durant, derailed by injuries, a vaccination refusal, an antisemitism controversy and suspension. Dallas: a February 2023 trade that rejuvenated his career. In 2023–24, Irving and Luka Dončić led the Mavericks to the Finals, losing to Boston in five.

Fifteen seasons. Nine All-Star selections. Three All-NBA Teams. A championship. An Olympic gold. Career averages of ~23 PPG, 5.7 APG. The Uncle Drew Pepsi character that became a feature film. The best ball-handler many have ever seen. And always the complications: flat-earth comments, controversies, absences, restlessness. Kyrie Irving has never been simple. His talent has never been in question.
bio.now:
In August 2018, Kyrie Irving traveled to the Standing Rock Sioux Reservation in South Dakota. His mother Elizabeth had been born into the Standing Rock Sioux Tribe. In a naming ceremony, Irving received the Lakota name Hĕla — Little Mountain. He donated food and supplies and has returned multiple times. He named his daughter Azurie Elizabeth, after the mother he lost at four.

On March 3, 2025, Irving tore the ACL in his left knee in the first quarter against Sacramento. Surgery on March 26. In February 2026, the Mavericks announced he would miss the entire 2025–26 season — the first full season he’s missed in fifteen years. He’s targeting 2026–27. His stepmother and agent, Shetellia Riley Irving, said the priority is making sure Kyrie is one thousand percent. Irving, now thirty-three, is mentoring Cooper Flagg, the Mavericks’ rookie — the next Duke Brotherhood member to carry the franchise.

He was born in Melbourne and lost his mother at four. His father raised him alone and taught him to want more. He wrote in fourth grade: I will play in the NBA, I promise. He showed up at St. Patrick’s in a uniform two sizes too big and two watches on one wrist. He played eleven games at Duke and was drafted first. He won a championship with a three over Curry when the cumulative Finals score was tied. He went to Standing Rock and received a name in his mother’s language. He has played for five teams, been suspended, celebrated, booed, brilliant, maddening, and undeniably one of the most gifted basketball players who has ever lived. He is recovering from a torn ACL. He is mentoring a nineteen-year-old from Maine. He promised his father he would finish his degree. He named his daughter after the mother he barely knew. Little Mountain is still climbing.
