bio.road:
Wendell Carter Jr.’s story begins before he was born. His father, Wendell Sr., was born to a convicted felon in Atlanta. Having a child out of wedlock violated her parole, so she abandoned the baby in an apartment. Police broke in and found an infant alone, diapers soiled. He was sent to an orphanage until age five or six, when a woman adopted him and raised him as her own.

Years later, Wendell Sr. drove his teenage son to that woman’s gravestone. They both broke down. That was the day Wendell Jr. learned his origin story — how his father had been abandoned, found by police, raised in an orphanage, and saved by a woman who chose to love him. “I could have easily ran with the wrong crowd,” Wendell Sr. said. “But I was so focused on basketball.”

Basketball saved him. He grew to 6’6 with a 42-inch vertical, played at Delta State, then professionally in the Dominican Republic. He met Kylia through her sister — he was heading to a pro-am game where Dominique Wilkins and Spud Webb played. Kylia was 6’5, a basketball player at Ole Miss. “I thought he was cute. But any excuse to go watch basketball.” At halftime: a dunk contest. Twenty-six years later, the trophy sits on their piano.

When Wendell Jr. arrived — 11 lbs 8 oz, 26 inches long — he was born to play basketball. At Pace Academy in Atlanta: 22.7/15.5/5.5 as a senior, a state championship with 30 points and 20 rebounds in the title game, Morgan Wootten National POY, 3.8 GPA, National Honor Society, the school’s Lance and Shield Award, and a role in the school play Thoroughly Modern Millie.

He seriously considered Harvard. His mother pushed hard for the Ivy League. In second grade, a teacher asked what students wanted to be. Wendell wrote “professional basketball player.” The class laughed. His mother still has the paper. He chose Duke — the compromise between Harvard’s academics and championship basketball.
bio.duke:
Carter arrived for 2017–18 alongside Marvin Bagley III and one of Duke’s most talented freshman classes. On a team where Bagley was the headliner, Carter was the anchor — the defense, rim protection, and efficiency that Bagley’s scoring required.

Over 37 starts: 13.5/9.1/2.0/2.1 blocks, shooting 56.1% from the field and 46.3% from three. Second all-time among Duke freshmen in rebounds (335), blocks (76), and double-doubles (16). Second Team All-ACC, unanimous ACC All-Freshman Team. The NBA comparison: Al Horford — a lack of flash but an abundance of fundamentals.

Duke reached the Elite Eight. Carter declared for the 2018 Draft.
bio.after:
Chicago took Carter 7th overall. His mother rejected the idea that being a first-round pick meant her 19-year-old was ready to live alone: “Every other 19-year-old lives at home with their momma. So why do we become helicopter parents because our kid’s a millionaire?”

Two and a half seasons in Chicago (~11/8), then traded to Orlando in March 2021, where his career found its fullest expression. He became the starting center of one of the NBA’s most promising young cores and signed a four-year, $50 million extension. In 2024–25: 68 games, 9.1/7.2/2.0, 29th in the NBA in rebounding. Surpassed 4,000 career points and 3,000 career rebounds. Averaged a double-double in the playoffs (10.2/10.8 in 32.4 min).

Through seven seasons: 383 games, 345 starts, 11.9/8.3/2.0, 50.9% FG. The modern NBA center: rim protector who stretches the floor, facilitates from the high post, and anchors a defense without demanding the ball.
bio.now:
Wendell Carter Jr. is the starting center for the Orlando Magic on his $50M extension. He and his parents founded A Platform Squared, a nonprofit focused on financial literacy, social justice, mentorship, and economic empowerment. He received the NBA Cares Bob Lanier Community Assist Award and the Presidential Scholar Award in 2022 for combining basketball with education advocacy. His mother Kylia became a prominent critic of NCAA exploitation and co-founded Elevate to empower athletes and families.

Wendell Carter Jr. was born at 11 lbs 8 oz in Atlanta. His father was abandoned as an infant, raised in an orphanage, saved by an adoptive mother, and went on to play professional basketball. His mother was 6’5 at Ole Miss and valued education above everything. They met at a dunk contest. Their son mimicked his father’s moves on the church court at age three. He earned a 3.8 GPA, won a state championship with 30/20, acted in Thoroughly Modern Millie, and seriously considered Harvard. He chose Duke, formed a frontcourt with Bagley, averaged a double-double, and was drafted seventh. Seven NBA seasons, 345 starts, 3,000 rebounds, $50 million contract. His mother still has the piece of paper from second grade where he wrote that he wanted to be a professional basketball player. The other kids laughed. Nobody is laughing now.

players.name: Cam Reddish
players.tagline: Norristown, PA. Father knew at age four. And1 founder as his high school coach. Mo Bamba as his teammate. Kevin Durant called him a star. The third piece of Zion/RJ/Cam — three top-10 picks from one class. Game-winner at FSU. Drafted 10th. Five NBA teams. Lithuania. Now the G-League. The heartbeat doesn’t stop.
players.now: Forward, San Diego Clippers (G-League); previously Lakers, Knicks, Hawks, Trail Blazers, Siauliai (Lithuania)
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 13.5/3.7/1.9 • 22 pts vs Kentucky debut • GW 3 at FSU • 10th pick • 5 NBA teams

sources.label: SI: Cam Reddish Could Reset Expectations for the Modern Basketball Player (2018)
PA Prep Live: Westtown’s Reddish signs NLI to attend Duke (2017)
GoDuke.com: Cam Reddish player bio
RotoWire: Cam Reddish career overview
Wikipedia: Cam Reddish

teams.note: G-League; previously Austin Spurs and Siauliai (Lithuania)

highlights.note: Rookie year: 10.5 PPG in 58 games with Atlanta
Signed with Siauliai (Lithuania); returned to US for personal reasons

nba.postCareer: Still active — San Diego Clippers (G-League, 2025-26); Reddish Family Foundation (0K food drive for Norristown, 2021)

bio.road:
Cameron Elijah Reddish was born September 1, 1999, in Norristown, Pennsylvania, a working-class suburb northwest of Philadelphia. His father Robert played college basketball at VCU. His mother Zanthia raised Cam and his younger brother Aaron in a household where basketball was the heartbeat. Robert knew his son would play in the NBA when Cam was four, doing things with a basketball that eight-year-olds couldn’t do.

Cam played varsity as an eighth-grader and freshman at the Haverford School, then transferred to Westtown School in West Chester — a 600-acre Quaker boarding school with rigorous academics and a basketball coach named Seth Berger, the founder of And1. Robert knew Berger because he’d coached Berger’s son in AAU. At Westtown, Cam teamed with Mohamed Bamba and Brandon Randolph, winning back-to-back Friends’ School League championships and state titles.

Senior year: 22.6/5.6, Mr. Pennsylvania Basketball, McDonald’s All-American, Jordan Brand Classic, Nike Hoop Summit. On the EYBL circuit: 23.8/7.6/3.1 for Team Final. Kevin Durant, watching his highlights: “I like this kid, man. He’s going to be a star.”

He was the #3 recruit in the Class of 2018. He chose Duke over UConn, Kentucky, Villanova, and UCLA. “I knew from the jump I wanted to go there.” His mother: “Lots of conversations and lots of prayer. When he decided it was Duke, we said Duke it is.” He would join RJ Barrett and Zion Williamson to form the most hyped freshman class in college basketball history.
bio.duke:
Reddish’s freshman season (2018–19) was defined by being the third star on a team with two transcendent ones. Zion was the most exciting player in college basketball. Barrett was the most prolific scorer. Reddish was the silky 6’8 wing — the versatile scorer who could stretch the floor and create his own shot.

He debuted with 22 points in Duke’s 118–84 demolition of Kentucky. Scored 25 vs Army, 24 vs BC, 22 in a comeback vs Louisville. On January 12, 2019, he hit a game-winning three at FSU in the final seconds. Over 36 games: 13.5/3.7/1.9.

The shooting was inconsistent — 33% from three, 35.6% overall — and Reddish acknowledged that playing alongside two ball-dominant stars limited his rhythm. “People mistake the smoothness of my game for a lack of motor. I went to Duke to grow and prepare for the NBA, and I did that.”

Draft night, June 20, 2019: Duke made history. Zion went #1 to New Orleans. Barrett went #3 to New York. Reddish went #10 to Atlanta. Three top-10 picks from one school — only the second time it had ever happened (Florida, 2007).
bio.after:
Atlanta took Reddish 10th overall. Rookie year showed flashes — 10.5 PPG, defensive versatility — but injuries accumulated: core muscle, right Achilles, abdominal issues. In January 2022, traded to the Knicks. Traded again to Portland, who waived him. Signed with the Lakers for 2023–24 under JJ Redick, a fellow Duke alumnus — 58 games, 4.4 PPG.

In summer 2025, Reddish signed with Siauliai of the Lithuanian league, his first overseas stint. He left in December by mutual agreement for personal reasons. In January 2026, assigned to the Austin Spurs (G-League), then traded to the San Diego Clippers.

At twenty-six, the tenth overall pick — the kid Durant called a star, the third member of Duke’s most celebrated class — is in the G-League, fighting to get back.
bio.now:
Cam Reddish is currently with the San Diego Clippers of the G-League. He has earned approximately $30 million across five NBA teams and five seasons. He has never stopped working.

In 2021, he and the Reddish Family Foundation hosted a food drive and donated $10,000 to Norristown. His favorite player growing up was Allen Iverson — the Philadelphia legend, the player who played with a chip on his shoulder and an answer for everyone who doubted him.

Cam Reddish was born in Norristown, the son of a VCU basketball player who knew his boy would be NBA at four. He played at Westtown for the founder of And1, winning state titles alongside Mo Bamba. Kevin Durant watched his highlights and said he was going to be a star. He joined Zion and RJ at Duke to form the most celebrated freshman class in history. He hit a game-winning three at FSU. He was drafted tenth — the third top-ten pick from one school in one draft. He has played for five NBA teams. He went to Lithuania and came home. He is now in the G-League at twenty-six, fighting to get back. His father knew at four. Durant said it on YouTube. The smoothness was always there. The motor was always there. People mistook one for the absence of the other. Cam Reddish is still playing basketball, because basketball is his heartbeat, and a heartbeat does not stop just because the arena gets smaller.

charity.name: Norristown Recreation Center
charity.note: Home of the Cam Reddish Room. Cam donated $10,000 to create a gaming and community space for Norristown youth. Non-profit youth recreation center offering basketball leagues, camps, and community programs.

players.name: AJ Griffin
players.tagline: Born into an NBA family. Shot on regulation hoops at age two while NBA players stopped to watch. Part of Coach K’s final season. 16th pick. Game-winning alley-oops as a rookie with his father coaching on the opposing bench. Then retired at 21 to follow Jesus. The Brotherhood’s most unexpected story.
players.now: Retired (Sept 2024, age 21); pursuing full-time Christian ministry; youngest voluntary retirement by an NBA first-round pick
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 10.4 PPG • .493/.447 shooting • ACC All-Freshman • 16th pick • 92 NBA games • Retired at 21

sources.label: CBS Sports: AJ Griffin explains why he gave up basketball to follow Jesus (2024)
BET: AJ Griffin Quits the NBA to Follow Jesus (2024)
EssentiallySports: Who Is AJ Griffin? (2024)
GoDuke.com: AJ Griffin player bio
Wikipedia: AJ Griffin (basketball)

teams.note: Rookie: 72 games, 8.9 PPG, 39% 3PT; two game-winning alley-oops
Traded June 2024; retired before playing a game

highlights.note: Game-winning alley-oop vs Toronto (Nov 19) with father coaching opposing bench
Game-winning alley-oop vs Chicago (Dec 11)
Retired September 29, 2024, at age 21 to pursue full-time Christian ministry

nba.postCareer: Retired at 21 (Sept 2024); pursuing full-time Christian ministry; youngest voluntary retirement by an NBA first-round pick

bio.road:
Adrian Darnell Griffin Jr. was born August 25, 2003, in Dallas, Texas. His father Adrian Griffin Sr. played ten NBA seasons after going undrafted from Seton Hall in 1996 — a journeyman wing who started in the 2006 NBA Finals for the Mavericks. His mother Aubrey Sterling was an All-American track athlete at Seton Hall. His brother Alan played at Illinois and Syracuse. His sister Aubrey played at UConn. Basketball and athletic excellence ran through every branch.

At age two, while other kids played with kiddie hoops, AJ took an NBA regulation basketball, cocked it behind his head, and launched it at a regulation net. The first time it went in, everyone thought it was cute. Then he kept doing it. The whole team stopped to watch. “His strength at that age was unbelievable,” his father said. “He just loved basketball.”

At Archbishop Stepinac in White Plains, NY, he played alongside his brother Alan as a freshman, winning the school’s first CHSAA title since 1984. As a sophomore: 20.9/10.9/3.9/3.5 blocks, forming one of the nation’s top backcourts with R.J. Davis. In 7th and 8th grade at Ossining, he played varsity alongside future NBA player Obi Toppin. Five-star recruit, projected top-five pick.

He committed to Duke in November 2019 over Kentucky and Villanova. But in 2020, during COVID lockdowns, AJ gave his life to Christ. “My values started to change. My heart started to change.” The transformation that would eventually lead him away from basketball had already begun.
bio.duke:
Griffin’s one season at Duke (2021–22) was Coach K’s final year. He started 25 of 39 games: 10.4 PPG on .493/.447 shooting — the fifth-highest 3PT% in Duke history, second-best by a freshman. He scored 12.6 in ACC play on .489 shooting.

Duke went 32–7, won the ACC regular-season and tournament titles, and reached the Final Four before falling to North Carolina in Coach K’s last game. ACC All-Freshman Team. Honorable Mention All-ACC. He declared for the 2022 Draft — part of a record five Duke players selected.
bio.after:
Atlanta took Griffin 16th overall. Rookie year was promising: 72 games, 8.9 PPG, 39% from three. He hit game-winning alley-oops in consecutive November and December games vs Toronto and Chicago — the Toronto winner coming with his father, then a Raptors assistant, on the opposing bench. The image of father watching son hit a game-winner against his own team became one of the season’s iconic moments.

His father’s career took its own turn: Adrian Sr. was named Bucks head coach in May 2023, then fired 34 games in despite a 30–13 record. AJ’s second season: just 20 games before being traded to Houston in June 2024.

Then, September 29, 2024: AJ Griffin retired. At twenty-one. “I gave up basketball to follow Jesus.” He wanted full-time Christian ministry. “You can have all the riches in the world, but the only thing that truly matters is your relationship with Jesus. He’s the only one who can completely satisfy you.” He traced it to 2020, when he gave his life to Christ during the pandemic. No first-round pick had ever voluntarily retired this young.
bio.now:
AJ Griffin is pursuing full-time Christian ministry. He is twenty-one. He earned approximately $8 million in two NBA seasons. He plans to share the Gospel through social media and in-person ministry. “As I step out of one season of my life, I’m excited to step into a new one with my Lord and Savior.”

AJ Griffin was born into an NBA family. He shot on regulation hoops at age two while players stopped to watch. He played varsity as a seventh-grader alongside Obi Toppin. He was part of Coach K’s final season, hitting threes at a rate that ranks among the best in Duke history. He was drafted 16th and hit game-winning alley-oops as a rookie with his father coaching on the opposing bench. He had every reason to keep playing. The money, the fame, the bloodline, the talent — all of it pointed toward a long career. And then, at twenty-one, he walked away. Not because of injury. Not because of failure. Because he found something he wanted more than basketball. The Brotherhood includes players who gave everything to the game. AJ Griffin is the first who gave the game back to give everything to something else. “He found me,” Griffin said, smiling. “It was the best decision of my life.”

players.name: Jeremy Roach
players.tagline: Picked up a basketball at six months old. Tore his ACL in high school, came back, committed to Duke. Played four years when everyone else played one. COVID season. Coach K’s farewell Final Four. Scheyer’s first ACC title. Two-time captain. 1,469 points. Then transferred to Baylor and faced his Brotherhood in the NCAA Tournament. The bridge between two eras.
players.now: Guard, Baylor University (5th-year transfer, 2024-25); faced Duke in 2025 NCAA Tournament 2nd round
players.drafted: Undeclared — used COVID extra year at Baylor for 2024-25
players.stat: 4 Duke seasons • 130 games • 108 starts • 1,469 pts • 14.0/3.3 senior • 42.9% 3PT • 2x captain

sources.label: GoDuke.com: Jeremy Roach player bio
Arlington Catholic Herald: Meet the diocesan high schooler headed to Duke (2020)
Hoops HQ: Jeremy Roach to face his former team (2025)
Duke Chronicle: Jeremy Roach commits to Baylor (2024)
CBS Sports: Jeremy Roach enters transfer portal (2024)

nba.postCareer: 5th-year guard at Baylor University (2024-25); faced Duke in 2025 NCAA Tournament 2nd round; two USA Basketball gold medals (FIBA U-16 Americas 2017, FIBA U-17 World Cup 2018)

bio.road:
Jeremy Roach was born November 1, 2001, in Washington, D.C., and raised in Leesburg, Virginia. His mother Carole says the story began at six months old: he sat down, picked up a full-size basketball, and started throwing it in the air. “He just had to have a ball.” Playing organized basketball by age four. On the AAU circuit with Team Takeover by middle school.

At St. Paul VI Catholic High School in Fairfax — a 45-minute commute each way from Leesburg — he became the first boys’ basketball player in school history to sign with Duke. State champion as a sophomore. 3.1+ GPA all four years. Two USA Basketball gold medals (FIBA U-16 Americas, FIBA U-17 World Cup). Virginia Gatorade Player of the Year.

Then in November 2018, junior year: ACL tear. A normal move — high ball screen, step left, step right, knee collapsed. He knew immediately. The Washington Post, USA Today, and Bleacher Report covered it. He came back. On May 8, 2019, he became the first five-star in the Class of 2020 to commit, choosing Duke over Kentucky, UNC, and Villanova.
bio.duke:
Roach played four Duke seasons (2020–24) and lived through nearly every scenario possible.

Freshman, 2020–21: the COVID season. Duke went 13–11 and missed the NCAA Tournament for the first time since 1995. Roach averaged 8.7/3.2 as the starting PG.

Sophomore, 2021–22: Coach K’s final season. Starting PG alongside Banchero, Griffin, Williams, Moore. Duke went 32–7, won the ACC regular-season title, reached the Final Four, lost to UNC in Coach K’s last game. Rumored he might transfer. He stayed.

Junior, 2022–23: Scheyer’s first year. Named captain, earned team MVP. Career-high 23 in the ACC championship game win over Virginia (19 in the second half). Matched it with 23 vs Oral Roberts in the NCAA Tournament. Averaged 13.6 PPG. He stayed again.

Senior, 2023–24: captain again. 14.0/3.3, career-best 42.9% from three. Surpassed 1,000 career points (68th in Duke history). Scored 14 second-half points vs #1 Houston in the Sweet Sixteen. Duke reached the Elite Eight. In four years: 130 games, 108 starts, Final Four, ACC Tournament title, ACC regular-season title, Elite Eight. Part of 121 wins — tied for 6th in ACC history. Two coaches. Two-time captain. In an era when everyone leaves after one year, Jeremy Roach stayed four.
bio.after: After four years, Roach entered the transfer portal in April 2024, using his extra COVID year. He committed to Baylor. Scheyer: “We’ve been through a lot together” — handled with love and honesty. Roach: “I am forever grateful to be part of the Brotherhood. I’ll always be a Blue Devil.”
bio.now:
In March 2025, the draw produced the moment: ninth-seeded Baylor faced top-seeded Duke in the NCAA Tournament second round. Jeremy Roach, wearing Baylor’s jersey, faced the program he had given four years to. “All those guys over there are my brothers. I’ll just imagine it’s some of the pickup runs we had in the summer.” Duke won. Roach played with the same toughness and poise he had shown for four years in Durham.

His brother Jordan played at Christopher Newport. His sister Chloe played at Eastern Mennonite. Basketball was the family language.

Jeremy Roach picked up a basketball at six months old and never put it down. He won two gold medals with USA Basketball before he could drive. He tore his ACL as a high school junior, came back, and became the first five-star in his class to commit to Duke. He played four years when everyone else played one. He lived through COVID, Coach K’s farewell, and Scheyer’s beginning. He was captain twice. He scored 1,469 points. He hit the biggest shots in the biggest games. He stayed when staying was harder than leaving. Then he left with grace, transferred to Baylor, and faced his Brotherhood on the tournament stage. In an era defined by departures, Jeremy Roach proved that four years at Duke still means something. He was the bridge between two eras, and bridges are built to last.

players.name: Kyle Filipowski
players.tagline: Two years in the post-K era.
players.now: Utah Jazz; 4-year/$12M contract; Scheyer era’s first cornerstone
players.stat: 2 Duke seasons • 70 games • Fr: 15.1 PPG/9.0 RPG (16 double-doubles) • ACC ROY • ACC Tourney MVP • 1st-Team All-ACC

bio.road:
Kyle Jarred Filipowski was born November 7, 2003, in Middletown, New York. His family was built on basketball the way some families are built on farming — the language spoken at the dinner table, the reason the car was always pointed toward a gymnasium.

His mother, Rebecca Hagerdon Filipowski, was a 6-4 force at Warwick Valley High School who scored 2,164 career points and was a 1982 Parade All-American. She played at Long Beach State. Knee injuries ended her career; she became a coach and teacher. Her sister Beverly played at Dartmouth. Her brother Randall at Boston College. His father David played at Slippery Rock University. David and Rebecca met at Warwick Valley. Four sons: Daniel, Taylor, and twins Kyle and Matthew.

Rebecca was Kyle’s first coach. She taught fundamentals, drove to AAU tournaments, eventually left teaching to invest in her sons’ development. David retired early to attend games. The family organized around basketball like a military family around deployments.

Kyle and twin Matt were inseparable on the court, playing together at Wilbraham & Monson Academy. Matt, also 6-11, committed to Harvard. Three high schools in five years: Minisink Valley (freshman), Fordham Prep (sophomore), Wilbraham & Monson (junior/senior). Each move elevated competition. In 99 prep games: 1,712 points, 973 rebounds. 2021 Gatorade Massachusetts POY. Naismith All-America first team. Also played baseball and water polo. Magna Cum Laude academic award. Won gold at the 2021 FIBA 3x3 U18 World Cup.

Committed to Duke July 29, 2021 — the first year of Jon Scheyer’s era. Filipowski would be the new coach’s first cornerstone.
bio.duke:
Filipowski arrived for Scheyer’s first season, 2022–23. The legend had retired. The banners hung in Cameron but the man who put them there was gone. Filipowski was asked to bridge the eras.

Freshman: 15.1 PPG, 9.0 RPG. Led all D-I freshmen with 16 double-doubles. 28 points/15 rebounds vs Pittsburgh. 22/13 vs North Carolina. In the ACC Tournament final vs Virginia: 20 and 10 — named Tournament MVP. ACC Rookie of the Year. A throwback big with modern versatility: post scoring, three-point shooting, high-post passing.

Despite being projected first round in the 2023 draft, Filipowski came back. A bet on himself and the program. In an era when the best freshmen almost always leave, he returned. Scheyer’s first cornerstone was also his most loyal.

Sophomore: 16.4 PPG, 8.3 RPG, 2.9 APG. First-Team All-ACC. Consensus second-team All-American. Career-high 30 vs Georgia Tech. Duke went 27–9.

On February 24, 2024, after Wake Forest upset Duke, the crowd stormed the court. Filipowski was struck by a rushing fan and suffered an apparent knee injury. The collision ignited a national conversation about court-storming safety. Scheyer called for a ban. Filipowski played four days later. The toughness was real.
bio.after:
Filipowski declared for the 2024 draft as a projected first-round pick. He received a green room invitation. He sat through the entire first round. His name was never called.

Reports connected the slide to concerns about his personal life — his relationship with his fiancée, Caitlin Hutchison, who is several years older, and his estrangement from the family that had organized its entire life around his basketball career. The personal swallowed the professional on draft night.

The Utah Jazz selected him 32nd overall. They stated they’d done a thorough background check and were excited regardless of the noise. He signed a 4-year, $12M contract on August 12, 2024. Rookie season: splitting time between the Jazz and their G League affiliate, the Salt Lake City Stars. Early returns: 6.3 PPG, 4.9 RPG, 1.9 APG with Utah. The tools are there. The development is ongoing.
bio.now:
Kyle Filipowski is 21 years old. He plays for the Utah Jazz. His twin brother Matt plays basketball at Harvard. His mother Rebecca, who scored 2,164 points at Warwick Valley and was his first coach, is no longer part of his daily life the way she once was. The family that organized itself around his career has fractured. The details belong to the Filipowskis. What is public is the pain.

What is also public: two seasons at Duke. 70 games. Sixteen double-doubles as a freshman — more than any freshman in D-I. ACC ROY. ACC Tournament MVP. Came back when the money said leave. First-Team All-ACC. Consensus All-American. Knocked down by a fan during a court-storming and played four days later. The first cornerstone of Scheyer’s Duke.

He was born in Middletown. His mother was a Parade All-American who taught him the game. His aunt at Dartmouth, his uncle at Boston College. He and his twin played side by side. Three high schools. Magna Cum Laude. Gold medal with USA Basketball. Chose Duke and became the best freshman in the country. Came back when he could have left. Got knocked down and got back up. Sat in the green room and watched thirty-one names called before his. Now he’s in Utah, twenty-one, building the career his family spent two decades preparing him for — navigating the distance between where he ended up and where he started. The cost of ambition is sometimes measured in that distance. Kyle Filipowski is still calculating.

highlights.note: Sophomore showing flashes as dynamic offensive threat from all three levels

nba.postCareer: Utah Jazz; 4-year/$12M contract; Scheyer era’s first cornerstone

Kyle Filipowski Utah Jazz Draft Pick - Deseret News

players.name: Dereck Lively II
players.tagline: Born in Philadelphia. Raised in Bellefonte. Coached by his mother. Lost her two months before the Finals.
players.now: Dallas Mavericks; NBA Finals 2024; recovering from foot surgery
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 34 games • 5.2 PPG/5.4 RPG/2.4 BPG • 65.8% FG • 82 blocks (led all D-I freshmen)

bio.road:
Dereck Jerome Lively II was born February 12, 2004, in Philadelphia. His mother, Kathy Phillips Drysdale, was a 6-4 standout at Penn State (1988–92): 1,295 points, 717 rebounds, 89 blocks, part of the Lady Lions team that reached #1 in the AP poll in 1991. She spent 13 years with the Philadelphia 76ers in game operations. His father, Dereck Sr., was a Philadelphia-born chef — boisterous, funny, full of life. But he battled drug addiction.

In 2011, Kathy moved the family to Bellefonte, PA, near State College, for a marketing role at Penn State. On January 5, 2012, five weeks before Dereck’s eighth birthday, his father died of an overdose. The boy walked to the stairs and peered down. He saw his father on the floor, black and blue, lifeless.

Not long after, Kathy told her nine-year-old she had Hodgkin’s lymphoma. She would fight it for eleven years — through chemotherapy, a stem cell transplant, a brief remission — while coaching her son in AAU ball, driving to every tournament, and working full-time at Penn State.

A HoopGroup event gave Dereck his first exposure. Team Final (Nike EYBL) recruited him. Kathy found a documentary called We Town about Westtown School’s basketball program (Mo Bamba, Cam Reddish) and made him watch it. He agreed to attend the boarding school three hours from home. An ankle injury cost him freshman year. COVID sent him back to Bellefonte, where he worked out daily at the local YMCA with his mother, adding muscle and an outside shot. When he returned, Kathy didn’t recognize him.

Senior year: #1 recruit nationally (ESPN 100). 14 PPG/14 RPG/4.5 BPG. PAISAA state championship. McDonald’s All-American. PA Gatorade POY. Chose Duke over Kentucky, Michigan, and his mother’s Penn State.
bio.duke:
Lively arrived as the headliner of a #1 nationally ranked class. The stat line looked nothing like what people expected: 5.2 PPG. But the impact was deafening.

He led all D-I freshmen with 82 blocks. Shot 65.8% from the field. His 7-7 wingspan made him a nightmare at the rim. ACC All-Freshman. ACC All-Defensive. In March Madness: 5.8 PPG, 7.4 RPG, 3.0 BPG on 76.5% shooting. The deeper the tournament went, the bigger he played. Duke won the ACC Tournament championship.

Coach Scheyer later told CBS Sports the summary: Lively was the most impactful defensive player in the country by the end of the season. He declared for the draft after one season.
bio.after:
Dallas selected Lively 12th overall — a pick born from an organizational tank that drew a $750K fine. The payoff was immediate. Rookie year: 55 games, 42 starts, 8.8 PPG/6.9 RPG/1.4 BPG on 74.7% shooting (would have led the NBA). All-Rookie Second Team. Dallas’s defense jumped from 21st to 6th.

His mother moved to Dallas in November 2023. She sat behind the bench. She attended community functions. On April 12, 2024, Kathy Drysdale died at age 53, surrounded by her son, her sisters, and the Mavericks organization. She’d fought cancer for 11 years. Before she died, she told him: ‘Dear Dereck, you made it. Persevered, but you are not done yet.’

Two months later, he was in the NBA Finals. In Game 4, down 3-0, Luka found Lively alone in the corner. He hadn’t attempted a three all postseason. He launched a moonshot that splashed through the net. Dallas won 122-84. He posted back-to-back double-doubles — the first rookie to do so since Magic Johnson in 1980. When asked about the three, he said his mother helped him make it. She was above him, guiding the ball.
bio.now:
Dereck Lively II is 21. In his second season (2024–25), he averaged 9.1 PPG/7.8 RPG/2.6 APG in 32 games before a stress fracture, then foot surgery in December 2025 ruled him out. The injuries test patience. He’s been tested before.

He is an only child. His father died when he was seven. His mother was diagnosed with cancer when he was nine. She coached him in a YMCA gym while undergoing chemo. She moved to Dallas to watch him play. She died two months before the Finals. The Mavericks’ statement said it best: so much of Kathy — her strength, tenacity, and joy — is reflected in her son.

Every game Dereck Lively II plays is a game played for two. His mother told him he wasn’t even close to being done. He’s not. He’s twenty-one years old, and there are so many more three-pointers to guide through the rim.

players.name: Cooper Flagg
players.tagline: Born in Newport, Maine. Raised by headlights. The #1 pick.
players.now: Dallas Mavericks; #1 overall pick 2025 NBA Draft; 2nd-youngest #1 pick in history
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 37 games • 19.2 PPG/7.5 RPG/4.2 APG/1.4 SPG/1.3 BPG • Naismith + Wooden Awards

bio.road:
In a house three miles from the one where she would raise him, Kelly Bowman fell in love with basketball at age seven. Her father Dan affixed a hoop to a shed in their backyard in Newport, Maine — population 3,200. They played so often the grass faded into dirt. When it got dark, he’d park a car and turn on the headlights so they could keep shooting.

Kelly starred at Nokomis Regional High School, then earned a D-I scholarship to the University of Maine (team captain, three-time conference champion). Ralph Flagg also attended Nokomis, played at Eastern Maine Community College. They met at a community center basketball game. He couldn’t stop her up-and-under move. As a wedding present, they received a crabapple tree and planted it next to the driveway hoop.

In 2004, Kelly went into premature labor at 24 weeks. Twins Hunter and Ryder were born at Maine Medical Center. Hunter: 1 lb 10 oz. Ryder: two ounces lighter. Ryder died two days later. Hunter spent 109 days in the NICU. Kelly refused to leave Portland. The Ronald McDonald House became home.

Cooper and Ace Flagg were born premature on December 21, 2006, but healthy enough to go home for Christmas. The household was organized around basketball. Kelly coached Nokomis girls’ varsity; the boys grew up in the gym. Driveway games ended when someone ran inside bleeding. Cooper played in a 4th-grade rec league as a 2nd-grader.

In March 2022, all three Flagg brothers — Hunter a senior, Cooper and Ace freshmen — led Nokomis to its first state championship. Cooper: 22 pts/16 reb in the title game. First freshman named Maine Gatorade POY (20.5/10/6.2/3.7/3.7). Then Montverde Academy. FIBA U17 gold (17 reb vs Spain in the final, USA Basketball Male Athlete of the Year at 15). Reclassified from 2025 to 2024, skipping junior year.

Planned to announce his Duke commitment October 27, 2023. Delayed three days after the Lewiston shootings — 18 killed in his home state. Committed October 30. Signed with New Balance because two factories are near Newport. First men’s college basketball player sponsored by Gatorade. Invited to join USA Select Team at 17, scored up to 17 pts against LeBron James, Curry, and Durant. KD: ‘He’s 17 years old playing like a veteran.’
bio.duke:
College debut November 4, 2024: 18 pts/7 reb/5 ast against the University of Maine — his mother’s alma mater.

19.2 PPG, 7.5 RPG, 4.2 APG, 1.4 SPG, 1.3 BPG. Led Duke in all five major categories — the only freshman in Wooden Award history to do so. 48.3% FG, 37.4% 3PT, 83.6% FT. 709 points (20th in Duke single-season history). ACC Rookie of the Week a record 12 times.

January 11, 2025: 42 points vs Notre Dame — Duke and ACC freshman single-game scoring record (11-14 FG, 4-6 3PT, 16-17 FT, 6 reb, 7 ast). First ACC player in 25 years with 500+ pts, 100+ ast, 30+ blk in a regular season.

Duke 29-4, 19-1 ACC (regular season + tournament champions). NCAA Tournament: 14/18/30/16 pts through four rounds. Final Four vs Houston: 27 pts/7 reb/4 ast/3 blk/2 stl — first player since 1986 to lead his team in all five categories in a Final Four game. Duke lost 70-67.

Naismith Award. Wooden Award. AP POY. USBWA POY. NABC POY. ACC POY + ROY (4th to win both, joining Zion, Bagley, Okafor). Consensus 1st-Team All-American. One of the five best one-and-done seasons in history.
bio.after:
Selected #1 overall by Dallas Mavericks in the 2025 NBA Draft. Age 18 years, 186 days — second-youngest #1 pick in history, 8 days older than LeBron James in 2003.

Before the draft, he didn’t fly to LA for workouts. He went home to Maine. Worked out at a D-III gym at Colby College with his twin brother. Drove to Newport to watch his classmates graduate from Nokomis — the ceremony that should have been his if he hadn’t reclassified.

In December 2024, the Flagg family donated $10,000 to the Ronald McDonald House in Durham. Kelly cried telling the story of Hunter and Ryder. The twins born premature in Portland — one who survived, one who didn’t, 109 days in the NICU — had grown into a young man giving back to the place that gave his family a home during the worst days of their lives.
bio.now:
Cooper Flagg is 18. He plays for the Dallas Mavericks. Twin brother Ace plays at the University of Maine. Hunter is studying sports management. Ryder, who lived for two days, is remembered every time the family walks through the doors of a Ronald McDonald House.

Kelly Bowman Flagg, who shot baskets by headlight in a town of 3,200 people, who captained Maine, who planted a crabapple tree next to the driveway hoop — watched her son become the #1 pick. Ralph watched beside her.

He signed with New Balance because of Maine. He delayed his commitment because of Lewiston. He went home before the draft to watch his classmates graduate. He scored 42 against Notre Dame. He led his team in every category in a Final Four game. He won every major national award. He is eighteen.

The story begins with headlights. A father parks his car and turns on the high beams so his seven-year-old daughter can keep shooting after dark. Forty years later, that girl’s son is the first pick in the NBA Draft. The headlights are still on. Cooper Flagg is just getting started.

players.name: Kon Knueppel
players.tagline: Five brothers. Five trophies. A Nintendo Wii taught him to love basketball. Milwaukee’s finest shooter.
players.now: Charlotte Hornets; 4th overall pick 2025 NBA Draft; Summer League Champion + MVP; ROY frontrunner
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 39 starts • 14.4 PPG/4.0 RPG/2.7 APG • 40.6% 3PT • 91.4% FT • ACC Tournament MVP

bio.road:
Kon Knueppel II was born August 3, 2005, in Milwaukee, Wisconsin, into a house with six basketball hoops scattered around the property. His father, also named Kon, had scored 2,064 career points at Wisconsin Lutheran College — the school’s all-time leading scorer — and was a four-time All-Lake Michigan Conference selection. His mother, Chari Nordgaard Knueppel, had scored 1,964 career points at the University of Wisconsin-Green Bay — the program’s all-time leading scorer. She was an All-Horizon League member every season, played in two NCAA Tournaments, played professionally in Greece, and was inducted into UW-Green Bay’s Hall of Fame in 2004. Her number 33 was retired in January 2025 during a ceremony she flew in for between her son’s Duke games.

The basketball bloodlines ran deeper than even Kon and Chari. Chari’s brother, Jeff Nordgaard, starred at UW-Green Bay before being drafted by the Milwaukee Bucks in the second round in 1996. He played 13 NBA games and spent thirteen years playing professionally overseas. Chari’s maternal grandfather, Arthur “Bud” Rose, played basketball at North Carolina State in 1931–32 and earned Honorable Mention All-American honors — the first All-American in NC State history. The Knueppel-Nordgaard family tree reads like a basketball encyclopedia stretching back nearly a century.

On Kon’s father’s side, there were the uncles: Klint, Klay, and Kole. They competed alongside Kon Sr. in the Gus Macker 3-on-3 Basketball Tournament as a team called the “Flying Knueppel Brothers” — having rejected the alternative name, “Free the Knueppel.” Uncle Kole became a college football referee in the Big Ten.

Kon is the oldest of five boys. His brothers, in order: Kager, Kinston, Kash, and Kidman. All five play basketball. When Kon committed to Duke in November 2023, he posted a photo on Instagram of himself and his four younger brothers standing inside the Duke locker room. Each boy was holding one of Duke’s five national championship trophies. Five brothers. Five trophies.

But here is the part nobody expects. When Kon was five and six, he was not into sports at all. His uncle Jeff Nordgaard, the former NBA player, remembers being surprised. Little Kon was bookish, using vocabulary beyond his age. The family was starting to think he might be a non-athlete — and then his parents bought a Nintendo Wii. It was the family’s first video game console. Kon played Wii Sports basketball obsessively. The virtual game awakened something. His parents enrolled him in a kindergarten league. He didn’t score a single point in his first ten games. He ducked when teammates passed him the ball. The all-time leading scorers at Wisconsin Lutheran College and UW-Green Bay had produced a child who couldn’t catch a bounce pass.

Kon Sr. ran a men’s league in Milwaukee — had been running it for twenty years. In eighth grade, Kon II started playing in it. Among the regulars: Ben Brust, a former Wisconsin Badger and the program’s all-time three-point leader. Brust kept beating Kon on backdoor cuts. By senior year, Kon was the one doing the cutting. The men’s league taught him what no AAU circuit could.

Chari ran a daycare from the family home since 2006. Kon Sr. worked as a school counselor in Milwaukee public schools. Six hoops on the property. Five boys. Two parents who were all-time leading scorers. Blue-collar Milwaukee.

At Wisconsin Lutheran High School: 10.8 PPG as a freshman, 19.7 as a junior, 26.4 as a senior. Perfect 30–0 record, WIAA Division 2 State Championship. Wisconsin Mr. Basketball. Gatorade Wisconsin POY. Also played baritone in the school band. He chose Duke because: “I want to play against the best players every day in practice, because that will make me better.” Enrolled June 2024. Roommate: Cooper Flagg.
bio.duke:
Knueppel started all 39 games as a freshman — tying Duke’s single-season record for starts by a freshman. 14.4 PPG, 4.0 RPG, 2.7 APG. Shot 47.9% from the field, 40.6% from three, and 91.4% from the free-throw line — highest FT% in the ACC, second-highest by a Duke freshman in history (only J.J. Redick shot better). Made 38 consecutive free throws from February through March.

Cooper Flagg was the headliner. Knueppel was the other half. Flagg was impossible to keep out of the lane. Knueppel was a lethal shooter who made the high-IQ plays he’d learned from years in his father’s men’s league. Scheyer: “Their personalities were perfect for each other — competitive, unselfish, and smart.”

One preseason practice, Flagg didn’t have his best stuff. The guy who called him out? Knueppel. He told the future number-one pick to get his head in the game. Scheyer’s staff still points to that moment.

Then the ACC Tournament. Flagg sprained his ankle in the quarterfinal and missed the rest. Knueppel stepped into the spotlight: 21.0 PPG, 5.7 RPG, 4.7 APG across the tournament. 18 points and 8 rebounds in the title game against Louisville. ACC Tournament MVP. Nobody cheered harder for him than Flagg on the sideline.

The same week, back in Wisconsin, brother Kager scored 10 points in the WIAA Division 1 state title game. Wisconsin Lutheran won back-to-back championships in a higher division. Mother Chari flew between Raleigh and Madison, refusing to miss a game from any son. She attended every Duke game in person that season. Every one.

NCAA Tournament: 20.5 PPG in the Sweet 16 and Elite Eight. 16 points in the Final Four against Houston. East Region All-Tournament Team. Season line: 84 three-pointers, 117-of-128 free throws, and the quiet, lethal efficiency that made every scout say: this kid is going to play in the NBA for a long time.
bio.after:
Charlotte selected Knueppel 4th overall in the 2025 NBA Draft. On draft night, Cooper Flagg was mid-interview on NBA TV when the pick was announced. Flagg shot out of his seat — the most animated he’d been all evening, including when his own name was called first. The former roommates, the freshman duo who pushed each other every day, were both top-five picks.

Knueppel led the Hornets to the 2025 NBA Summer League championship, scoring 21 in the title game. Summer League Championship MVP. Regular-season debut October 22: 11 points and 5 rebounds vs Brooklyn. By early November: a 30-point game against Miami, Eastern Conference Player of the Week nomination. On November 14, he scored 32 in an overtime loss against Milwaukee — his hometown team, the franchise his uncle Jeff had once played for — hitting two late threes to force OT. Averaging 19.2 PPG, 5.5 RPG, 3.4 APG. Alongside Flagg, the two former roommates are frontrunners for NBA Rookie of the Year.
bio.now:
Kon Knueppel is 19 years old. He plays for the Charlotte Hornets. His former roommate Cooper Flagg plays for the Dallas Mavericks. The two are the top candidates for NBA Rookie of the Year.

Back in Milwaukee, the Knueppel machine keeps running. Kager, a 6-8 sophomore, has D-I offers and led Wisconsin Lutheran to a second consecutive state title. Kinston is generating recruiting buzz. Kash and Kidman won the national Lutheran grade-school basketball tournament in Fort Wayne, Indiana. Chari is retiring from the daycare to focus full-time on five sons and their schedules. Kon Sr. still runs the men’s league. The six hoops are still on the property.

He was born in Milwaukee into a house with six basketball hoops. His father scored 2,064 points at Wisconsin Lutheran College. His mother scored 1,964 at UW-Green Bay. His uncle was drafted by the Bucks. His great-grandfather was NC State’s first All-American in 1932. His father’s brothers called themselves the Flying Knueppel Brothers. He didn’t score a point in his first ten basketball games because a Nintendo Wii had to teach him to love the sport first. He learned to play off the ball from a former Badger in his father’s men’s league. He played baritone in the high school band. He led Wisconsin Lutheran to a perfect season. He chose Duke because he wanted to practice against the best. He was Cooper Flagg’s roommate. He told the future number-one pick to get his head in the game. He won ACC Tournament MVP when Flagg went down. He was the fourth pick. He scored 32 against his hometown Bucks. And when he committed to Duke, he posted a photo: five brothers, each holding one of the program’s five national championship trophies. Five brothers. Five trophies. One family. The Knueppels don’t just play basketball. They are basketball.

players.name: Jared McCain
players.tagline: Three shots on a ten-foot hoop. TikTok star. Painted nails. Pluto energy. The kid who wouldn’t choose between basketball and everything else.
players.now: Oklahoma City Thunder (traded from 76ers Feb 2026); defending NBA champions; ~5M TikTok followers
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 36 games • 14.3 PPG • 41.4% 3PT • 35 pts vs FSU (tied Zion’s freshman record) • ACC All-Rookie • Elite Eight

bio.road:
Jared Dane McCain was born February 20, 2004, and grew up in Corona, California — a sprawling city in the Inland Empire, east of Los Angeles. He is the son of Lance and Jina McCain. His father is African American. His mother has White and partial Filipino ancestry. Jared grew up navigating multiple cultural identities in a household that valued hard work, faith, and competition.

Lance McCain played college basketball at Midland College in Texas alongside Spud Webb — the 5-7 guard who would win the 1986 NBA Slam Dunk Contest and play twelve NBA seasons. After college, Lance did not pursue professional basketball. He became a flight attendant at Southwest Airlines. Jina was a track and field athlete at Sacramento State. They were not basketball royalty. They were a working family from the Inland Empire who happened to raise a basketball prodigy.

Jared’s first dream was baseball. He has a childhood poster that reads ‘I want to be an MLB player.’ That dream ended at age four, when he was playing up against older kids without a protective cup and took a hit that sent him home in tears. He made what he later called a business decision. He dropped baseball. He also played soccer — left defender, naturally left-footed. He is ambidextrous: eats and writes left-handed. But he shoots a basketball right-handed, because he broke his left thumb as a child and started shooting with his right out of necessity. The injury that could have derailed him instead gave him his shooting hand.

His older brother Jayce, four years his senior, played at Folsom High School and Cal State San Marcos, where he started 31 games as a junior and earned All-Conference honors. Jared idolized Jayce. From age three, his single goal was to play on his brother’s team. He watched Jayce’s games and begged his parents to let him practice. Jina set the terms: ‘Once you can make it in a ten-foot hoop three times in a row, we will put you on a team.’ Jared went outside, shot relentlessly, and made his first, second, and third shots. He was on a team that year.

Jayce showed no mercy in one-on-one. The tough love turned Jared into a competitive dog. But Jayce’s career was interrupted by health issues that prevented him from going professional. The brother who made the younger one became the brother who couldn’t follow him to the highest level.

At Centennial High School in Corona: junior year 16.9 PPG, Gatorade California POY. Appeared in three seasons of Believe, a YouTube docuseries. Senior year: McDonald’s All-American. Five-star recruit. Committed to Duke March 18, 2022, over Gonzaga, Houston, Kansas, and Louisville.

But basketball was only half the phenomenon. By the time he arrived at Duke, he had millions of TikTok followers — dance videos, lip-syncs, vlogs, trending challenges. He painted his nails before games as a ritual of self-expression. He was a Disney superfan who identified with Pluto because of Pluto’s happy personality and positive energy. He had his own T-shirt line with his face on it. He was the first Duke basketball player equally famous as a social media personality and as a player. Some loved it. Some said he should focus on the game. Jared McCain didn’t see a contradiction.
bio.duke:
McCain started all 36 games as a freshman at Duke, 2023–24. 14.3 PPG, 3.0 RPG, 1.9 APG, shooting 44.7% from the field and 41.4% from three. ACC All-Rookie Team. On February 17, 2024, he scored a career-high 35 points against Florida State — tying the Duke freshman single-game scoring record held by Zion Williamson.

Duke went 27–9 and reached the Elite Eight before falling to NC State. McCain earned All-Tournament honors at the ACC Tournament and the NCAA East Regional. His shooting was his calling card — 41.4% from three as a freshman is elite at any level. But what surprised coaches and scouts was his poise. He played with the maturity of someone who had been performing in front of millions of people since high school, because he had. The TikTok fame, far from being a distraction, had given him a comfort with pressure that most nineteen-year-olds don’t possess.

He declared for the draft after one season.
bio.after:
Philadelphia selected McCain 16th overall in the 2024 draft. Instant fan favorite — the TikTok presence, the painted nails, the personality impossible to ignore. In 23 rookie games: 15.3 PPG, 2.6 APG, 2.4 RPG, 46% FG. Career-high 34 points and 10 assists vs Cleveland. Eastern Conference Rookie of the Month for November — first Sixer since Ben Simmons in 2018.

December 14, 2024: lateral meniscus tear, left knee. Season over. Twenty-three games of brilliance, then silence.

The 2025–26 season brought more adversity. UCL tear in his right thumb before the season, surgery, delayed return until late October. In 37 games with Philadelphia: 6.6 PPG in 16.8 minutes. The rhythm was gone. On February 4, 2026, the Sixers traded McCain to the Oklahoma City Thunder for a first-round pick and three future second-rounders. The kid who had been Rookie of the Month was traded for draft capital before his twenty-second birthday.

He landed in the best possible situation. The Thunder had won the 2025 championship and had the league’s best record. They needed guard depth with SGA sidelined. McCain was reunited with Chip Engelland, the Brotherhood’s legendary shooting coach, now on Oklahoma City’s staff. It took one game for Thunder fans to embrace him.
bio.now:
Jared McCain is 21 years old. He plays for the Oklahoma City Thunder, the defending champions. He has nearly five million TikTok followers. He still paints his nails. He still dances. Jason Williams has publicly questioned whether McCain wants to be ‘a hooper or a TikToker.’ The criticism represents a generational fault line in basketball culture: the old guard that demands singular focus, and the new generation that sees no contradiction between self-expression and competition.

McCain’s answer has always been the same. He doesn’t choose. He is a basketball player who scored 15.3 points per game as a rookie and a content creator with millions of followers. He is a Disney fan who identifies with Pluto and a competitor who tied Zion Williamson’s freshman scoring record. He paints his nails and drains threes. The people who insist he must pick one don’t understand that the refusal to pick is the point.

His father Lance plays pickup and flies for Southwest Airlines. His mother Jina set the bar — three shots on a ten-foot hoop — and watched her son clear it on the first try. His brother Jayce, whose career was cut short, watches from a distance that is both proud and bittersweet. The McCains are not a basketball dynasty. They are a family that gave their son a challenge, and he has been answering it ever since.

He was born in Corona. His father played with Spud Webb. His first dream was baseball until a four-year-old made a business decision. He is naturally left-handed but shoots right because of a broken thumb. His mother told him to make three shots before she’d let him play. He made them immediately. His brother beat him one-on-one without mercy. He became a TikTok star and a McDonald’s All-American. He tied Zion’s record at Duke. He was drafted 16th and averaged 15.3 as a rookie before a torn meniscus ended his season. He was traded to the defending champions. He identifies with Pluto. He paints his nails. He won’t apologize for being himself. Jared McCain is twenty-one, and the people who think he needs to choose between basketball and everything else have not been paying attention. He’s never chosen. That’s why he’s interesting.

players.name: Cameron Boozer
players.tagline: Born via IVF to save his brother’s life. Four state titles. First since LeBron to win Mr. Basketball USA twice. Carlos Boozer’s son — but now Carlos is known as Cameron’s dad.
players.now: Duke Blue Devils freshman; projected top pick 2026 NBA Draft; 2x Gatorade National POY; 2x Mr. Basketball USA
players.stat: HS: 4 consecutive FL state titles • 2025 Chipotle Nationals Champions • 2x Gatorade National POY • 2x Mr. Basketball USA (first since LeBron) • FIBA U16 + U17 MVP

bio.road:
The story of Cameron Boozer begins before he was born — and it begins with someone else’s survival.

On May 31, 2006, Carlos Boozer and his wife CeCe received the diagnosis that would reshape their family. Their firstborn son, Carmani, had sickle cell disease. Carlos had just signed a six-year, $70 million contract with the Utah Jazz. He was a two-time NBA All-Star, a Duke championship hero, a man who had grown up in Juneau, Alaska. None of it mattered. His son was sick.

Sickle cell disease is a genetic blood disorder in which red blood cells become rigid and crescent-shaped, blocking blood flow and causing episodes of extreme pain, organ damage, and potentially death. Both Carlos and CeCe carried the sickle cell trait. Their best hope was a bone marrow transplant from a sibling’s umbilical cord blood. But they couldn’t simply conceive and hope — if their next child also carried the disease, it would mean two sick children. They needed a child who was both free of sickle cell and a perfect bone marrow match.

Carlos and CeCe chose in-vitro fertilization. Doctors harvested 34 eggs from CeCe. Ten became viable embryos. Only two were both sickle-cell-free and perfect bone marrow matches. Both were implanted. Both survived. On July 18, 2007, in Salt Lake City, Cameron Sikandar Boozer and his fraternal twin brother Cayden were born. They began their lives saving the life of their brother.

Three weeks later, stem cells from the twins’ umbilical cords were transplanted into Carmani at Miami Children’s Hospital. Forty nights in the hospital. It worked. Carmani was cured. ESPN documented the story in 2008 and returned in 2024 for Blood Brothers.

Cameron grew up in Miami. His parents divorced in 2015 but both remained present. CeCe had met Carlos when she was at NC State and Carlos was at Duke. She was 5-10, a D-I volleyball player. Carlos was 6-9, a two-time All-Star, Olympic gold medalist. At fourteen, the twins looked like they’d been built in a basketball lab. Carlos said the light bulb went on in junior high. CeCe said she knew by the eighth grade.

At Christopher Columbus High School: Cameron was the 6-9 power forward, Cayden the 6-4 point guard who knew where his brother wanted the ball before Cameron did. Sophomore year: 21.1 PPG/11.3 RPG/4.2 APG/2.0 BPG, state championship. Four consecutive Florida state titles. 2025 Chipotle Nationals crown. Cameron was named Gatorade National POY as a sophomore (2023) and again as a senior (2025). Mr. Basketball USA in 2023 and 2025 — the first player to win it twice since LeBron James. FIBA U16 Americas MVP (2023, leading USA to gold by 82 over Canada). FIBA U17 World Cup MVP (2024). The most fundamentally sound player in high school basketball.

The commitment came October 11, 2024. Cameron and Cayden chose Duke together, over Miami and Florida. The video featured Carmani — the brother whose life they’d saved. Carlos told ESPN: ‘I’m proud of them. They’ve worked their butts off.’ Then he shared what defines how far the family has come: ‘Hey, Mr. Boozer, you’re Cameron and Cayden’s dad? I get that all the time now.’ The two-time All-Star is now recognized for what his children are becoming. And he loves it.
bio.duke:
Cameron enrolled at Duke for the 2025–26 season alongside Cayden. They are the centerpiece of Scheyer’s next recruiting class — the follow-up to Flagg and Knueppel. Cameron was the #2 overall recruit in the 2025 class.

His freshman season is being written. What he brings to Durham is already clear: a 6-9 power forward with elite scoring touch, a rebounder who attacks the glass like his father, a passer with unusual vision for his size, and a winner who has never known a season without a championship. Four state titles. Two national MVPs. Two FIBA gold medals. He is eighteen, and his resume already reads like a career.

What makes Cameron different from his father at the same age is the modern game. Carlos at Duke was a low-post scorer, a physical rebounder built for the paint. Cameron has the same frame but plays facing the basket. He can shoot from the perimeter, handle in transition, run pick-and-roll as screener or initiator. The NBA he’s preparing for is different from the one his father played in.

Cayden is beside him — the same chemistry they’ve shared since they were toddlers wearing Bulls jerseys at Heat games. The connection is almost telepathic. Cayden doesn’t have to look. He knows where Cameron is.
bio.after:
Cameron Boozer’s Duke career is just beginning. He is projected as a top pick in the 2026 NBA Draft — mock drafts have placed him in the top three since he was a sophomore in high school. His combination of size, skill, basketball intelligence, and pedigree makes him one of the most valuable prospects in amateur basketball.

The NBA story will come. What matters now is the Duke story — and the family story. Cameron will play in Cameron Indoor Stadium, the same court where his father scored, rebounded, and won a national championship in 2001. He will wear Duke blue as the son of a Brotherhood legend, alongside his twin brother, the one conceived through the same act of medical science and parental love, delivered into the world with the same purpose: to save Carmani’s life and then to build lives of their own.
bio.now:
Cameron Boozer is 18 years old. He is a freshman at Duke. His twin Cayden is his teammate. His older brother Carmani — cured of sickle cell disease by stem cells from the twins’ umbilical cords — is 19, a freshman pitcher at the University of Fort Lauderdale, living a full life that exists because of a decision his parents made in 2006. Carlos works as an ACC Network analyst and recently joined the Utah Jazz front office. CeCe remains deeply involved.

The parents who drove a 1985 Dodge van from Washington, D.C., to Juneau, Alaska, because a six-year-old boy was shot on a basketball court — Carlos Sr. and Renee — moved back to Juneau. The family born from fear produced an NBA All-Star, an Olympic gold medalist, a medical miracle, and two of the most celebrated high school basketball players in the country.

Cameron was born in Salt Lake City on July 18, 2007. His umbilical cord blood contained the stem cells that saved his brother’s life. He was three weeks old when they were transplanted into Carmani. He didn’t know. He couldn’t know. He was a newborn. Seventeen years later, he is a Duke Blue Devil, a four-time state champion, a two-time national player of the year, a two-time Mr. Basketball USA, and the son of a man who now gets recognized as ‘Cameron and Cayden’s dad.’ The story of Cameron Boozer is a story about basketball, but it is first and forever a story about blood. The blood that was sick. The blood that healed it. And the brotherhood — the real one, between three brothers in Miami — that made all of it possible.

players.name: Cayden Boozer
players.tagline: The loud twin. The passer in a family of scorers. My brother’s keeper — since the day he was born.
players.now: Duke Blue Devils freshman; projected 1st-round 2026 NBA Draft; best passer in high school basketball
players.stat: HS: 4 FL state titles • 3 Nike EYBL titles • Jr: 12.0 PPG/7.6 APG • Led FIBA U17 World Cup in assists • 2x USA Gold

bio.road:
Every twin story has two sides, and in the Boozer family, the sides could not be more different. Cameron is stoic and serious. Cayden is loud and rambunctious. Their mother CeCe put it plainly: without Cayden, Cameron would have never gone to movies or done regular kid things. Cameron is the introvert with a basketball and a plan. Cayden is the extrovert who makes sure his brother — and everyone around him — actually lives.

Cayden Boozer was born July 18, 2007, in Salt Lake City, three weeks before he and Cameron performed the most important assist of their lives. Stem cells from their umbilical cords were transplanted into their older brother Carmani, who had sickle cell disease. The transplant worked. Carmani was cured. The story of how Cameron and Cayden were conceived through IVF — 34 eggs, 10 viable embryos, only 2 that were both sickle-cell-free and perfect bone marrow matches — is told in full in Cameron’s profile and in their father Carlos’s. It is the foundational story of the Boozer family. Cayden carries it with the knowledge that his life began as an act of love designed to save someone else’s.

CeCe described the family mantra as ‘I am my brother’s keeper.’ That phrase defines Cayden more than any stat line. He is the keeper. The one who watches out. The one who sees the floor — on the court and off it. Even as children, Cayden was the social one who dragged Cameron to birthday parties and movies. Cameron, left to his own devices, would have spent every waking hour in the gym. Cayden made sure his brother had a childhood alongside the basketball.

The twins grew up in Miami after Carlos’s NBA career wound down. CeCe and Carlos divorced in 2015 but both remained present. The boys wore Bulls jerseys to Heat playoff games as children, chanting ‘Let’s go Heat!’ At Columbus High School, Cameron was the scorer. Cayden was the conductor. At 6-5 and 210 pounds, Cayden had the size of a small forward but the vision of a floor general. He knew where Cameron wanted the ball before Cameron did — because they’d been playing together since before they could form sentences.

Junior year at Columbus: 12.0 PPG, 7.6 APG, 2.7 RPG, 1.8 SPG. Scouts called him the best passer in high school basketball. Assist-to-turnover ratio consistently better than 3:1. Nike EYBL: 6.5 APG leading the league. Peach Jam: 6.9 APG, including 11 in a semifinal. FIBA U17 World Cup: led the tournament in assists (6.4 APG) despite averaging just 18 minutes. He could score — 22 against Montverde, 22 against Link Academy — but he chose to pass.

ESPN compared him to Tre Jones. Others saw Andre Miller. The common thread: reliability. Cayden Boozer is the point guard who never panics, never forces, never turns it over when it matters. CeCe called him ‘trustworthy.’ For a point guard, there is no higher compliment. Committed to Duke October 11, 2024, alongside Cameron. Duke’s first five-star point guard since Jeremy Roach in 2020.
bio.duke:
Cayden arrived at Duke for 2025–26 alongside Cameron. His freshman year is underway. What he brings to Durham is what he has always brought: the ability to make everyone around him better. He organizes the offense, sets the tempo, finds the open man, and turns Cameron’s raw talent into easy baskets. He is the connector.

The basketball world will focus on Cameron, the projected top pick in the 2026 draft. But the people who watch Duke every game will know what Cayden knows, what CeCe knows, what every Columbus teammate knew for four years: Cameron is great because of Cameron, but Cameron is greater because of Cayden. The stoic one needs the loud one. The scorer needs the passer. The introvert needs the extrovert who drags him to the movies. That architecture has produced four state titles, three EYBL championships, two FIBA gold medals, and a national championship. It works because Cayden makes it work.
bio.after:
Cayden is projected as a first-round pick in the 2026 draft alongside Cameron. He may not be drafted as high — point guards who pass first are historically undervalued compared to dominant scorers — but his impact could be equally profound. Teams that value winning over individual statistics will see what Columbus and Duke have seen: Cayden Boozer makes every team he’s on better.

The question every Boozer fan asks is whether the twins will end up on the same NBA team. The odds are long, but the possibility is tantalizing: two brothers, conceived through IVF to save their sibling, playing professional basketball together the way they have since elementary school.
bio.now:
Cayden Boozer is 18. He is a freshman at Duke playing point guard alongside Cameron. Carmani — cured of sickle cell by the twins’ stem cells — is 19, a freshman pitcher at the University of Fort Lauderdale. Carlos works as an ACC Network analyst. CeCe remains deeply involved.

Cayden is the loud one, the rambunctious one, the one who makes sure his brothers live outside the gym. He is the passer in a family of scorers. He is the keeper of his brothers — both the one he saved at birth and the one he saves every game with a perfectly timed entry pass. He has spent his entire life making other people better: Carmani by being born, Cameron by being his point guard, his Columbus teammates by finding them in their spots, his USA Basketball squads by leading tournaments in assists while playing eighteen minutes a game.

He was born in Salt Lake City on July 18, 2007. Three weeks later, the stem cells from his umbilical cord helped cure his brother’s disease. He grew up in Miami wearing Bulls jerseys to Heat games. He is the fraternal twin of the most fundamentally sound player in high school basketball, and he chose to be the one who delivers the ball rather than the one who scores it. He averaged 7.6 assists because the game, for Cayden, has always been about making the people around him better. ESPN compared him to Tre Jones. His mother compared him to a brother’s keeper. She was right. Cayden Boozer has been keeping his brothers since the day he was born. He is the connector, the facilitator, the loud twin who makes the quiet twin whole. I am my brother’s keeper. That is the Boozer family mantra, and nobody has lived it more completely than Cayden.

players.name: Isaiah Evans
players.tagline: Showtime Slim. JV as a freshman. Didn’t make his seventh-grade team. Bought his own cones. Single mother believed in the slow build. 48 points in the state quarterfinal — 21 in a row.
players.now: Duke Blue Devils sophomore; 14.7 PPG; projected 1st-round 2026 NBA Draft; jersey #0 retired at North Mecklenburg
players.stat: HS: 2x NC Mr. Basketball • McDonald’s AA • 4A State Champion • 27.5 PPG senior year • 48 pts state QF • Duke So: 14.7 PPG

bio.road:
Isaiah Demonte Evans was born December 6, 2005, in Fayetteville, North Carolina, and grew up in the orbit of Charlotte — close enough to feel the pull of one of the South’s great basketball cities, far enough away that nobody was looking for him. He was raised by his mother, Marieke Lemon, a single mother who worked long hours to provide for Isaiah and his younger brother. His grandmother stepped in when Marieke needed help. The household was built on sacrifice, structure, and the quiet understanding that nothing would be given.

Isaiah played everything as a kid — soccer, football, basketball. His mother supported every sport. He stopped playing football around sixth grade. Moved on from soccer when he was nearly ten. Basketball stuck. He started on local rec teams, and the love grew from there.

But here is the part nobody believes: Isaiah Evans did not make his seventh-grade team. A scheduling mix-up kept him off the roster. The following year, as a freshman at North Mecklenburg High School in Huntersville, he was placed on the junior varsity team. JV. Future five-star recruits do not play JV. Future McDonald’s All-Americans do not play JV. Isaiah Evans played JV because he was not yet the player the world would come to know, and his mother and coaches believed in a slow build. They were right.

The summer after freshman year, something activated. He spent the entire off-season grinding — not at a fancy facility, not with a personal trainer, not on an elite AAU team. He bought his own cones, ladders, and training equipment with money he had saved. He worked out three times a day: before school, after practice, late at night. Nobody was watching. Nobody was recruiting. He was a JV player from Huntersville teaching himself to be great with equipment he’d bought himself.

Sophomore year: varsity, nearly 20 PPG. Junior year: 26.0 PPG, 6.4 RPG, North Carolina Mr. Basketball. Senior year: 27.5 PPG, 6.3 RPG, 3.0 APG, shooting 53% from the field and 43% from three. Undefeated season. Class 4A state championship. In the state quarterfinal against nationally ranked Myers Park, the defending champions, he scored 48 points — including 21 in a row during the second half. SLAM Magazine called it a 48-piece, no fries. His nickname — Showtime Slim, for his slender 175-pound frame and his tendency to deliver under the brightest lights — was cemented.

North Mecklenburg retired his jersey. Number zero. First and last to wear it. ‘A lot of people don’t know this, but I’m going to be the first and last person to wear number zero. It really meant a lot to me.’

One more detail: Isaiah Evans grew up a Tar Heel fan. In April 2023, a Carolina kid committed to Duke — over Kansas, NC State, Tennessee, Texas, Auburn, and Florida State. His mother’s reaction to the whole journey: ‘You’ll hear that a lot. They’re like, Oh, he came out of nowhere.’ He didn’t come out of nowhere. He came out of Fayetteville, where his mother worked long hours and his grandmother helped, where he bought his own cones and worked out three times a day, where nobody was watching until everybody was.
bio.duke:
Evans’s freshman season at Duke, 2024–25, was a lesson in patience. He played behind Cooper Flagg, Kon Knueppel, and a roster loaded with future NBA picks. He averaged 6.8 PPG in limited minutes. But when he was hot, the building knew. Against Auburn in December, he hit six three-pointers in the first half alone — 18 points on 6-for-8 from deep — and Cameron Indoor erupted. Teammate Khaman Maluach: ‘He gave us a lot of energy.’

When Flagg went down with an ankle injury in the ACC Tournament quarterfinal, Evans stepped into the starting lineup and helped Duke win the conference championship. He shot 41.6% from three for the season. Duke reached the Final Four before falling to Houston. 35–4 record, three points from a championship game.

He tested the waters of the 2025 draft but returned to Duke for his sophomore year. The decision signaled he understood the developmental arc his mother had always believed in — the slow build, the patient climb.

Sophomore year, 2025–26: promoted to the starting lineup alongside Cameron and Cayden Boozer. Through 29 games: 14.7 PPG, 2.9 RPG, 1.3 APG, 43% from the field, 36.3% from three, 88.8% from the free-throw line. Hit a game-winning three against Florida. He was no longer the sixth man hoping for hot streaks. He was a starter on a 27–2 team sitting atop the ACC. At last, the player his mother always saw in the driveway.
bio.after:
Isaiah Evans’s Duke career is still unfolding. He is projected as a first-round pick in the 2026 NBA Draft. His combination of size (6-6), shooting range, and developing playmaking ability makes him attractive to scouts who value wings that stretch the floor. The path from JV freshman to first-round pick would be one of the most unlikely developmental arcs in modern basketball.

His mother Marieke will be in the stands. She has been in the stands since the rec leagues in Fayetteville, since the JV games at North Meck, since the state championship, since Cameron Indoor and the Final Four. She has never missed the show, even when the show was a fifteen-year-old playing junior varsity in front of forty people. That is the part of the Isaiah Evans story no stat line can capture: the mother who believed in the slow build when everyone else wanted the fast track.
bio.now:
Isaiah Evans is 20. He is a sophomore at Duke, starting alongside Cameron and Cayden Boozer on a team that is 27–2. He is nicknamed Showtime Slim. He hosts basketball camps for kids in Concord, North Carolina, through the nonprofit Destined 4 Greatness Hoops. His message to campers: consistent work and keeping your head down is what’s going to put you in the same position as me.

He was born in Fayetteville. His mother raised him alone, working long hours, with her mother helping. He played every sport before basketball chose him. He didn’t make his seventh-grade team. He played JV as a freshman. He bought his own cones and ladders and trained three times a day when nobody was watching. He became the best player in North Carolina. He scored 48 in a state quarterfinal — 21 in a row. He was a Tar Heel fan who committed to Duke. His jersey was retired at North Mecklenburg: number zero, first and last. He sat behind Cooper Flagg as a freshman and hit six threes in one half against Auburn. He came back because he believed in the slow build. He is averaging 14.7 and hit a game-winner against Florida.

His mother said it best. People say he came out of nowhere. He didn’t come out of nowhere. He came out of Fayetteville, where a single mother worked long hours and a kid bought his own cones with saved money and trained in the dark until the world had no choice but to notice. That’s not nowhere. That’s everywhere. That’s every kid who was told he wasn’t good enough, who played JV when he should have been varsity, who missed the seventh-grade team because of a scheduling mix-up. The difference is Isaiah Evans kept going. Showtime Slim doesn’t come out of nowhere. He comes out of the work.
